question: A team adds the Camunda Spring Boot Starter and declares `@JobWorker` methods whose parameters they want bound to process variables _by name_, without specifying annotation values on each one. Per the getting-started page, what build setting must be enabled to make this work?

options.text: Add `@EnableConfigurationProperties` to the Spring Boot application class.
Build with JDK 17 — modern JDKs retain parameter names automatically with no extra flag.
Enable the Java compiler flag `-parameters` (e.g. via `maven-compiler-plugin` `compilerArgs`).
Set `camunda.client.worker.bind-by-name: true` in `application.yaml`.

c.text: The getting-started page lists this as Step 2: enable the Java compiler `-parameters` flag so parameter names are retained at runtime and can be matched to process variables without explicit annotation values.

a.text: `@EnableConfigurationProperties` exposes `@ConfigurationProperties` beans; it has nothing to do with retaining method parameter names in bytecode.

d.text: There is no such configuration property — name binding depends on bytecode-level parameter names, which the compiler emits only when `-parameters` is passed.

b.text: The JDK version is unrelated; even on JDK 17+ the compiler strips method parameter names from class files unless `-parameters` is passed to `javac`.

explanation: By default the Java compiler does not emit method parameter names into the class file. The Spring Boot Starter needs those names to bind `@JobWorker` parameters to process variables without explicit annotation values, so the docs instruct teams to enable the `-parameters` compiler flag via `maven-compiler-plugin`, Gradle's `tasks.withType(JavaCompile)`, or the IntelliJ Java Compiler settings.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - Get started

question: A team is reviewing the Camunda Spring Boot Starter introduced in 8.8 before adopting it. Which of the following statements about it is **NOT** true?

options.text: It uses the new Camunda Java Client under the hood.
It uses gRPC as its default protocol and requires the Spring Zeebe SDK as a runtime dependency.
The Spring Zeebe SDK is scheduled to be removed in Camunda 8.10, and teams should migrate before that upgrade.
It is part of the Camunda 8 public API and follows Semantic Versioning, except for alpha features.

b.text: This statement is false on both counts: REST is the default protocol (gRPC is _configurable_), and the new starter _replaces_ the Spring Zeebe SDK rather than depending on it.

c.text: True — the docs state "Spring Zeebe SDK will be removed in version 8.10" and direct teams to migrate before that upgrade to avoid breaking changes.

d.text: True — the docs note the starter is part of the public API and follows SemVer except for alpha features; minor and patch releases will not introduce breaking changes.

a.text: True — the migration callout states the new starter "uses the new Camunda Java Client under the hood".

explanation: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of 8.8. REST is the default protocol (gRPC is configurable), it is part of the public API with SemVer guarantees, and it wraps the new Camunda Java Client. The false statement combines two wrong claims: gRPC-by-default and a Spring Zeebe SDK runtime dependency.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - Getting started

question: According to the Camunda Spring Boot Starter version-compatibility table for 8.8, what is the minimum JDK required by every published starter artifact (`camunda-spring-boot-starter`, `camunda-spring-boot-3-starter`, `camunda-spring-boot-4-starter`)?

options.text: JDK 17 or later.
JDK 11 or later.
JDK 21 or later — only the `-4-starter` accepts JDK 17.
JDK 8 or later.

a.text: All three rows of the compatibility table list `≥ 17` in the JDK column — the minimum is JDK 17 regardless of which starter artifact is used.

b.text: JDK 11 is below the published minimum for any of the 8.8 starter artifacts.

d.text: JDK 8 is far below the published minimum and corresponds to older Camunda 7 / Spring Boot 2.x ecosystems.

c.text: JDK 21 is _allowed_ (it satisfies ≥ 17) but is not the published minimum; the table requires only JDK 17 for every artifact, including the `-4-starter`.

explanation: The 8.8 version-compatibility table requires JDK 17 or newer for every starter variant: `camunda-spring-boot-starter` (Spring Boot 3.5.x), `camunda-spring-boot-3-starter` (alias, Spring Boot 3.5.x), and `camunda-spring-boot-4-starter` (Spring Boot 4.0.x).

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - Version compatibility

question: A team is adding the default Camunda Spring Boot Starter to a new Spring Boot 3.5 project on Camunda 8.8. Which Maven `groupId` / `artifactId` pair does the getting-started page prescribe?

options.text: `org.springframework.boot` / `spring-boot-starter-camunda`
`io.camunda` / `camunda-spring-boot-starter`
`io.camunda.zeebe` / `spring-zeebe-starter`
`org.camunda.bpm.springboot` / `camunda-bpm-spring-boot-starter`

b.text: Step 1 of the getting-started page prescribes exactly this coordinate: `io.camunda` / `camunda-spring-boot-starter` at version `8.8.x`.

a.text: Spring does not publish a Camunda starter under its own group; this coordinate does not exist.

c.text: `io.camunda.zeebe` / `spring-zeebe-*` belonged to the Spring Zeebe SDK, which the Camunda Spring Boot Starter _replaces_ as of 8.8 and which is removed in 8.10.

d.text: `org.camunda.bpm.springboot` is the Camunda 7 (`camunda-bpm`) starter group — a different product line, not Camunda 8.

explanation: The Camunda 8 Spring Boot Starter is published under `io.camunda` / `camunda-spring-boot-starter`. The `org.camunda.bpm.springboot` group belongs to Camunda 7, and `io.camunda.zeebe` belonged to the now-removed Spring Zeebe SDK.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - Get started

question: In the recommended data-accessor pattern, what is the purpose of a Process Variable Accessor?

options.text: It is a Java interface of `String` constants holding the variable names so the IDE can refactor them safely.
It stores complex business data in a relational database alongside the process so the engine only holds a reference.
It encapsulates the access to exactly one variable and is useful when the same variable is reused across different process definitions.
It knows the names and types of all process variables of one process definition and is the central point to declare them.

c.text: The docs describe a Process Variable Accessor as a class that encapsulates access to exactly one variable, which makes it the right choice when the same variable is reused across different processes.

d.text: That description matches a Process Data Accessor, which knows the names and types of all process variables of a certain process definition and serves as the central declaration point.

b.text: Storing complex data in a relational database is the separate 'complex data as entities' pattern, not what either data accessor flavor does.

a.text: An interface of `String` constants is the simpler 'constants' pattern the docs introduce first; data accessors go further by handling type conversion and serialization in addition to naming.

explanation: The handling-data-in-processes best practice identifies two flavors of Data Accessor: a Process Data Accessor (knows the names and types of all variables of one process definition) and a Process Variable Accessor (encapsulates access to exactly one variable, useful when that variable is reused in different processes). Plain `String` constants are a simpler related pattern that solves only the naming/typo problem.

question: A team adopts the 'complex data as entities' (externalized process context) pattern. What is stored as the actual process variable inside Camunda?

options.text: A signed pointer that Zeebe automatically dereferences when a task activates.
Only a reference to the entity's primary key, typically an artificial UUID.
The entire entity, serialised to JSON, on every process step.
The DDL of the relational table that holds the entity.

b.text: The docs say you will only store a reference to the entity's primary key (typically an artificial UUID) as the real process variable; the complex business data lives in custom tables alongside the process.

c.text: Serializing the full entity into process variables defeats the purpose of the pattern, which is precisely to keep heavy data out of process variables and avoid variable size limits.

d.text: DDL describes the table schema, not runtime payload, and nothing in the docs says the schema is held as a process variable.

a.text: Zeebe has no built-in 'signed pointer' construct that auto-dereferences external entities; worker code is responsible for loading the entity by the stored key.

explanation: In the 'complex data as entities' (or 'externalized process context') pattern, only a reference — typically an artificial UUID primary key — lives in Camunda as a process variable, while the complex business data lives in custom relational tables. Workers load entities by that key. This unlocks rich SQL queries, custom data-migration strategies, and proper data modeling at the cost of an extra database and access code.

question: A team migrating from Camunda 7 drops BPMN data objects onto the diagram expecting them to drive a gateway as process variables. How does Camunda 8 treat BPMN data objects?

options.text: Camunda does not treat BPMN data objects as process variables; they are recommended only for documentation, and excessive use is discouraged.
They are stored as XML attachments on the process instance and exposed via a dedicated REST endpoint.
They are automatically registered as JSON-typed process variables of the same name and are readable at gateways.
They behave like Camunda 7 typed variables and support any Java object out of the box.

a.text: The docs state explicitly that Camunda does not treat BPMN data objects as process variables; the recommendation is to use them occasionally for documentation and to avoid excessive usage.

c.text: Variable values are stored as JSON in Camunda 8, but BPMN data objects are not auto-registered as variables — they are diagram annotations only.

b.text: There is no XML-attachment mechanism for data objects in Camunda 8; the docs simply state they are not variables.

d.text: Camunda 7 handles variables differently (not JSON, with a different set of supported value types), but BPMN data objects are not the same as process variables there either.

explanation: Camunda 8 stores variable values as JSON, but BPMN data objects on the diagram are not process variables at all. The docs state this explicitly, recommend using data objects only occasionally for documentation, and link to a 'avoid excessive usage of data objects' modeling guideline. A common Camunda 7→8 misconception is to treat data objects as if they were typed variables of the engine.

question: A tweet approval process has a leading system that already stores the full tweet text. According to the best practice, what should be kept as a process variable?

options.text: The full tweet text, mirrored on every task so the worker never needs the leading system.
The tweet text as a BPMN data object on the diagram, leaving Camunda variables empty.
Both `tweet` and `tweetId`, kept in sync via a periodic background job.
Only a reference such as `tweetId`, fetching the tweet text from the leading system when needed.

d.text: The 'storing references only' guideline says that when a leading system holds the business data, store only an ID (e.g. `tweetId`) as a process variable rather than the full payload.

a.text: Mirroring large payloads on every task wastes variable space, risks hitting the variable size limitation, and duplicates state already owned by the leading system.

b.text: BPMN data objects are not treated as process variables in Camunda 8 and cannot be read at gateways or by workers; the docs recommend them only for documentation.

c.text: Keeping both in sync introduces duplicated state and an extra background job — the opposite of the 'store as few variables as possible' rule.

explanation: When a leading system already stores the business data, the docs recommend keeping process variables lean by storing only a reference (e.g. `tweetId`) in Camunda. Payload as a process variable is justified only when it drives gateway decisions, supports a message-oriented hand-off, acts as a cache, postpones data changes in the leading system, tracks historical development, or no leading system exists.

question: In the Java client `JobHandler` shown above, which call signals to the broker that the job finished successfully and forwards `emailSent: true` into the process instance?

options.text: Returning a `Map<String, Object>` from `JobHandler.handle()`, which the Java client auto-completes for you.
The `newCompleteCommand(...).variables(...).send()` chain on `JobClient`, optionally followed by `.join()` to block until the broker acknowledges.
Closing the `JobWorker` via the try-with-resources — that flushes the active job back to the broker as completed.
Calling `client.newFailCommand(job.getKey()).retries(0).send()` — zero remaining retries is the standard happy-path completion.

b.text: The Quick start example uses `client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).variables(Map.of("emailSent", true)).send().join()`. `send()` issues the gRPC complete command, and the variables are merged into the process instance.

a.text: Returning a map from `handle()` does nothing in the bare Java client — completion is explicit. (Auto-completion from a return value exists in the Spring SDK with `autoComplete = true`, not here.)

d.text: `newFailCommand` is used when work fails; with zero remaining retries it raises an incident rather than signalling success.

c.text: Closing the `JobWorker` only shuts the worker down; it does not complete individual jobs. Activated-but-unfinished jobs simply time out on the broker side.

explanation: In the bare Java client there is no auto-complete. The handler must call `client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).variables(...).send()` (typically with `.join()`) to mark the job successful and merge variables into the process instance; the corresponding `newFailCommand(...)` is used when something goes wrong.

question: A Java job worker is configured with `maxJobsActive = 10`. After it has just activated 10 jobs from a single poll, at what point does it issue its next poll for more jobs?

options.text: When the count of unhandled jobs drops below 30% of `maxJobsActive` rounded up — in this case below 3.
Immediately after handling any one job, so the in-flight buffer is always topped up to `maxJobsActive`.
After a fixed `pollInterval`, regardless of how many jobs are still in flight.
Only after all 10 activated jobs have been handled, never before.

a.text: Each time a job is handled, the worker checks whether the unhandled count has dropped below 30% of `maxJobsActive` (rounded up). The first time that condition is met, it polls for more — for `maxJobsActive = 10` that threshold is 3.

b.text: The worker does not re-poll after every single handled job; the docs describe a 30%-of-`maxJobsActive` threshold instead of one-at-a-time topping-up.

d.text: Waiting until all jobs are handled would leave the worker idle right before re-polling; the threshold deliberately tops up before the buffer empties.

c.text: `pollInterval` governs how long the worker waits when the previous poll returned no jobs (or failed). After a successful poll, the 30% threshold drives the next poll, not `pollInterval`.

explanation: The Java job worker pre-fetches `maxJobsActive` jobs and then polls again only when the number of unhandled jobs drops below 30% of `maxJobsActive`, rounded up. For `maxJobsActive = 10` that threshold is 3 unhandled jobs — matching the docs' worked example (with `maxJobsActive = 3` the threshold is 1). `pollInterval` applies to the empty-result and error paths, not to mid-batch top-ups.

question: Which strategy does the Java client's default `BackoffSupplier` use, and which gRPC error response is it specifically designed to soak up?

options.text: No backoff by default — the worker re-polls immediately and relies entirely on the cluster's backpressure.
Exponential backoff, applied after error responses such as `GRPC_STATUS_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` from the Zeebe cluster.
Linear backoff, applied only when a long-polling request times out.
A fixed delay equal to `pollInterval`, applied after every successful poll.

b.text: The docs state the default `BackoffSupplier` is an exponential backoff (configurable via `BackoffSupplier.newBackoffBuilder()`), and that it is especially useful for soaking up `GRPC_STATUS_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` responses when the cluster is under too much load.

c.text: The default is exponential, not linear, and it applies to failed polls in general — not specifically to long-polling timeouts.

d.text: Backoff is triggered by failed polls, not successful ones, and it is independent of `pollInterval`.

a.text: The Java client ships with a default exponential backoff; without one the worker would hammer an already-overloaded cluster.

explanation: When a poll fails with an error response, the worker waits for the delay supplied by its `BackoffSupplier` before polling again. The default is an exponential backoff (replaceable via `.backoffSupplier(...)` on the builder), and the docs explicitly call out `GRPC_STATUS_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` — emitted by Zeebe's backpressure mechanism under high load — as the canonical case the backoff is designed to handle.

question: A team subscribes to the two built-in `JobWorkerMetrics` counters and wants the number of jobs currently buffered by the worker but not yet handled. Which calculation gives that count?

options.text: `jobHandled - jobActivated`
`jobActivated * pollInterval`
`jobHandled / maxJobsActive`
`jobActivated - jobHandled`

d.text: The docs state that subtracting the two counters (`jobActivated - jobHandled`) gives the count of queued or buffered jobs — jobs the worker has activated but not yet handled.

a.text: Reversing the subtraction would always be ≤ 0 because, for a single worker, `jobHandled` cannot exceed `jobActivated`.

b.text: Multiplying a count by a time interval mixes unrelated dimensions and is not a calculation the docs describe.

c.text: Dividing handled count by `maxJobsActive` does not produce an in-flight count; the docs simply describe a subtraction.

explanation: `JobWorkerMetrics` exposes two counters: `jobActivated` is incremented before a job is passed to the handler, and `jobHandled` is incremented when the handler returns (whether or not it succeeded). The docs explicitly call out that `jobActivated - jobHandled` gives the in-flight (queued or buffered) job count — useful for tuning concurrency, scaling workers, or sizing `maxJobsActive`.

question: An ops engineer wires the Java client to Prometheus through the built-in Micrometer `JobWorkerMetrics`. Which metric name will keep working after version 8.10?

options.text: `camunda.client.worker.job.activated`
`zeebe.client.worker.job.activated`
`camunda.client.worker.job.queued`
`zeebe.client.worker.job.handled`

a.text: The Micrometer implementation publishes `camunda.client.worker.job.activated` and `camunda.client.worker.job.handled` as the new, supported metrics — these are the names to use going forward.

b.text: `zeebe.client.worker.job.activated` is marked deprecated in the docs and will be removed at 8.10; teams must migrate to the `camunda.client.*` equivalent before upgrading.

d.text: `zeebe.client.worker.job.handled` is the other deprecated counter that will also be removed at 8.10.

c.text: There is no built-in `camunda.client.worker.job.queued` metric; the queued/in-flight count is derived by subtracting handled from activated, not published as its own counter.

explanation: The built-in Micrometer `JobWorkerMetrics` exposes four metrics today: the new `camunda.client.worker.job.activated` and `camunda.client.worker.job.handled`, plus the deprecated `zeebe.client.worker.job.activated` and `zeebe.client.worker.job.handled`. The deprecated `zeebe.*` counters will be removed at 8.10, so only the `camunda.client.*` names survive long-term.

question: The built-in `JobWorkerMetrics` only counts jobs activated and jobs handled. To also observe how often the worker issues `ActivateJobs` polls, which mechanism does the Java-client workaround recommend wiring into the `CamundaClientBuilder`?

options.text: A gRPC `ClientInterceptor` (or Apache HttpClient `AsyncExecChainHandler`) attached via `withInterceptors(...)` / `withChainHandlers(...)` on the client builder.
An additional Micrometer tag on the existing `camunda.client.worker.job.activated` counter that breaks it down per poll.
A `pollInterceptor` registered directly on the `JobWorker` instance after `open()`.
A new `pollCount` method on `JobWorkerBuilder.metrics(...)` that publishes a third built-in counter.

a.text: The 'workarounds for additional metrics' section recommends a gRPC `ClientInterceptor` or Apache HttpClient `AsyncExecChainHandler` wired through `withInterceptors(...)` / `withChainHandlers(...)` on the builder. The docs show a Micrometer example using `MetricCollectingClientInterceptor` and `ObservationExecChainHandler`.

d.text: There is no `pollCount` on `JobWorkerBuilder.metrics(...)`; the `JobWorkerMetrics` API is limited to `jobActivated(int)` and `jobHandled(int)`.

b.text: Tags cannot synthesize a counter the built-in metrics do not increment; activated/handled are the only two `JobWorkerMetrics` records.

c.text: There is no `pollInterceptor` API on `JobWorker`; the workaround intentionally routes through the client builder's gRPC/HTTP interceptors.

explanation: Built-in `JobWorkerMetrics` is deliberately narrow (activated/handled). For poll-level observability, the docs route teams through the underlying transport: attach a gRPC `ClientInterceptor` (e.g. Micrometer's `MetricCollectingClientInterceptor`) or, for the HTTP transport, an Apache HttpClient `AsyncExecChainHandler` (e.g. `ObservationExecChainHandler`) via `CamundaClientBuilder.withInterceptors(...)` / `.withChainHandlers(...)`.

question: A Java job worker is opened with `streamEnabled(true)` and `maxJobsActive = 32`. The worker is already handling 32 jobs when a 33rd job becomes activate-able on the broker. What happens?

options.text: The worker silently bypasses `maxJobsActive` because streaming is enabled — the cap only applies to polling.
The 33rd job is dropped on the worker and an incident is raised on the process instance immediately.
The gRPC stream blocks via built-in flow control, signalling the gateway to stop pushing more jobs until one in-flight job completes.
The 33rd job is buffered in worker memory indefinitely until any of the in-flight jobs finishes.

c.text: With streaming, the Java client relies on built-in gRPC flow control. At `maxJobsActive` capacity the gRPC thread blocks, which signals the gateway to stop sending more jobs until in-flight work drops below the cap.

b.text: Backpressure does not drop jobs at the worker. Jobs are only 'dropped' on the broker side when the worker blocks longer than the job's deadline — and even then, the job is later re-pushed after timing out.

a.text: The docs are explicit that backpressure applies to BOTH pushing and polling when streaming is enabled, so `maxJobsActive` still bounds concurrent in-flight work.

d.text: There is no unbounded memory buffer beyond `maxJobsActive`; the gateway is told to pause sending more.

explanation: Enabling `streamEnabled(true)` opens a long-living stream over which jobs are pushed without polling. `maxJobsActive` remains the soft cap on concurrent in-flight jobs (and a soft bound on memory: roughly `maxJobsActive × maxMessageSize`). When that cap is reached, the gRPC thread blocks, the built-in flow control signals the gateway to stop pushing, and the worker resumes receiving jobs once in-flight work drains.

question: Which statement about configuring multi-tenancy on a Java job worker is **NOT** true?

options.text: `client.newWorker().tenantIds("myTenant", "myOtherTenant").open()` opens one worker that picks up jobs for both tenants.
Default tenants for every opened worker can be set with the env var `CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS` (or the equivalent system property).
If the client is unauthorised for one of the configured tenants, the worker still picks up jobs for the other authorised tenants.
A single-tenant worker is opened with `client.newWorker().tenantId("myTenant").open()`.

c.text: This is the false statement (and therefore the correct pick for a NOT question). The docs are explicit: the client must be authorised for ALL provided tenants; if it is not, the job worker will not work on any jobs at all — there is no silent subsetting to the authorised ones.

a.text: The `.tenantIds("myTenant", "myOtherTenant")` builder call really does open a worker for both tenants in a single subscription — this statement is true.

b.text: Setting `CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS` (or `camunda.client.worker.tenantIds`) does configure default tenants for every worker opened by that client — this statement is true.

d.text: Calling `.tenantId("myTenant")` opens a single-tenant worker exactly as the docs show — this statement is true.

explanation: Java job workers can be scoped to one tenant (`.tenantId`), to several (`.tenantIds`), or driven by client-wide defaults (`CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS` / `camunda.client.worker.tenantIds`). The critical authorisation rule is all-or-nothing: the client must be authorised for every configured tenant. If authorisation is missing for any one of them, the worker activates no jobs — not even for the tenants it is authorised for.

question: A team wants to attach `trace-id:3004` as an immutable label that travels with every job created during a process instance, for log correlation. Which job property is designed for this?

options.text: Tags — immutable labels copied from the process instance to each job at creation time.
Variables — the contextual business data of the process instance required by the worker.
Key — the unique identifier used to complete or fail the job.
Custom headers — static metadata defined per task in the BPMN model and shared across all instances.

a.text: The job-workers concept page lists Tags as immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation, with `reference:1234`, `team:accounting`, and `trace-id:3004` as the docs' own examples — exactly the per-instance correlation use case.

d.text: Custom headers are static metadata defined on the BPMN task in the model (e.g. a Slack channel a reusable worker reads), not copied per-instance from the process at job creation time.

b.text: Variables hold mutable contextual business data the worker uses to do its work; they are not designed as immutable correlation labels.

c.text: Key is the unique identifier the system assigns to a job for use with `complete`/`fail` commands — a system identifier, not a free-form label.

explanation: Tags are immutable labels copied from the process instance into every job at the moment the job is created; the docs name `reference`, `team`, and `trace-id` as canonical examples. Custom headers are per-task static metadata in the model, Variables are mutable business payload, and Key is the system handle used by `complete`/`fail` commands — none of those match a per-instance, immutable correlation label.

question: A team enables long polling on their Zeebe client by setting `request-timeout` on the `ActivateJobs` call. What does the cluster do when no job of the requested type is available?

options.text: It raises an incident on every queued process instance until a worker is available.
It returns an empty response immediately, and the worker re-polls on its own schedule.
It holds the request open and completes it as soon as a matching job becomes available, or when `request-timeout` elapses — whichever comes first.
It starts pushing every future job of that type to the worker over a long-lived stream for the duration of `request-timeout`.

c.text: Long polling keeps the activate-jobs request open while no jobs are available and completes it as soon as one becomes available; `request-timeout` sets the upper bound on how long the request waits before returning empty.

b.text: Returning immediately on empty is the ordinary (non-long-polling) behaviour the docs contrast against — it produces many wasted polls, which is exactly what long polling exists to avoid.

d.text: Long polling holds a single open request; it does not open a persistent push stream beyond that request. Job streaming is a separate Java-client feature.

a.text: Zeebe queues jobs while no worker is available — that is the docs' 'job queuing' insulation against worker outages — it does not raise incidents for absent workers.

explanation: With long polling, an `ActivateJobs` request is held open while no jobs of the requested type are available and completes as soon as one becomes available — or returns empty when `request-timeout` elapses. This avoids the repeated empty polls of plain polling. Zeebe also queues jobs while no workers are around, decoupling job creation from consumption so the system stays resilient to worker outages.

question: A worker fails a job with `remaining retries` set to `0` and supplies a `reason` variable. What happens next, and where does `reason` live?

options.text: An incident is raised; the `reason` variable is created only in the task's local scope and is not propagated to higher scopes.
The job is retried immediately and `reason` is merged up to higher scopes for visibility everywhere in the process.
The `retry back off` delays the next retry indefinitely; `reason` is attached automatically to the incident itself.
The process instance is cancelled, and `reason` is discarded because the job did not complete.

a.text: The docs state that when remaining retries is zero or negative, an incident is raised and the job is not retried until the incident is resolved. They also state that variables supplied when failing a job are created only in the local scope of the task — they are not merged upward like on completion.

b.text: Immediate retry only happens when remaining retries is greater than zero (and no `retry back off` is set). With retries at zero, an incident is raised instead.

d.text: An unresolved incident pauses progress at the failed job; it does not cancel the process instance.

c.text: `Retry back off` only delays retries that would otherwise occur, so it has no role when retries are already exhausted. Variables supplied on fail are not auto-attached to the incident.

explanation: Failing a job with zero (or negative) remaining retries raises an incident, and the process instance pauses at that job until the incident is resolved. Variables supplied on `fail` are scoped only to the local task; they propagate to higher scopes only on `complete`. This local-vs-merged scoping difference is a key contrast between the complete and fail commands and is often useful for capturing diagnostic context when an incident is later analysed.

question: A worker activated a job with a configured activation timeout and crashed before completing it. The timeout elapses. What does Zeebe do with the job and its remaining retries?

options.text: Zeebe decrements `remaining retries` by one and reassigns the job to another worker.
Zeebe completes the job with an empty payload so the process can continue.
Zeebe creates an incident on the job; it stays unactivated until an operator resolves it.
Zeebe reassigns the job to another worker without decrementing `remaining retries`.

d.text: Per the docs: if a job is not completed or failed within the configured activation timeout, Zeebe reassigns it to another worker. This does not affect the number of `remaining retries`.

a.text: Retries decrement on fail commands, not on activation timeouts. A timeout-driven reassignment leaves `remaining retries` unchanged.

c.text: Timeouts cause silent reassignment, not an incident. Incidents arise when retries are exhausted via fail commands or other errors.

b.text: Zeebe never auto-completes a job on timeout; that would skip the worker's actual work and break process semantics.

explanation: Activation timeouts make Zeebe an at-least-once system: when a job is not completed or failed in time, the broker simply reassigns it to another worker, and `remaining retries` is untouched. Workers must therefore be idempotent because two workers can briefly process the same job, with the second `complete job` rejected with a `NOT FOUND` error.

question: Two job worker clients open `StreamActivatedJobs` streams for the same job type. Under what condition will the gateway treat them as logically equivalent and aggregate them into one stream registered with each broker?

options.text: Same gateway endpoint and same TLS certificate, regardless of worker name.
Same job type only; worker name and timeout are ignored for aggregation.
Same job type and same tenant; activation timeout is decided broker-side.
Same job type, same worker name, same job activation timeout, and same fetch variables.

d.text: The docs define logically equivalent streams as those that would activate the same job in the exact same way: same job type, same worker name, same job activation timeout, and same fetch variables.

b.text: Worker name and activation timeout are explicitly part of the equivalence criteria, not optional aggregation hints.

a.text: Equivalence is about job activation semantics, not transport-level details like gateway endpoint or TLS.

c.text: Tenant is not in the equivalence list; the four criteria are job type, worker name, activation timeout, and fetch variables.

explanation: Job streaming aggregates logically equivalent streams on the gateway and registers the aggregate with each broker. Two streams are equivalent only when they target the same job type, worker name, activation timeout, and fetch variables — because they would otherwise activate the same job differently and could not safely share an aggregated stream.

question: A job worker's `StreamActivatedJobs` receive method is blocked, causing the gateway's send buffers to fill. The gateway marks this client as `not-ready`. What does the gateway do with the next pushed job for that job type?

options.text: Hold the job in the gateway buffer indefinitely until the slow client recovers.
Immediately fail the job and emit a `Job.YIELD` command back to the client.
Drop the job and decrement its `remaining retries` so it eventually creates an incident.
Re-route the job to another logically equivalent worker; if none on this gateway are ready, return the job to the broker.

d.text: Per the docs, if a client is not ready, the gateway tries to re-route the job to another logically equivalent worker. If that fails (e.g. all such workers on the gateway are not ready), the job is returned to the broker, which may retry to another gateway.

c.text: Backpressure-driven rerouting does not decrement retries; retries are unrelated to client readiness.

a.text: The gateway does not hold jobs indefinitely — that would defeat backpressure and could exhaust gateway memory.

b.text: `Job.YIELD` is a log-level signal of backpressure activity, not a per-job failure command sent to the client.

explanation: Backpressure relies on gRPC/HTTP-2 flow control. When a client stalls, its send buffers fill on the gateway and it becomes `not-ready`. The gateway first reroutes the job to another logically equivalent worker on the same gateway; only if none are ready does the job go back to the broker, which may then try a different gateway with a logically equivalent worker.

question: A worker for job type `foo` activates no jobs. Hitting `/actuator/jobstreams` on the gateway shows it as a client stream there, but on one broker it is missing from the remote stream's consumers. What does the troubleshooting guide recommend as the workaround?

options.text: Restart that gateway so its streams are recreated and re-registered with the brokers; this situation indicates a bug.
Restart the worker process and let it reopen the client stream.
Lower the worker's `maxJobsActive` so the broker stops applying backpressure to it.
Rename the job type so the broker re-registers the stream from scratch.

a.text: Per the docs, missing presence on a broker indicates a bug; the suggested workaround is to restart the gateway, which causes some service interruption but forces all streams for that gateway to be recreated properly.

b.text: Restarting the worker is the fix when the stream is missing from the gateway as a client stream, not from a broker as a remote-stream consumer.

c.text: `maxJobsActive` tuning is for queue sizing under backpressure, not for missing remote stream registrations.

d.text: Renaming the job type would change process semantics and is not a documented troubleshooting step.

explanation: The `/actuator/jobstreams` endpoint distinguishes client streams (on the gateway) from remote streams (the aggregated streams as seen by each broker). If a worker is present as a client stream but absent as a consumer on a broker, the docs flag it as a bug and suggest restarting that gateway to force its streams to be recreated. Restarting the worker is reserved for the case where the client stream itself is missing.

question: A process instance has tags `Priority:High` and `region:EU`. When a service task activates, a job is created. Later, an operator adds `Priority:Low` to the same process instance. What tag set will the worker see on that already-created job?

options.text: `Priority:High`, `Priority:Low`, and `region:EU` — tag updates on the process instance propagate to in-flight jobs.
`Priority:Low` and `region:EU` — a newer value for an existing tag name overrides the older one on the job.
Exactly `Priority:High` and `region:EU` — the tag set on the job is a snapshot taken at job creation and never changes.
An empty tag set, because `Priority` is case-sensitive and conflicts with `priority` reserved by the engine.

c.text: Per the docs, when a BPMN element is activated and creates a job, the job receives a copy of all tags from the process instance at that exact moment, and the tag set on the job is immutable thereafter.

a.text: Job tags are explicitly immutable after creation; later changes to the process instance do not flow into existing jobs.

b.text: Tags are not overwritten by name on the job — the entire tag set is a snapshot, frozen at creation.

d.text: `Priority` is not a reserved key, and case-sensitivity (`Priority:High` ≠ `priority:high`) has no bearing on emptiness here.

explanation: Job tags are a snapshot copy of the process instance tag set, taken once when the BPMN element is activated. After creation, the job's tag set cannot be modified, added to, or removed — including by changes made later on the parent process instance. Tags are case-sensitive, so `Priority:High` and `priority:high` would be treated as distinct entries.

question: An element template references `secrets.MY_TOKEN` and runs in a custom connector deployed as part of a Camunda 8 Run Docker Compose setup. Where do you provide the value for `MY_TOKEN`?

options.text: Add `MY_TOKEN=value` (one secret per line) to `connector-secrets.txt` in the root directory of the Camunda 8 Run setup.
Set it as a property in the element template JSON next to the field that references it.
Export `MY_TOKEN` as a shell environment variable before running `start.sh`; C8 Run reads it automatically.
Create a Kubernetes `Secret` named `MY_TOKEN` and mount it into the connector container.

a.text: The docs say to add secrets to `connector-secrets.txt` in the C8 Run root directory, one `NAME=VALUE` per line; the value is then exposed to the connector runtime as `secrets.NAME`.

b.text: Element templates only reference secrets; the values must be provisioned to the connector runtime, not embedded in the template.

c.text: C8 Run's documented mechanism for the Docker Compose setup is the `connector-secrets.txt` file, not arbitrary shell env vars on the host.

d.text: Kubernetes Secrets apply to Helm/K8s installs, not to a C8 Run Docker Compose setup; that section explicitly points to `connector-secrets.txt`.

explanation: When custom connectors are deployed as part of the C8 Run Docker Compose setup, secrets are provisioned via `connector-secrets.txt` in the root directory, one `NAME=VALUE` per line. The connector runtime then exposes each entry as `secrets.NAME`. Only when you run the connector outside the C8 Run compose setup does the docs guide you to configure secrets as environment variables instead.

question: How do you provision a custom connector runtime so that it executes jobs in a Camunda 8 Run setup?

options.text: Run `mvn install` on the connector project so it is registered in the local Maven repository that C8 Run reads on startup.
Place the `.jar` path in `connector-secrets.txt` and reference its name from the element template.
Add the connector's Maven coordinates to `application.yaml` under `camunda.connectors.custom` and restart.
Copy the connector's `*-with-dependencies.jar` into the `custom_connectors` directory in the root folder of the Camunda 8 Run setup, then start C8 Run.

d.text: The docs instruct you to build the fat jar with `mvn clean verify package` and copy the resulting `target/connector-template-*-with-dependencies.jar` into the `custom_connectors` directory in the C8 Run root, then start C8 Run with Docker Compose.

a.text: C8 Run does not consume the local Maven repository; the jar must be placed in the `custom_connectors` directory.

c.text: There is no `application.yaml` registration mechanism for custom connectors documented here — provisioning is jar drop-in.

b.text: `connector-secrets.txt` is for secrets, not for connector binaries; jars go into `custom_connectors`.

explanation: C8 Run picks up custom connector runtimes by reading the `custom_connectors` directory in its root folder. You build the connector as a fat jar (for example `mvn clean verify package`), drop the `*-with-dependencies.jar` into that directory, and start C8 Run with Docker Compose. The connector is then ready to execute jobs as soon as a process references it.

question: An element template references a form `user-signup.form`. You want a CI script — not Desktop Modeler — to deploy the form to a local Camunda 8 Run cluster. Which Orchestration Cluster REST API call does the docs example use?

options.text: A multipart `POST` to `/v2/deployments` with the form file uploaded as the `resources` field.
A `PUT` to `/v2/forms/user-signup` with the form file as the JSON body.
A `POST` to `/v1/forms` with the form file as `application/json`.
Drop the form file into the `custom_connectors` directory so C8 Run auto-deploys it on next start.

a.text: The docs show exactly this: a multipart `POST` to `/v2/deployments` with `-F resources=@.../user-signup.form`, returning a deployment payload that includes the form's `formKey`, `formId`, and `version`.

b.text: There is no `PUT /v2/forms/{id}` endpoint in this guide; deployments are created through `POST /v2/deployments`.

c.text: The example uses the v2 deployments endpoint, not a `v1/forms` JSON endpoint; resources must be sent as multipart form data, not JSON.

d.text: `custom_connectors` is for connector runtimes; forms are deployed via the Orchestration Cluster REST API or Desktop Modeler.

explanation: To deploy additional dependencies — forms, DMN diagrams, subprocesses — the docs recommend scripting against the Orchestration Cluster REST API with a multipart `POST /v2/deployments`. The response confirms the deployment with the resource's key, id, and version, and the element template can then reference it (for example `user-signup.form`). Desktop Modeler is the manual alternative for the same workflow.

question: Which statement about configuring logging for the Camunda Java client using the documented `log4j2.xml` example is NOT correct?

options.text: The file `log4j2.xml` must be on the classpath of your application.
The example uses a Console appender targeting `SYSTEM_OUT` with a `PatternLayout`.
The file must be named `logback.xml` and placed in `src/main/resources/META-INF/`.
The example sets the root logger level to `info`, so every log message is written to the console.

c.text: False — the docs configure the Java client using Log4j 2 via `log4j2.xml` on the classpath, not Logback's `logback.xml`. There is also no required `META-INF/` subpath.

a.text: True — the first instruction is to add a file called `log4j2.xml` to the classpath of the application.

b.text: True — the snippet declares `<Console name="Console" target="SYSTEM_OUT">` with a `<PatternLayout pattern="..."/>`.

d.text: True — `<Root level="info">` with the Console `AppenderRef` is what causes every log message to be written to the console.

explanation: The documented configuration uses Log4j 2, not Logback: a `log4j2.xml` file must be present on the application classpath. The example wires a Console appender to `SYSTEM_OUT` via a `PatternLayout`, and sets the root logger to `info` so every log message reaches the console. There is no requirement to name the file `logback.xml` or to place it under `META-INF/`.

question: Which set of operations does the Orchestration Cluster REST API let you perform on a Camunda 8 cluster?

options.text: Read-only access to process and task data — write operations require the Java client.
Only deploy BPMN definitions; runtime interaction must go through the Operate UI.
Only complete user tasks; process instance lifecycle requires a separate Tasklist API.
Start, manage, and query process instances; complete user tasks; resolve incidents; and manage variables.

d.text: The docs describe the API as a programmatic interface for process orchestration: starting, managing, and querying process instances, completing user tasks, resolving incidents, and managing variables — all at scale.

a.text: The API is explicitly write-capable; start, complete, and resolve operations are part of its core surface.

b.text: Deploying BPMN is one capability, but runtime orchestration (instances, tasks, incidents, variables) is also covered by the same API.

c.text: User task completion is included, but so is the full process lifecycle; there is no separate Tasklist API requirement for these operations.

explanation: The Orchestration Cluster REST API is the programmatic front door to Camunda 8's process orchestration capabilities. It covers the full lifecycle — start, manage, and query process instances; complete user tasks; resolve incidents; manage variables — and it is what enables process-driven applications, custom user task UIs, and external systems to start and monitor processes.

question: The Orchestration Cluster REST API is part of the Camunda 8 public API and is covered by SemVer stability guarantees. According to the docs, which endpoints are excluded from that guarantee?

options.text: Endpoints used by Operate or Tasklist UIs internally.
All endpoints under `/v2/` — only `/v1/` is stable.
Endpoints clearly marked as alpha.
There are no exclusions; every endpoint is covered without exception.

c.text: The docs state the API is covered by SemVer stability guarantees "except for clearly marked alpha endpoints," so alpha endpoints are the documented exclusion.

b.text: The `/v2/` path is the current stable API; the docs do not exclude entire path prefixes from SemVer.

a.text: Internal UI consumers are irrelevant to the public-API stability contract.

d.text: There is an explicit exception for clearly marked alpha endpoints, so this is not accurate.

explanation: The page positions the Orchestration Cluster REST API as a stable public-API surface backed by Camunda's SemVer guarantees. The only carve-out is endpoints that are clearly marked as alpha — those can change without backward-compatibility guarantees so the team can iterate on new functionality before promoting it to stable.

question: A developer just started Camunda 8 Run locally and wants to verify the connection with `curl http://localhost:8080/v2/topology`. What authentication does the default C8 Run setup require for this request?

options.text: None — for local development, Camunda 8 Run exposes the API without requiring credentials or tokens by default.
Basic authentication with the default `demo:demo` user.
An API key passed in the `X-Camunda-Auth` header.
An OAuth2/OIDC bearer token issued by Keycloak.

a.text: The prerequisites section says C8 Run (and Docker Compose) are intended for local development and expose the API without requiring credentials or tokens by default. The documented connection test is a plain `curl` with no auth header.

b.text: Basic authentication is listed as an option for simple shared setups, not as the C8 Run default.

d.text: OIDC tokens are recommended for production environments, not the default C8 Run local-dev mode.

c.text: There is no documented `X-Camunda-Auth` API-key scheme; the documented options are none, basic, and OIDC.

explanation: Camunda 8 Run (and Docker Compose) is designed for friction-free local development and exposes the Orchestration Cluster REST API without authentication by default. That is why the documented connection test is a plain `curl http://localhost:8080/v2/topology`. Basic auth or OIDC are then layered on for simple shared setups or production deployments respectively.

question: Looking at the endpoint `POST /user-tasks/{userTaskKey}/assignment`, which naming conventions of the Orchestration Cluster REST API does it illustrate?

options.text: Plural camelCase resource (`userTasks`), kebab-case identifier (`user-task-key`), and the noun `assignment`.
Plural kebab-case resource (`user-tasks`), camelCase `userTaskId` because path parameters never use the `Key` suffix, and a verb (`assign`).
Plural kebab-case noun for the top-level resource (`user-tasks`), camelCase identifier with a `Key` suffix in the path parameter (`userTaskKey`), and a noun (`assignment`) instead of a verb.
Singular snake_case resource (`user_task`), uppercase identifier (`USER_TASK_KEY`), and a verb (`assign`) as the action segment.

c.text: The docs list exactly these conventions: nouns over verbs (`assignment`, not `assign`), plural top-level resources (`user-tasks`), kebab-case in path segments, camelCase in path/query parameters, and the `Key` suffix for unique technical identifiers like `userTaskKey`.

d.text: snake_case, uppercase identifiers, and verbs as actions are all the opposite of the documented conventions.

a.text: Top-level resource segments are kebab-case (not camelCase), and identifiers are camelCase (not kebab-case).

b.text: Path parameters do use the `Key` suffix when referring to unique technical identifiers; `userTaskKey` is the documented example, and the action is the noun `assignment`.

explanation: The naming conventions section locks in four rules: nouns over verbs in action segments (`assignment`), plural top-level resources (`user-tasks`), kebab-case in path segments, and camelCase in path/query parameters (`userTaskKey`). Unique technical identifiers (usually numeric) are suffixed with `key`, distinguishing them from identifiers copied from BPMN XML, which are suffixed with `id`.

question: A client calling the Orchestration Cluster REST API starts receiving `HTTP 503` responses during a traffic spike. What does the docs recommend the client do?

options.text: Stop sending requests permanently and reauthenticate before any further calls.
Switch to a different cluster endpoint because `503` indicates the cluster is permanently down.
Treat it as a backpressure signal and eventually retry the requests after a backoff period.
Treat it like a `409 Conflict` and resend the request immediately without any backoff.

c.text: Per the docs, `503` means the service is currently unavailable, often because the system is signalling backpressure to prevent compute resources from being exhausted. Clients are recommended to eventually retry those requests after a backoff period.

a.text: `503` is not an authentication failure (that would be `401`), and the docs do not advise stopping all traffic permanently.

d.text: `409 Conflict` is a separate code meaning the resource is not in the correct state; conflating it with `503` and skipping the backoff defeats backpressure protection.

b.text: `503` is transient (backpressure or temporary unavailability), not a sign that the cluster is permanently gone.

explanation: The status-code reference describes `503` as the backpressure signal: the cluster is protecting itself from resource exhaustion and the appropriate client behaviour is to back off and retry, not to fail hard, reauthenticate, or hammer the endpoint. Other codes have distinct meanings — `400` for bad input, `401` for auth, `404` for missing resources, `409` for state conflicts, `412` for failed preconditions, and `500` for generic server errors.

question: A client receives an error response from the Orchestration Cluster REST API, formatted per RFC 9457. Which field is NOT one of the documented fields in that error body?

options.text: `type`
`instance`
`stackTrace`
`status`

c.text: The docs list `type`, `status`, `title`, `detail`, and `instance` as the fields in the RFC 9457 error response. `stackTrace` is not included — exposing internal stack traces over the public API surface is intentionally avoided.

a.text: `type` is documented as a URI identifier for the error type.

d.text: `status` is documented as the HTTP status code (e.g. 400, 404).

b.text: `instance` is documented as a URI reference identifying the specific occurrence of the problem.

explanation: The error-handling section pins the response shape to RFC 9457 (Problem Details for HTTP APIs) with five fields: `type`, `status`, `title`, `detail`, and `instance`. There is no `stackTrace`, `cause`, or other internal-server detail in the contract — clients parse these documented fields plus the HTTP status code.

question: A client integrates against the Operate API v1 and needs the raw BPMN XML for a deployed `process-definitions/{key}`. According to the Operate API overview, how is the XML representation surfaced?

options.text: By setting an `Accept: application/xml` header on the standard `process-definitions/{key}` request; the same endpoint negotiates JSON or XML.
By calling the Zeebe gateway gRPC `GetProcess` RPC, since Operate itself only returns JSON.
By exporting the deployment from Operate's UI; the API does not expose the XML form.
Through an additional endpoint on `process-definitions` that returns the process definition as XML.

d.text: The overview states that requests and responses are JSON, but some objects have additional endpoints — and `process-definitions` specifically has an endpoint to get the process-definition as XML representation.

a.text: The overview does not describe content negotiation on the JSON endpoint; the XML form is exposed as a separate additional endpoint, not via an `Accept` header.

b.text: The overview is about the Operate API itself; it does not redirect XML retrieval to a Zeebe gRPC RPC.

c.text: The API does expose the XML, via an additional endpoint on `process-definitions`; there is no need to fall back to the Operate UI.

explanation: The Operate API overview is explicit: requests and responses are JSON, but some objects have additional endpoints — and `process-definitions` in particular has an endpoint to retrieve the process definition as its XML representation.

question: Which statement about the Operate API v1 Swagger UI limitation is NOT correct?

options.text: The limitation also applies to the Orchestration Cluster REST API v.2, which returns `id` and `key` as numbers.
If you need precise IDs to use in other queries, use another REST client such as Postman or `curl` instead of the Swagger UI.
The root cause is that Zeebe `key` values can exceed JavaScript's `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` (`2^53-1`).
The Swagger UI may display incorrect `id` and `key` values for data created on Zeebe partitions `>= 4`.

d.text: True. The docs explicitly call out that the Swagger UI may display incorrect `id`/`key` values for data created on Zeebe partitions `>= 4`.

c.text: True. The cause is that Zeebe may generate `key` values greater than JavaScript's `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` (`2^53-1`), which the Swagger UI cannot represent precisely.

a.text: False — and therefore the correct answer to a NOT question. The Orchestration Cluster REST API v.2 returns `id` and `key` as strings, so the JS number-precision limitation does not apply to it.

b.text: True. The docs recommend using Postman or `curl` when you need to copy exact IDs out of responses to reuse in other queries.

explanation: The Operate API overview warns that the Swagger UI may misrender `id`/`key` for partitions `>= 4` because Zeebe keys can exceed `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`. The mitigation is to use Postman/`curl` or to use the Orchestration Cluster REST API v.2, which returns those values as strings — so the v.2 API does not share the limitation.

question: A client posts an empty body `{}` to `/v1/process-instances/search`. Per the Operate API query defaults, which statement about the resulting query is NOT correct?

options.text: Results are sorted by `key` ascending by default.
The default page size is `50` items per response.
Up to `10` items are returned per response by default.
All items match because the default `filter` is `null` (empty).

c.text: True. The `size` component defaults to `10`.

d.text: True. The default `filter` is `null`, meaning empty / all fields match.

a.text: True. The default `sort` is `[{"field":"key","order":"ASC"}]`.

b.text: False — the correct answer to a NOT question. The default size is `10`, not `50`; `50` only appears in an example that explicitly sets `size`.

explanation: The Query section defines defaults: `filter=null` (all match), `size=10`, `sort=[{"field":"key","order":"ASC"}]`, and `searchAfter=null`. So the only incorrect statement is the one claiming a default page size of 50.

question: Given the `filter` above on `startDate`, what does the `||/m` modifier match in the Operate API query?

options.text: Items whose `startDate` is within `1` millisecond of `2022-03-17T11:50:25.729+0000`.
Items whose `startDate` exactly equals the provided ISO-8601 timestamp; the suffix is ignored.
Items whose `startDate` falls within the same month as `2022-03-17`.
Items whose `startDate` falls within the same minute as `2022-03-17 11:50:25`.

d.text: Correct. The modifier table maps `||/m` to "Within a minute", so the filter matches all items whose `startDate` is in the same minute as the given timestamp.

c.text: `||/M` (uppercase M) means "Within a month". Lowercase `||/m` is "Within a minute", which is what the example uses.

a.text: There is no millisecond modifier; the finest-grained option in the table is `||/s` ("Within a second").

b.text: The modifier is significant; without it, the filter would require an exact-value match on the date. With `||/m`, Operate broadens the match to the minute window.

explanation: Date filters take the form `yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSSZZ` with an optional range modifier appended via `||/<unit>`. The example uses `||/m` (lowercase) which maps to "Within a minute", so any item whose `startDate` is in the same minute as `2022-03-17 11:50:25` matches.

question: An Operate API search response includes `"total": 10000`. Per the Results section, what does this value tell you about the matching data set?

options.text: `10,000` is the maximum allowed value of `size` for any single Operate query.
`items` was truncated to `10,000` entries and you must page with `searchAfter` to receive the remaining ones in the same response.
`total` is only guaranteed exact up to `10,000`; the true number of matches may be higher, and the docs suggest making the query more specific.
Exactly `10,000` items matched; `total` is always an exact count regardless of magnitude.

c.text: Correct. The Results docs state that `total` is an exact value until `10,000`; if there are more matches, you should try to make the query more specific.

d.text: `total` is only guaranteed exact up to `10,000` — above that ceiling its precision is not guaranteed, contrary to this claim.

a.text: The `10,000` ceiling applies to the exactness of `total`, not to the value you can set for `size`. The docs do not define `size`'s upper bound here.

b.text: `total` describes the total number of matches, not the size of `items` in the response. Pagination uses `searchAfter` with values from `sortValues`, independent of the `total` figure.

explanation: The Results section says the `total` field is exact up to `10,000` matches; if the matching set is larger, `total` ceases to be exact and the recommendation is to narrow the filter. The `items` array's size is controlled by `size`, not by `total`.

question: A Spring Boot worker publishes a message to Camunda without setting a TTL on the publish command and without overriding any `camunda.client.*` property. How long does the message remain buffered, per the starter defaults?

options.text: `PT10S` — the default of `camunda.client.request-timeout`.
`PT1H` — the default of `camunda.client.message-time-to-live`.
`PT5M` — the default job timeout.
Indefinitely, until a process instance correlates with it.

b.text: The properties reference lists `camunda.client.message-time-to-live` with a default value of `PT1H`, which is the default TTL used when no value is supplied on the publish command.

a.text: `PT10S` is the default `camunda.client.request-timeout` — the gRPC/REST request timeout, not the message TTL.

c.text: `PT5M` is the default job-worker `timeout` (`camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout`), unrelated to message TTL.

d.text: Messages do not buffer indefinitely; without an explicit TTL the starter applies `camunda.client.message-time-to-live`, which defaults to `PT1H`.

explanation: The Spring Boot starter property `camunda.client.message-time-to-live` provides the default TTL for messages published without an explicit value, and its default is `PT1H`. The request timeout (`PT10S`) and the worker timeout (`PT5M`) are different concerns.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: With the Camunda Spring Boot starter at default settings, which transport does the client pick for an operation that is supported by both gRPC and REST?

options.text: gRPC, because gRPC is always the primary transport for Zeebe operations.
REST, because `camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc` defaults to `true`.
REST or gRPC at random — the starter has no preference until you set `camunda.client.mode`.
It depends on `camunda.client.max-message-size`; payloads above `5MB` are sent over REST and smaller ones over gRPC.

b.text: The properties reference defines `camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc` with a default value of `true`, meaning REST is preferred whenever both protocols support an operation.

a.text: gRPC is not unconditionally preferred; the starter's default actually flips the preference toward REST via `prefer-rest-over-grpc: true`.

c.text: The starter has a deterministic preference (`true`) — REST is the default. `camunda.client.mode` only governs SaaS vs self-managed defaults for addresses.

d.text: `camunda.client.max-message-size` sets the maximum inbound gRPC message size (default `5MB`); it does not switch transports based on payload size.

explanation: The `camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc` property defaults to `true`, so operations supported by both protocols are issued over REST. `camunda.client.mode` and `max-message-size` are unrelated concerns.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A team scales out a Spring Boot application that runs several job workers but never sets `camunda.client.execution-threads`. How many threads does the starter use by default to invoke those job workers?

options.text: One thread per available CPU core, detected at startup.
`32`, matching `camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active`.
`100`, matching `camunda.client.max-http-connections`.

a.text: The properties reference gives `camunda.client.execution-threads` a default value of `1`, so a single thread is used for job-worker invocation unless overridden.

c.text: `32` is the default `camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active` (jobs activated per worker), not the number of execution threads.

d.text: `100` is the default `camunda.client.max-http-connections`, a transport concern unrelated to worker thread count.

b.text: The starter does not auto-size threads to CPU cores; the documented default is the fixed value `1`.

explanation: `camunda.client.execution-threads` defaults to `1`. Worker concurrency is bounded by this thread count unless the value is raised, regardless of `max-jobs-active` or HTTP connection limits.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: Which statement about `camunda.client.auth.*` in the Spring Boot starter is NOT correct?

options.text: `camunda.client.auth.method` defaults to `basic` when not explicitly configured.
`camunda.client.auth.connect-timeout` and `camunda.client.auth.read-timeout` both default to `PT5S`.
`camunda.client.auth.method` accepts the values `none`, `basic`, and `oidc`.
`camunda.client.auth.credentials-cache-path` defaults to `$HOME/.camunda/credentials`.

c.text: True. The reference lists the property as `enum[none, basic, oidc]`.

b.text: True. Both timeout properties show a default of `PT5S`.

d.text: True. The default for `credentials-cache-path` is exactly `$HOME/.camunda/credentials`.

a.text: False — and therefore the correct answer to a NOT question. The default for `camunda.client.auth.method` is `null`; the actual method is auto-detected from the presence of `username`/`password`/`client-id`/`client-secret`, or set by `camunda.client.mode: saas`.

explanation: All other statements come straight from the auth properties table. `camunda.client.auth.method` defaults to `null` and is auto-detected from configured credentials or the client mode; it is not hard-defaulted to `basic`.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: What is the default value of `camunda.client.auth.connect-timeout` in the Camunda Spring Boot starter?

options.text: `null` — no timeout is applied by default.
`PT1S`
`PT10S`
`PT5S`

d.text: The properties reference lists `camunda.client.auth.connect-timeout` ("connection timeout for requests to the OAuth credentials provider") with a default of `PT5S`.

c.text: `PT10S` is the default for `camunda.client.request-timeout`, not the OAuth connect timeout.

b.text: `PT1S` is the default for `camunda.client.request-timeout-offset`, not the auth connect timeout.

a.text: The auth connect timeout has a concrete default of `PT5S`; it is not unset.

explanation: Both `camunda.client.auth.connect-timeout` and `camunda.client.auth.read-timeout` default to `PT5S` for requests to the OAuth credentials provider. Don't confuse these with `request-timeout` (`PT10S`) or the timeout offset (`PT1S`).

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A Spring Boot application sets only `camunda.client.auth.username` and `camunda.client.auth.password` and leaves `camunda.client.auth.method` unset. Which authentication method does the starter choose?

options.text: Startup fails — `camunda.client.auth.method` is mandatory and has no auto-detection.
`none`, because `method` is `null` and no explicit method was selected.
`basic`, because the method is auto-detected from the presence of a username and password.
`oidc`, because the auth client only supports OAuth flows when configured via Spring Boot.

c.text: The properties reference says of `camunda.client.auth.method`: "If not set, it is detected based on the presence of a username, password, client ID, and client secret." Configuring only username and password resolves to `basic`.

d.text: OIDC is auto-selected when `client-id` and `client-secret` are present, not when only `username`/`password` are configured.

b.text: `none` is one of the enum values, but it is not the auto-detected outcome when basic credentials are supplied.

a.text: The property is not mandatory; the starter explicitly auto-detects an appropriate method when it is left unset.

explanation: `camunda.client.auth.method` has an enum of `none|basic|oidc` and a `null` default. The docs state the method is auto-detected from configured credentials, so username+password → `basic`, and client-id+client-secret → `oidc`.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A Spring Boot application configures `camunda.client.auth.client-assertion.keystore-path` and `keystore-password` but leaves both `keystore-key-alias` and `keystore-key-password` unset. Which statement about this configuration is NOT correct?

options.text: `keystore-password` unlocks the keystore referenced by `keystore-path`.
The first alias in the keystore is used to sign the client assertion certificate when `keystore-key-alias` is unset.
Leaving `keystore-key-alias` unset causes the assertion to fail because no signing key can be selected.
The keystore password is reused as the key password when `keystore-key-password` is unset.

d.text: True. The reference for `keystore-key-password` states: "If not set, the keystore password is used."

b.text: True. The reference for `keystore-key-alias` states: "If not set, the first alias from the keystore is used."

a.text: True. `keystore-password` is documented as "The password of the referenced keystore" — i.e. unlocks the keystore at `keystore-path`.

c.text: False — therefore the correct answer to a NOT question. The docs explicitly fall back to the first alias when `keystore-key-alias` is unset, so the assertion does not fail simply because the alias was omitted.

explanation: The `camunda.client.auth.client-assertion.*` table documents two fallbacks: an unset `keystore-key-alias` falls back to the first alias in the keystore, and an unset `keystore-key-password` falls back to the `keystore-password`. So the only false statement is that omitting the alias causes failure.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A team needs to point their Spring Boot starter at a non-production Camunda SaaS environment while keeping their existing `cluster-id`, `region`, and OAuth credentials. Which property in `camunda.client.cloud.*` is intended for this override?

options.text: `camunda.client.cloud.port`
`camunda.client.cloud.domain`
`camunda.client.cloud.region`
`camunda.client.cloud.cluster-id`

b.text: The properties reference describes `camunda.client.cloud.domain` as "The domain the Camunda client connects to. Change this to connect to a non-production instance of Camunda Cloud." That is exactly the documented use case.

d.text: `cluster-id` identifies a specific cluster; the requirement is to keep the existing cluster-id while moving to non-production.

c.text: `region` identifies the SaaS region; the requirement is to keep the existing region.

a.text: `port` overrides the port the client connects to but is not the documented switch for non-production SaaS endpoints.

explanation: Only `camunda.client.cloud.domain` is documented as the switch for connecting to non-production SaaS instances. Cluster-id and region identify the cluster and region; port is a transport detail.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: With no per-worker overrides, what is the default value of `camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout` — the time a job remains exclusively assigned to a worker?

options.text: `PT10S` — matching the default request timeout.
`PT1H` — matching the default message TTL.
`PT0.1S` — matching the default poll interval.
`PT5M`

d.text: The properties reference for `camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout` ("The time a job remains exclusively assigned to the worker.") gives a default of `PT5M`.

c.text: `PT0.1S` is the default `poll-interval`, not the job lock timeout.

a.text: `PT10S` is `camunda.client.request-timeout`, unrelated to how long a job remains locked to a worker.

b.text: `PT1H` is the default `camunda.client.message-time-to-live`, not the worker job timeout.

explanation: `camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout` controls how long an activated job stays exclusively locked to the worker, and its default is `PT5M`. Poll interval (`PT0.1S`), request timeout (`PT10S`) and message TTL (`PT1H`) are separate properties.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A team reports that new jobs are not eagerly pushed to their Spring Boot workers — the workers always wait for the next poll. They have not touched `camunda.client.worker.defaults.stream-enabled`. What is the default, and what does it mean for how the worker fetches jobs?

options.text: The default is `true`, but polling and streaming are mutually exclusive — enabling streaming would have disabled the existing poll.
The default is `false`; streaming is opt-in, so the worker uses polling only until the team sets `stream-enabled: true`.
The default is `null`, which falls back to streaming whenever a stream-timeout is configured.
The default is `true`; the worker should already be streaming, so the symptom indicates a broker outage.

b.text: The properties reference describes `stream-enabled` as an "Opt-in feature flag that enables job streaming" with a default of `false`. When disabled, the worker uses polling alone; enabling it makes the worker use both streaming and polling.

d.text: The default is `false`, not `true`; no broker fault needs to be assumed to explain the absence of pushed jobs.

c.text: The default is the boolean literal `false`, not `null`; `stream-timeout` only matters once streaming is enabled.

a.text: When streaming is enabled, the worker uses both streaming and polling — they are explicitly described as complementary, not mutually exclusive.

explanation: `camunda.client.worker.defaults.stream-enabled` defaults to `false`. While disabled, workers rely solely on polling at `poll-interval` (default `PT0.1S`). Enabling streaming makes the worker open a long-lived stream that eagerly pushes new jobs, with polling continuing to pick up jobs created before the stream opened.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: An `application.yaml` contains the snippet above. What does this override do for the worker registered with `@JobWorker(type = "payment-charge")`?

codeBlocks.stem:
camunda:
  client:
    worker:
      override:
        payment-charge:
          max-jobs-active: 5

options.text: Sets the worker's executor thread pool size to 5.
Caps the total number of jobs of any type across all workers in the application at 5.
Sets the maximum number of retries for `payment-charge` jobs to 5.
Caps the number of `payment-charge` jobs that worker may have exclusively activated at the same time to 5.

d.text: The key of an override under `camunda.client.worker.override` is the job type, so `payment-charge.max-jobs-active=5` applies only to that worker. The docs define `max-jobs-active` as the maximum number of jobs exclusively activated for this worker at the same time.

b.text: `max-jobs-active` is a per-worker setting under the per-job-type override; it does not impose a global cap across workers.

a.text: Thread pool sizing is governed by `execution-threads` on the client, not by `max-jobs-active`, which controls the activation backlog.

c.text: Retry counts are configured via the separate `max-retries` property, not via `max-jobs-active`.

explanation: Under `camunda.client.worker.override`, the key is the job type. Setting `payment-charge.max-jobs-active=5` overrides only the `payment-charge` worker's activation backlog — the maximum number of jobs the broker will exclusively assign to it concurrently. It does not change retries, thread pools, or affect other workers.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: Which statement about properties under `camunda.client.worker.override.<job-type>` is FALSE?

options.text: The default value of `auto-complete` for every worker is `true` whenever the override is present.
Setting `enabled=false` disables the matching job worker.
The key of the override is the job type, so each override applies only to that specific worker.
`max-retries` caps the number of retries before automatic complete, fail, or BPMN error responses are no longer attempted.

c.text: The docs state explicitly that the key of the override is the job type, so this is true and is not the false statement.

b.text: `enabled` under the override toggles the matching job worker on or off, so this is correctly stated.

d.text: `max-retries` is documented as the maximum number of retries before automatic responses (complete, fail, BPMN error) for jobs are no longer attempted, so this is true.

a.text: This is the false statement: the documented default for every property under `camunda.client.worker.override.<job-type>`, including `auto-complete`, is `null`. The override only takes effect when you explicitly set a value.

explanation: Every property listed under `camunda.client.worker.override.<job-type>` has `null` as its default value in the reference. An override applies only when explicitly set; it does not silently force `auto-complete` (or any other field) to `true` just by existing.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: A worker is annotated as shown and the properties also declare `camunda.client.worker.override.charge-card.fetch-variables=["customerId"]`. Which variables does the broker fetch on activation?

codeBlocks.stem:
@JobWorker(type = "charge-card", fetchVariables = {"orderId", "amount"})
public void chargeCard(JobClient client, ActivatedJob job) { ... }

options.text: Only `orderId` and `amount` — the annotation always wins over the override.
All process variables, because any override implicitly forces fetching everything.
`orderId`, `amount`, and `customerId` — the override extends the annotation's list.
Only `customerId` — the override replaces the list from the annotation.

d.text: The docs are explicit: when `fetch-variables` is set in an override, it replaces the list of variables to fetch. So only `customerId` is fetched on activation.

c.text: Extension behaviour applies only when `fetch-variables` is set in `defaults`. For an override under a specific job type, the list is replaced, not merged.

b.text: Fetching all variables is controlled by `force-fetch-all-variables`, which is not set here. Setting `fetch-variables` in an override does not force fetching all.

a.text: The override wins for that job type: it explicitly replaces the annotation's `fetchVariables` list, so the annotation's values are not used.

explanation: Under `camunda.client.worker.override.<job-type>.fetch-variables`, the docs state that when set in an override, the list replaces the variables to fetch. To extend rather than replace, the property must be set under `camunda.client.worker.defaults` instead.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: An older `application.yaml` configures a SaaS connection via the now-deprecated `camunda.client.cluster-id`. Which property is its documented replacement?

options.text: `camunda.client.zeebe.cluster-id`
`camunda.client.worker.defaults.cluster-id`
`camunda.client.tenant-id`
`camunda.client.cloud.cluster-id`
