d.text: The deprecation table for `camunda.client` maps `camunda.client.cluster-id` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_CLUSTERID`) to `camunda.client.cloud.cluster-id` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_CLOUD_CLUSTERID`).

a.text: `camunda.client.zeebe.*` is itself the deprecated Zeebe-client namespace; it does not host the SaaS cluster id.

c.text: `tenant-id` selects a multi-tenancy tenant for requests; it does not identify a SaaS cluster.

b.text: Per-worker defaults under `worker.defaults` cover worker behaviour (autocomplete, fetch variables, timeouts) and do not include the cluster id.

explanation: SaaS-specific identifiers such as the cluster id and region now live under the `camunda.client.cloud.*` namespace. The deprecated table maps `camunda.client.cluster-id` directly to `camunda.client.cloud.cluster-id` so existing configurations migrate cleanly.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: While upgrading a Spring Boot app that uses `camunda.client.mode: saas`, you find the deprecated `camunda.client.cloud.base-url` in the config. Which property is its documented replacement?

options.text: `camunda.client.cloud.region`
`camunda.client.cloud.domain`
`camunda.client.base-url`
`camunda.client.rest-address`

b.text: The `camunda.client.cloud` deprecation table lists `camunda.client.cloud.domain` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_CLOUD_DOMAIN`) as the documented replacement for `camunda.client.cloud.base-url`.

d.text: `camunda.client.rest-address` is the unified REST endpoint used when you wire an address directly; it does not compose SaaS connection details from cluster id, region, and domain.

a.text: `camunda.client.cloud.region` selects the geographic region of the SaaS cluster; it is not the base URL.

c.text: There is no `camunda.client.base-url` property in the reference; SaaS connections are assembled from the `cloud.*` properties.

explanation: When `camunda.client.mode: saas`, the client composes its endpoints from `camunda.client.cloud.cluster-id`, `camunda.client.cloud.region`, and `camunda.client.cloud.domain`. The legacy `cloud.base-url` is deprecated in favour of `cloud.domain`.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: While migrating a self-managed Spring SDK app you find `camunda.client.identity.audience` and `camunda.client.identity.scope` in the config. Which replacements does the Camunda 8.8 reference prescribe, and why?

options.text: `camunda.client.identity.token-audience` and `camunda.client.identity.token-scope` — only the property suffix changes.
No replacement is needed; both properties still work because Identity remains a separate component.
`camunda.client.cloud.audience` and `camunda.client.cloud.scope` — Identity values now belong to the cloud namespace.
`camunda.client.auth.audience` and `camunda.client.auth.scope` — Identity is now part of Camunda, so auth settings live under `camunda.client.auth`.

d.text: The deprecation table for `camunda.client.identity` maps `audience` to `camunda.client.auth.audience` and `scope` to `camunda.client.auth.scope`, with the note that Identity is now part of Camunda.

a.text: There is no `identity.token-audience` or `identity.token-scope` namespace in the reference; the identity namespace as a whole is deprecated.

b.text: These properties are explicitly listed as deprecated. The replacement namespace is `camunda.client.auth`, not `camunda.client.identity`.

c.text: `camunda.client.cloud.*` carries SaaS cluster connection details (domain, region, cluster id), not OAuth audience or scope.

explanation: Because Identity is now part of Camunda, OAuth client-credentials properties move out of `camunda.client.identity.*` and into the unified `camunda.client.auth.*` namespace. `audience` and `scope` keep their leaf names and pair with the rest of the auth settings (token URL, client id, client secret).

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: In the deprecated `camunda.client.zeebe` namespace, which property is the documented replacement for `camunda.client.zeebe.base-url`?

options.text: `camunda.client.rest-address`
`camunda.client.cloud.base-url`
`camunda.client.grpc-address`
`camunda.client.zeebe.address`

a.text: The `camunda.client.zeebe` deprecation table maps `base-url` to `camunda.client.rest-address` — the unified REST endpoint for the Camunda 8 client.

c.text: `camunda.client.grpc-address` is the replacement for the separate `camunda.client.zeebe.grpc-address` property, not for `base-url`.

b.text: `cloud.base-url` is itself a deprecated SaaS-composition property, replaced by `cloud.domain`; it is not the replacement for `zeebe.base-url`.

d.text: There is no `camunda.client.zeebe.address` property in the reference; the Zeebe-specific namespace is being phased out, not renamed in place.

explanation: Camunda 8 unifies REST traffic for the orchestration cluster behind a single endpoint, configured by `camunda.client.rest-address`. The Zeebe-specific `base-url` from the deprecated namespace maps onto that unified address.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: Which mapping from a deprecated `camunda.client.zeebe.*` property to its documented replacement is NOT correct per the Camunda 8.8 reference?

options.text: `camunda.client.zeebe.execution-threads` → `camunda.client.execution-threads`
`camunda.client.zeebe.audience` → `camunda.client.auth.audience`
`camunda.client.zeebe.scope` → `camunda.client.scope`
`camunda.client.zeebe.prefer-rest-over-grpc` → `camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc`

b.text: The deprecation table maps `zeebe.audience` to `camunda.client.auth.audience`, so this mapping is correctly stated.

a.text: The table maps `zeebe.execution-threads` to `camunda.client.execution-threads`, so this mapping is correct.

d.text: The table maps `zeebe.prefer-rest-over-grpc` to `camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc`, so this is correct as well.

c.text: This is the incorrect mapping. The documented replacement for `zeebe.scope` is `camunda.client.auth.scope` (in the auth namespace), not `camunda.client.scope`. Like `audience`, OAuth scope moves under `auth`.

explanation: OAuth properties from the deprecated `camunda.client.zeebe` namespace (such as `audience` and `scope`) move into `camunda.client.auth`, not directly under `camunda.client`. Non-auth settings (timeouts, addresses, threading, message size) move to the top-level `camunda.client.*` namespace.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: An `application.yaml` still carries `camunda.client.zeebe.max-message-size=4MB`. To which Camunda 8.8 property should it be renamed?

options.text: `camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-message-size`
`camunda.client.zeebe.message-size-max`
`camunda.client.max-message-size`
`camunda.client.rest.max-message-size`

c.text: The deprecation table maps `camunda.client.zeebe.max-message-size` to `camunda.client.max-message-size` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_MAXMESSAGESIZE`). Transport sizing moves up to the top-level client namespace.

b.text: There is no renamed `message-size-max` property; the rename only drops the `zeebe.` segment from the path.

a.text: `worker.defaults.*` covers job worker defaults (autocomplete, fetch variables, timeouts), not transport-level message size.

d.text: There is no `camunda.client.rest.max-message-size` property in the reference; the size limit is configured once on the client, not per protocol.

explanation: Transport-level settings from the deprecated `camunda.client.zeebe` namespace — `max-message-size`, `max-metadata-size`, `keep-alive`, `request-timeout`, `override-authority` — are promoted to the top-level `camunda.client.*` namespace as part of the unified Camunda 8 client.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: An older config sets `camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.auto-complete=false` for all job workers. Which Camunda 8.8 property replaces it directly?

options.text: `camunda.client.worker.defaults.auto-complete`
`camunda.client.zeebe.auto-complete`
`camunda.client.auto-complete`
`camunda.client.worker.auto-complete`

a.text: The deprecation table for `camunda.client.zeebe.defaults` maps `auto-complete` directly to `camunda.client.worker.defaults.auto-complete` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_AUTOCOMPLETE`).

b.text: `camunda.client.zeebe.*` is itself deprecated; worker defaults are migrating out of that namespace, not into a shorter form under it.

c.text: There is no top-level `camunda.client.auto-complete` property; the setting is scoped to job worker defaults.

d.text: The defaults block lives under `camunda.client.worker.defaults`, not directly under `camunda.client.worker`. Per-job-type overrides live under `worker.override.<job-type>`.

explanation: Default settings for all job workers have moved from `camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.*` to `camunda.client.worker.defaults.*`. Leaf property names are preserved during the rename, so `auto-complete` continues to control whether the SDK completes jobs automatically after the worker method returns.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: What is the documented replacement for the deprecated `camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.force-fetch-all-variables` property?

options.text: `camunda.client.worker.defaults.force-fetch-all-variables`
`camunda.client.zeebe.force-fetch-all-variables`
`camunda.client.worker.override.force-fetch-all-variables`
`camunda.client.force-fetch-all-variables`

a.text: The deprecation table lists `camunda.client.worker.defaults.force-fetch-all-variables` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_FORCEFETCHALLVARIABLES`) as the direct replacement.

c.text: `worker.override` is keyed by job type for per-worker overrides; it is not where you set the default for every worker.

b.text: The entire `camunda.client.zeebe` namespace is deprecated; worker defaults now sit under `camunda.client.worker.defaults`.

d.text: There is no top-level `camunda.client.force-fetch-all-variables` property; the setting is scoped to worker defaults, not the client as a whole.

explanation: When migrating from the legacy `camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.*` namespace, each leaf property keeps its name and moves under `camunda.client.worker.defaults.*`. `force-fetch-all-variables` overrides `fetch-variables` so the worker pulls every process variable on activation.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: A team relies on `camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.stream-enabled=true` to opt every worker into job streaming. Which single rename preserves the same behaviour in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Remove it entirely — job streaming is on by default in 8.8 and is no longer configurable.
Rename it to `camunda.client.worker.defaults.stream-enabled`.
Rename it to `camunda.client.zeebe.stream-enabled`.
Rename it to `camunda.client.stream-enabled` at the top level.

b.text: The deprecation table maps `camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.stream-enabled` directly to `camunda.client.worker.defaults.stream-enabled`. The opt-in streaming feature flag is still configured per worker default.

c.text: The entire `camunda.client.zeebe` namespace is deprecated. The stream-enabled flag does not live there in 8.8.

a.text: Job streaming is still described as an opt-in feature flag in the docs; it is not silently enabled for every worker.

d.text: There is no top-level `camunda.client.stream-enabled` property; the setting is scoped to worker defaults, with per-job-type overrides under `worker.override.<job-type>`.

explanation: All worker default properties from the deprecated `camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.*` namespace move to `camunda.client.worker.defaults.*` with the same leaf names. `stream-enabled` remains an opt-in flag that pairs polling with a long-lived stream so the broker eagerly pushes new jobs.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: An older config still sets `common.url=https://camunda.example.com`. Which Camunda 8.8 property is the documented replacement, given that the REST address is the unified endpoint for all interaction with Camunda?

options.text: `camunda.client.cloud.base-url`
`camunda.client.url`
`common.base-url`
`camunda.client.rest-address`

d.text: The `common` deprecation table maps `common.url` to `camunda.client.rest-address` and explicitly notes that the REST address is the unified endpoint for all interaction with Camunda.

b.text: There is no `camunda.client.url` property in the reference; addressing flows through `rest-address` (and `grpc-address` for the gRPC channel).

a.text: `cloud.base-url` is itself a deprecated SaaS-composition property, replaced by `cloud.domain`. It is not the replacement for `common.url`.

c.text: `common.base-url` is another deprecated alias in the same table; it also redirects to `camunda.client.rest-address`, not back into the `common.*` namespace.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 funnels REST traffic for the orchestration cluster and its component APIs through a single REST address, `camunda.client.rest-address`. Both `common.url` and `common.base-url` from the legacy namespace are redirected onto it.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: A team is migrating off the deprecated `common.keycloak.*` namespace. They previously set `common.keycloak.url` and `common.keycloak.realm`. What does the Camunda 8.8 reference prescribe?

options.text: Rename `common.keycloak.realm` to `camunda.client.auth.realm` — only `realm` is renamed; `url` continues to work unchanged.
Keep `common.keycloak.url` and `common.keycloak.realm` — they are still the recommended way to point at Keycloak in self-managed deployments.
Set `camunda.client.keycloak.url` and `camunda.client.keycloak.realm` — Keycloak remains a first-class identity provider with its own namespace.
Set `camunda.client.auth.token-url` to the issuer URL and drop `realm` — there is no Keycloak-specific configuration for Camunda; the issuer is provided as a URL.

d.text: The deprecation table maps both `common.keycloak.url` and `common.keycloak.token-url` to `camunda.client.auth.token-url`, with the explicit hint that there is no Keycloak-specific configuration for Camunda — the issuer is provided as a URL. `common.keycloak.realm` has no replacement entry for the same reason.

c.text: There is no `camunda.client.keycloak.*` namespace in the reference; the docs state plainly that Keycloak-specific configuration is no longer used by Camunda.

a.text: `realm` is not renamed under `auth.realm`. The docs collapse Keycloak's `<base>/realms/<realm>/...` structure into the single issuer URL the user supplies.

b.text: Both properties are listed in the deprecation table, so they are not the recommended ongoing way to configure auth.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 treats the OAuth issuer as an opaque URL set via `camunda.client.auth.token-url`. The legacy `common.keycloak.*` properties — including `realm`, which has no direct replacement — were specific to one identity provider and are deprecated; the issuer URL is now the only Keycloak-related setting needed.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: An older config carries `zeebe.client.connection-mode=ADDRESS`. Which Camunda 8.8 property is the documented replacement, given that client modes are now available?

options.text: `zeebe.client.mode`
`camunda.client.connection-mode`
`camunda.client.mode`
`camunda.client.zeebe.mode`

c.text: The `zeebe.client` deprecation table maps `connection-mode` to `camunda.client.mode` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_MODE`), with the hint that client modes (such as `saas` and `self-managed`) are now available there.

b.text: There is no `camunda.client.connection-mode` property in the reference; the unified term is just `mode`.

a.text: The entire `zeebe.client.*` namespace is deprecated, so `zeebe.client.mode` is not the modern home for this setting.

d.text: `camunda.client.zeebe.*` is itself deprecated; the client mode selector belongs to the top-level `camunda.client.*` namespace.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 introduces unified client modes under `camunda.client.mode` (for example `saas` or `self-managed`), which implies the appropriate transport, auth, and address defaults. The legacy `zeebe.client.connection-mode` enum has been superseded by this mode selector.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference

question: On the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot starter, what is the replacement property for the deprecated `zeebe.client.broker.keep-alive`?

options.text: `camunda.client.broker.keep-alive`
`camunda.client.grpc.keep-alive`
`camunda.client.keep-alive`
`zeebe.client.keep-alive`

c.text: The migration table maps the deprecated `zeebe.client.broker.keep-alive` to the new `camunda.client.keep-alive` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_KEEPALIVE`).

a.text: The new namespace drops the `broker` segment entirely; keep-alive is no longer scoped under a `broker.` sub-key.

d.text: The replacement lives under the `camunda.client.*` namespace, not under `zeebe.client.*`; the whole `zeebe.client.*` tree is deprecated.

b.text: There is no `camunda.client.grpc.keep-alive` property; keep-alive sits directly under `camunda.client.*` regardless of transport.

explanation: In the 8.8 Spring Boot starter the `zeebe.client.broker.*` properties are deprecated. The `keep-alive` setting moves directly to `camunda.client.keep-alive` per the migration table on the properties-reference page.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot app still sets `zeebe.client.cloud.port`. What does the properties-reference page say about migrating this property?

options.text: Rename it to `camunda.client.cloud.port`; the value carries over unchanged.
Set `camunda.client.zeebe.port` instead; the cloud prefix is dropped but `port` stays.
There is no direct replacement — the Zeebe client URL is now configured as an HTTP/HTTPS URL, so the port is part of the URL.
Rename it to `camunda.client.grpc-port`; gRPC has its own dedicated port property.

c.text: The migration table leaves the replacement cell empty for `zeebe.client.cloud.port` and explicitly hints: 'The Zeebe client URL is now configured as HTTP/HTTPS URL'. The port travels inside the URL.

a.text: `camunda.client.cloud.port` is not on the page; the cloud sub-namespace keeps `cluster-id` and `region` but not `port`.

d.text: There is no `camunda.client.grpc-port` property; the gRPC endpoint is given as a full URL via `camunda.client.grpc-address`.

b.text: `camunda.client.zeebe.port` does not exist in the 8.8 starter; ports are configured through the URL of `grpc-address` or `rest-address`.

explanation: On the deprecated `zeebe.client.cloud.*` table, `zeebe.client.cloud.port` has no replacement property. Instead, the Zeebe client endpoint is given as a full HTTP/HTTPS URL via `camunda.client.grpc-address` or `rest-address`, which already encodes the port.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A Spring Boot 8.8 worker still has `zeebe.client.job.timeout=PT5M` in `application.yaml`. Which new property should it migrate to?

options.text: `camunda.client.job.timeout`
`camunda.client.defaults.job-timeout`
`camunda.client.worker.timeout`
`camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout`

d.text: The `zeebe.client.job.*` migration table maps `zeebe.client.job.timeout` to `camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_TIMEOUT`). The `defaults` stanza is where job-worker behaviour now lives.

a.text: The new namespace does not keep a top-level `job` segment; what used to be `zeebe.client.job.*` is now under `camunda.client.worker.defaults.*`.

c.text: There is no `camunda.client.worker.timeout`; the timeout is one level deeper under `worker.defaults.timeout`.

b.text: No such property exists in the 8.8 starter; `defaults` is a child of `worker`, not of `client`.

explanation: Job-worker tuning that used to live under `zeebe.client.job.*` now sits under `camunda.client.worker.defaults.*`. So a per-worker default timeout migrates from `zeebe.client.job.timeout` to `camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout`, exactly as the migration table states.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A worker uses `zeebe.client.message.time-to-live=PT10M` on Camunda 8.8. Which replacement property does the migration table point to?

options.text: `camunda.client.message.time-to-live`
`camunda.client.worker.defaults.message-time-to-live`
`camunda.client.ttl.message`
`camunda.client.message-time-to-live`

d.text: The `zeebe.client.message.*` table maps `zeebe.client.message.time-to-live` to `camunda.client.message-time-to-live` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_MESSAGETIMETOLIVE`) — flattened, not nested under a `message.` sub-key.

a.text: The new property name flattens the `.message.` segment; `camunda.client.message.time-to-live` (with the dot) is not the form the docs list.

b.text: Message TTL is a client-level concern, not a worker default; it does not live under `worker.defaults.*` in the new namespace.

c.text: There is no `camunda.client.ttl.*` group on the page; the docs reuse a single flat key `message-time-to-live`.

explanation: The deprecated `zeebe.client.message.time-to-live` is replaced by the flat property `camunda.client.message-time-to-live`. Note the migration drops the `.message.` segment in favour of the hyphenated key. The sibling `max-message-size` is migrated the same way to `camunda.client.max-message-size`.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot client still sets `zeebe.client.security.plaintext=true`. What does the migration table specify as its replacement?

options.text: `camunda.client.plaintext=true` — the security prefix is dropped but the flag is kept.
`camunda.client.security.plaintext` keeps the same boolean semantics.
There is no replacement property — plaintext vs TLS is now determined by the URL protocol (HTTP vs HTTPS) of the configured client address.
`camunda.client.tls.enabled=false` — TLS is opt-out on the new namespace.

c.text: The migration table leaves the replacement column empty for `zeebe.client.security.plaintext` and explicitly hints: 'plaintext is now determined by the URL protocol (HTTP or HTTPS)'. So you encode it in the address scheme, not in a flag.

b.text: There is no `camunda.client.security.plaintext` property in 8.8; the whole `.security.plaintext` flag is gone, not just renamed.

d.text: `camunda.client.tls.enabled` is not the property the page documents as the replacement; the docs say the URL scheme alone decides plaintext.

a.text: There is no `camunda.client.plaintext` property either; the flag is removed in favour of using HTTP/HTTPS in the client URL.

explanation: In the deprecated `zeebe.client.security.*` block, `cert-path` and `override-authority` get straight renames under `camunda.client.*`, but `plaintext` has no replacement entry. The docs say the new client URL scheme — HTTP for plaintext, HTTPS for TLS — is what now controls transport security.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: On the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot starter, what replaces the deprecated `zeebe.client.worker.threads` property?

options.text: `camunda.client.worker.threads`
`camunda.client.worker.defaults.execution-threads`
`camunda.client.worker.defaults.threads`
`camunda.client.execution-threads`

d.text: The migration table maps `zeebe.client.worker.threads` to the client-level `camunda.client.execution-threads` (env `CAMUNDA_CLIENT_EXECUTIONTHREADS`). Thread-pool sizing moves out of the worker namespace.

a.text: `worker.threads` is not preserved on the new namespace; the property is renamed and relocated to the top-level client config.

c.text: Other worker properties (name, type, max-jobs-active) move to `worker.defaults.*`, but threads specifically is promoted to `camunda.client.execution-threads`.

b.text: There is no `worker.defaults.execution-threads`; the docs only list `execution-threads` directly under `camunda.client`.

explanation: Most `zeebe.client.worker.*` properties relocate under `camunda.client.worker.defaults.*` (default-name, default-type, max-jobs-active). The thread count is the exception — it migrates to the client-wide `camunda.client.execution-threads` because the executor pool is shared across all workers.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

question: A process must accept several distinct external triggers that each create a new instance, and you need to differentiate which trigger started which instance. Which start-event type does the routing best-practice recommend?

options.text: A single none start event, with a service task right after deciding the trigger.
Message start event — multiple per process, one per trigger type.
Conditional start event, with one condition per trigger.
Signal start event, because signals can carry the trigger name.

b.text: The start-event comparison table says to use message start events when you have to 'differentiate several start events', and explicitly allows multiple message start events per process definition.

a.text: The docs allow only one none start event per process definition and reserve it for the case where you have a single, clearly standard starting point.

d.text: Signal start events are described as 'rarely used' and intended for starting several process instances at once via broadcast, not for differentiating between alternative triggers of one process.

c.text: The conditional event is not the recommended pattern here, and the docs note conditional events are not yet supported for execution in Camunda 8 — you must use a message event instead.

explanation: The routing-events best-practice recommends message start events whenever you need to differentiate between several alternative triggers. The comparison table emphasises that you can have multiple message start events in a single process definition, but only one none start event.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices

question: Per the routing-events best-practice, which intermediate event type is currently unsupported for execution in Camunda 8?

options.text: Intermediate signal catch event
Intermediate timer event
Intermediate conditional event
Intermediate message catch event

c.text: The intermediate-events comparison table marks the conditional event as 'Not yet supported in Camunda 8', recommending that you determine the condition externally yourself and use a message event instead.

d.text: Intermediate message catch events are fully supported and are the recommended way to route an incoming message to a specific, unique process instance via a correlation key.

b.text: Intermediate timer events are supported and recommended whenever a process instance must wait for a point in time or a duration.

a.text: Intermediate signal catch events are supported and are recommended when an incoming signal needs to be routed to all process instances waiting for it.

explanation: Message, timer, signal and receive-task intermediate elements are all supported. The conditional event is the outlier — the docs say it is not yet supported in Camunda 8, so the recommended workaround is to determine the condition externally yourself and drive the process forward with a message event.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices

question: A running process instance must react automatically when one of its own process variables changes value, with no external system pushing a message. What does the routing best-practice say about doing this in Camunda 8?

options.text: Add an event subprocess with an interrupting signal start event named after the variable.
Use an intermediate conditional event — Camunda 8 evaluates the condition on every variable write.
Camunda 8 does not yet support the BPMN conditional event, so a different mechanism (e.g., evaluate the condition externally and publish a message) is required.
Use a non-interrupting boundary timer with cycle `R/PT0S` to re-evaluate the variable on every change.

c.text: The 'reacting to process-internal events' section carries a caution: Camunda 8 does not yet support the conditional event. The recommended workaround is to evaluate the condition externally and route the result back via a message.

d.text: Timer cycles fire on time, not on variable writes; a timer cannot act as a variable-change listener.

a.text: Signal events are broadcast triggers from outside the instance; they are not how an instance subscribes to its own variable changes.

b.text: Conditional events are exactly the construct that Camunda 8 does not yet support; the docs warn against assuming the engine will re-evaluate on every variable write.

explanation: The loan-application example in the best-practice page describes a conditional boundary event watching a variable changed by a user task. The caution box right next to it states that Camunda 8 does not yet support the conditional event, so the supported workaround is to externalise the condition check and feed the result back to the process via a message.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices

question: A client starts a new process instance through `PublishMessage` against a message start event. According to the routing best-practice, what is the implication for version selection?

options.text: All deployed versions of the process that declare the message are started in parallel.
You cannot influence the version of the process definition that gets instantiated.
The oldest deployed version that declares that message is used by default.
The publisher selects the version via a `version` field on the published message.

b.text: The docs state plainly: 'you cannot influence the version of the process definition used when starting a process instance by message'. The trade-off for not needing the BPMN process ID is losing the ability to pin the version.

d.text: `PublishMessage` does not take a version field; version pinning is only available on `CreateProcessInstance`, where you call `.bpmnProcessId(...).version(n)`.

c.text: The engine does not fall back to the oldest version — the docs simply say the publisher has no influence over which version is used.

a.text: A published message starts at most one new instance per matching subscription; the engine does not fan out one publication to every deployed version.

explanation: Starting by message via `PublishMessage` frees the client from knowing the BPMN process ID, but the trade-off, as the docs spell out, is that the client cannot influence which version of the process definition is used. If version selection matters, you must instead call `CreateProcessInstance` with `.bpmnProcessId(...).version(n)`.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices

question: Another team will push invoice events to your Camunda 8.8 process over AMQP. According to the routing best-practice, what is the recommended way to land these messages on the workflow engine?

options.text: Configure the Zeebe broker with an AMQP listener so it consumes the queue directly.
Run an AMQP consumer (or another channel: Kafka, REST, SOAP, an Apache Camel route, etc.) that receives the message and calls the Camunda API to start or correlate.
Point the AMQP traffic at the Zeebe gateway port; the gateway speaks AMQP natively.
Replace the AMQP transport with HTTP — the workflow engine only accepts REST events.

b.text: The 'Technology examples' section says you need a mechanism that receives the message and routes it to the engine — citing direct API calls, AMQP or Kafka consumers, SOAP endpoints using the Camunda API, or even an Apache Camel hot-folder poller.

a.text: The Zeebe broker has no built-in AMQP listener; the docs say the workflow engine is by design not concerned with the technical part of receiving external messages.

c.text: The gateway does not speak AMQP; it offers gRPC and REST APIs that your consumer must call after picking up the AMQP message.

d.text: AMQP, JMS, Kafka and other transports are all explicitly named as valid sources — there is no requirement to replace them with HTTP.

explanation: The workflow engine is intentionally agnostic about external transports. Your job is to plug in a consumer (REST handler, AMQP or Kafka subscriber, SOAP endpoint, Apache Camel route, etc.) that receives the external event and then calls the Camunda API to create a process instance or correlate a message.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices

question: Following the Camunda BPMN Framework convention, an `invoice` process has exactly one start event and it is modelled as a message start event so the diagram can show the collaboration with the surrounding human flow. How can clients start an instance?

options.text: The engine starts a new instance on every deployment because there is only one start event.
Either via `CreateProcessInstance` against the BPMN process ID or by publishing the named message — both APIs work.
Only by publishing the named message; `CreateProcessInstance` is rejected because the process has no none start event.
You must add an explicit none start event in parallel — the engine cannot start a process whose only start event is a message event.

b.text: The docs state that if there is exactly one message start event for the whole process definition, it can also be treated as if it were a none start event when starting an instance — so both `CreateProcessInstance` by BPMN ID and `PublishMessage` work.

c.text: `CreateProcessInstance` against the BPMN process ID is explicitly allowed in this single-message-start case; it is not rejected.

d.text: The engine has no problem with a sole message start event; no parallel none start event is required for clients to start the process.

a.text: Deployment never auto-starts process instances in Zeebe; start events are triggered by API calls, messages, timers or signals, not by deployment events.

explanation: The Camunda BPMN Framework convention is to use message start events even when a process has only one start, so the diagram can communicate the collaboration with the surrounding human flow. To avoid burdening clients, the engine treats a sole message start event as equivalent to a none start event for instantiation — so either API works.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices

question: Inside a `@JobWorker` you must deliver a payment notification to another, already running process instance. Which Zeebe client command does the docs sample use?

options.text: `newPublishMessageCommand()` with a `messageName` and a `correlationKey` (e.g., `orderId`).
`newSignalBroadcastCommand()` with the recipient's instance key used as the signal name.
`newCreateInstanceCommand()` with the recipient's instance key passed as the `bpmnProcessId`.
`newThrowErrorCommand()` targeting the recipient's instance.

a.text: The 'Sending messages to other processes' code sample shows a `@JobWorker(type="notifyOrder")` calling `zeebeClient.newPublishMessageCommand().messageName("MsgPaymentReceived").correlationKey(orderId).variables(...).send()`.

c.text: `newCreateInstanceCommand` starts a brand-new process instance; the docs show it for the 'route to a new instance' case, not for correlating to a running one.

b.text: Signal broadcasts target all instances subscribed to a signal name, not one specific running instance addressed by a correlation key.

d.text: `newThrowErrorCommand` raises a BPMN error against the current job; it is not a cross-process notification mechanism.

explanation: When one process must talk to another already-running process instance, the documented pattern is to publish a named message and let Zeebe correlate it via a correlation key (e.g., an `orderId`). The sample worker uses `newPublishMessageCommand().messageName(...).correlationKey(...).variables(...).send()`; for starting a brand-new instance, the sibling sample uses `newCreateInstanceCommand().bpmnProcessId(...).latestVersion()`.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices

question: An accountant reconciles incoming bank payments against many waiting `order` process instances. The active trigger comes from the bank account, not from Tasklist. What does the routing best-practice recommend for modelling and integration?

options.text: Use a non-interrupting boundary signal event so one 'Paid' broadcast updates all open orders at once.
Model a user task per order so the accountant claims and completes it in Tasklist.
Model an intermediate message catch event in each order process; build a custom search screen so the accountant can correlate a 'Paid' click to the right instance(s) via the Camunda API.
Model a multi-instance receive task at process start so a single payment fans out to every order.

c.text: The 'Handling messages sent by a user' section says to model a message wait point (not a user task) and to build a custom search screen; correlating the user's action to the right instance is done by custom code calling the Camunda API.

b.text: The docs argue against a user task here — the accountant is not waiting on a Tasklist entry; the active trigger is the external payment event in the bank account.

d.text: A multi-instance receive task would block one parent instance until all sub-instances complete, which does not match the fan-in pattern where each order waits independently for its own payment.

a.text: Signals are broadcast and uncorrelated, so a single 'Paid' signal would notify every waiting order, not just the one that was actually paid.

explanation: When the human is not the active trigger (the external bank payment is), the docs recommend modelling the wait point as a message catch event rather than a user task, because the accountant does not want to monitor a queue in Tasklist. A custom screen lets the accountant find waiting orders, and the 'Paid' action becomes a correlated message published through the Camunda API to the right process instance.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices

question: A service worker publishes an order over AMQP and blocks until a response message arrives on the corresponding reply queue before completing the job. Per the service-integration-patterns docs, which communication pattern is this?

options.text: Asynchronous request/response — asynchronous technical communication, synchronous business behaviour.
Asynchronous messages or events — because AMQP itself is a messaging system.
Fire-and-forget — there is no HTTP status code, so no acknowledgement is implied.
Synchronous request/response — because the worker blocks on the response.

a.text: The docs define this pattern exactly: two independent asynchronous messages (the request and the reply), but the sender blocks until the response is received, 'hence logically making it a request/response'. AMQP and JMS are the named technologies.

d.text: Synchronous request/response is reserved for synchronous transports such as HTTP; the table assigns 'Synchronous' to the technical column, which AMQP is not.

b.text: 'Asynchronous messages or events' is the pattern where the business behaviour itself is asynchronous (no immediate response expected); blocking for a reply keeps the business behaviour synchronous.

c.text: Fire-and-forget is not one of the three named patterns; the docs explicitly list synchronous request/response, asynchronous request/response, and asynchronous messages or events.

explanation: The service-integration-patterns page distinguishes three options by combining technical and business communication style. The asynchronous request/response pattern uses asynchronous transports (AMQP, JMS) but is logically a request/response because the sender blocks on a reply — which matches the worker that waits for the response message before completing.

question: Modelers debate whether a step that publishes a Kafka record should be drawn as a service task or a send task. According to the docs, what is the technical difference between the two?

options.text: Send tasks bypass job workers and emit messages through a built-in broker-side AMQP/Kafka adapter.
Send tasks complete only after the broker acknowledges the message; service tasks complete as soon as the job is activated by a worker.
There is none — send tasks behave exactly like service tasks; the alternative symbol only signals an asynchronous message to stakeholders.
Service tasks block the process until a synchronous response arrives; send tasks are always fire-and-forget at the engine level.

c.text: The docs are explicit: technically, send tasks behave exactly like service tasks. The send symbol is just a more intuitive icon for stakeholders when the step sends an asynchronous message (AMQP, Kafka, etc.) — execution semantics are identical.

a.text: There is no broker-side messaging adapter; both elements use the standard job-worker mechanism. A worker still has to pick up the job and complete it.

b.text: The engine does not wait for any external broker ACK. Both elements complete when the worker completes the job — broker acknowledgement is the worker's concern, not the engine's.

d.text: Whether a task blocks for a response is a worker-implementation choice, not a property of the BPMN element. The docs warn that using send tasks for fire-and-forget when you actually care about the response is typically confusing.

explanation: Per the docs: 'Technically, send tasks behave exactly like service tasks. However, the alternative symbol makes the meaning of sending a message easier to understand for some stakeholders.' Send tasks are recommended specifically for asynchronous messages like AMQP messages or Kafka records — the choice is about communicating intent, not about runtime behavior.

question: Which statement about using a plain service task (instead of a send + receive task combo) for an asynchronous request/response is NOT correct?

options.text: Timeout handling and retry logic are built into the service task implementation of Zeebe.
You must generate a unique correlation ID yourself (typically a UUID) before completing the job.
The Zeebe-internal job instance key serves as a unique correlation identifier that is generated for you.
If the process instance is cancelled and restarted, the new job key cannot correlate the outstanding response anymore.

c.text: True per the docs: with a service task the job instance key is generated for you and is unique for every message interchange — you don't have to think about race conditions or idempotency yourself.

b.text: False — owning the correlation ID applies to the send + receive task pattern, where the docs say 'you need to provide the correlation ID yourself ... most likely end up with generated UUIDs.' With a plain service task, Zeebe generates the job key for you.

a.text: True — the docs explicitly call out that timeout handling and retry logic are built into the service task implementation, along with a clear API for technical or business errors.

d.text: True — the docs warn that cancelling and restarting an instance for operational reasons can lead to a new ID, and outstanding responses cannot be correlated anymore in such cases.

explanation: The service task path leans on Zeebe's auto-generated job instance key for correlation — option b describes the send + receive task pattern instead. The docs explicitly contrast them: service tasks get a generated key 'for you'; send + receive forces you to supply and own the correlation ID.

question: According to Camunda's recommendation, what should be the default BPMN element for both synchronous and asynchronous request/response service calls?

options.text: Send task followed by a receive task
Service task
Event-based gateway after a receive task
Send task

b.text: Per the docs' rule-of-thumb: 'we recommend using the service task as the default option for synchronous and asynchronous request/response calls.' It removes visual clutter and lets the diagram stay focused on business logic.

d.text: Send tasks are recommended for fire-and-forget asynchronous messages, not as the default for request/response. The docs warn that using send tasks when you do care about the response is typically confusing.

a.text: The send + receive combo is recommended when the response will take longer than a few seconds, or when you need a correlation ID under your own control — not as the default.

c.text: The event-based gateway is in the 'try to avoid' category in the response-handling table; it is not a default modeling choice.

explanation: The docs explicitly recommend the service task as the default option for both synchronous and asynchronous request/response calls. Service tasks remove visual clutter and let stakeholders focus on the business problem, ignoring whether the underlying protocol is synchronous or asynchronous.

question: A process integrates with a callback-style payment gateway whose response can arrive minutes — sometimes hours — later. Which BPMN element best fits this request/response, and why?

options.text: A send task followed by a receive task — you control the correlation ID and don't rely on an internal job instance key over long waits.
A service task — Zeebe's generated job key is the simplest correlation, and waiting hours on an open job is fine.
A send task only — fire-and-forget removes the need to wait for the callback at all.
A service task — message buffering will queue the late response until the process catches up.

a.text: Correct per the docs' summary table: use send + receive 'when the response will take time (> some seconds), or you need a correlation ID you can control.' Over long waits, operational restarts can change a job key and break correlation, so an owned correlation ID is safer.

b.text: Service tasks are fine for millisecond-range request/response, but the docs warn that cancelling and restarting an instance creates a new job key, breaking outstanding response correlation — a real risk over hours.

d.text: Message buffering is a feature of the messages concept used by send + receive tasks, not of service tasks. Service tasks do not buffer messages.

c.text: A send-task-only pattern is fire-and-forget — the process won't observe the callback at all, which fails the actual business requirement.

explanation: For long-running responses the docs recommend the send + receive task combo because (a) you own the correlation ID and don't depend on the engine-internal job instance key, and (b) message buffering and explicit correlation are part of the messages concept built into Zeebe. Service tasks are best when responses return within milliseconds.

question: According to the docs, what is the one technical capability that send/receive tasks have but the equivalent send/receive events do not, despite their otherwise identical execution semantics?

options.text: Only tasks can be correlated by a message name.
Only tasks can carry input and output payload mappings.
Only tasks can have boundary events attached.
Only tasks can be marked as interrupting.

c.text: Per the docs: the execution semantics of send/receive events is identical with send/receive tasks, but only tasks can have boundary events — which is what lets you easily model cancelling the wait for a message.

a.text: Message correlation works the same for tasks and events; it isn't a task-only feature.

b.text: Both tasks and events support payload mappings; this isn't the differentiator the docs call out.

d.text: 'Interrupting' is a property of boundary events themselves, not of tasks vs events. The docs' point is that tasks can host those boundary events at all.

explanation: Send and receive events have identical execution semantics to the equivalent tasks, but only tasks can host boundary events. That matters whenever you want to cancel waiting for a message, attach a timeout, or otherwise react around the activity.

question: When choosing among the patterns for handling different response message types, which pattern do the docs explicitly say you should NOT use as a default?

options.text: Event-based gateway selecting between several message types.
Receive task followed by a payload-driven XOR gateway.
Event subprocesses that activate when a deviation message arrives.
Receive task with one or two boundary message events.

d.text: The docs recommend this pattern when it is important to observe message types visually — but suggest limiting it to two boundary message events. It is a recommended option, not one to avoid.

b.text: The docs recommend the payload + XOR gateway pattern when there are more response types or the response can be treated as a result; it has the best understandability rating.

a.text: Per the docs' recommendation column, the event-based gateway is the only pattern marked 'Try to avoid' — it is hard to understand for non-BPMN professionals and shares the downside that the happy path is not easily spotted.

c.text: Event subprocesses are recommended when you need bigger scopes where you can react to events, especially anytime-cancellation scenarios; they are not in the avoid bucket.

explanation: Among the four response-handling patterns the docs catalogue (boundary message events, payload + XOR, event-based gateway, event subprocess), only the event-based gateway is labelled 'Try to avoid'. The docs cite poor understandability for non-BPMN readers and the inability to spot the happy path.

question: A process must react to a cancellation message that can arrive at any point during a long sequence of activities — not at one specific wait state. Which response-handling pattern best fits this requirement?

options.text: An event-based gateway placed after each receive task.
An event subprocess scoped over the relevant chunk of the process.
A boundary message event attached to each activity in the chunk.
A payload + XOR gateway after the next receive task.

b.text: Per the docs: 'the event subprocess has a superpower worth mentioning: you can now wait for cancellation messages in whole chunks of your process — it could arrive anytime.' That is exactly the requirement here.

c.text: Boundary events are attached to a single activity. Putting one on every activity in a long chunk to cover anytime cancellation is exactly the visual clutter the event subprocess avoids.

a.text: An event-based gateway only fires at one specific waiting point in the flow, not across a whole chunk — and the docs put this pattern in the 'try to avoid' bucket anyway.

d.text: A payload + XOR gateway runs after a single receive task; it cannot react to a message arriving while the process is busy elsewhere.

explanation: Event subprocesses are the docs' answer to 'react to a message that can arrive anywhere in this scope'. They activate whenever the event is received while the process is still active somewhere in the enclosing scope — the canonical fit for anytime-cancellation patterns.

question: According to the docs, where is the transformation between a technical message type on the wire (e.g. an AMQP message) and the BPMN message type used in the process typically implemented?

options.text: In glue code that receives the technical message and calls the workflow engine API.
In an event-based gateway that branches on the wire format.
In a FEEL expression on the receive task's correlation key.
Inside the BPMN message definition via a `messageRef` mapping.

a.text: The docs are explicit: when correlating technical messages (e.g. from AMQP) you typically write glue code that receives the message and calls the workflow engine API, and that glue code can do various transformations between technical and BPMN message types.

d.text: BPMN's `messageRef` references a message definition; it doesn't transform wire-level message types into BPMN message types.

b.text: Event-based gateways operate inside the process model on already-correlated BPMN messages — they don't see the wire format.

c.text: A FEEL correlation key extracts the correlation value from an already-correlated message; it doesn't bridge between wire format and BPMN message type.

explanation: Wire-level message types and BPMN message types do not need to match. The docs recommend doing any mapping — collapsing several queues to one BPMN type, or splitting one queue into different BPMN types based on a header — in the glue code that bridges your messaging client and the Camunda client API.

question: A document storage service requires regular polling because firewall rules block real callbacks. According to the docs, what is the recommended way to keep the main business process diagram free of this technical complexity?

options.text: Encode the polling loop as a job-worker retry policy with a short backoff between failed attempts.
Hide the polling inside a FEEL function on an input mapping of the document storage service task.
Extract the polling into a separate adapter process model and invoke it from the main process via a call activity.
Replace the integration with a non-interrupting timer boundary event that fires on every polling interval.

c.text: The docs describe exactly this: a separate 'document storage adapter' process implements the polling loop and persistent waiting, and the main business process simply invokes it via a call activity — so no technicalities clutter that diagram.

b.text: FEEL on input mappings is for data shaping, not for orchestrating a polling loop with persistent waits.

d.text: A non-interrupting timer fires next to the activity but doesn't model the polling-and-waiting protocol. It also pushes the technical concern back into the main diagram.

a.text: Job-worker retries are for failed jobs, not for polling a remote system. The docs' recommendation is structural (a separate process behind a call activity), not a retry-policy tweak.

explanation: When a service integration's technicalities (here: polling because callbacks are blocked) would clutter the main process, the docs recommend creating a separate process for those technicalities and pulling it in via a call activity. The main business process stays focused on business logic; the adapter process owns the polling and waiting.

question: According to the docs, what is the recommended overall approach to testing executable BPMN processes?

options.text: Run only manual exploratory tests directly against the production environment to keep tests in sync with real-world data.
Test BPMN processes purely with end-to-end UI tests, because the workflow engine cannot be isolated in a unit test.
Skip automated tests for the process model itself — process behavior can only be meaningfully validated by domain experts in production.
Write fast automated unit tests against a localized, isolated workflow engine, and verify with integration tests in an environment that mirrors production before release.

d.text: The docs open with this guidance directly: test executable BPMN processes as you would any software, write fast automated unit tests with a localized and isolated workflow engine, and verify with integration tests in an environment that closely mirrors production before releasing.

c.text: The docs explicitly endorse automated tests; expert-only validation in production is not the recommended baseline.

a.text: Manual exploratory testing in production is mentioned as a complement to integration testing, not as a substitute for fast automated tests.

b.text: Camunda 8.8 Process Test makes it straightforward to embed an isolated engine in a unit test, so end-to-end UI tests are not the only option.

explanation: The docs frame process testing as ordinary software testing: prefer fast automated tests against an isolated workflow engine for everyday feedback, and use integration tests in a production-like environment — including human-driven exploratory tests — before a release.

question: A developer wants a fast, frequently-runnable test that exercises a BPMN process model along with its glue code and the data flowing through the process, without depending on external resources. Which test scope does that fit?

options.text: Process tests
Integration tests
Unit tests of business code only
Human-driven exploratory tests

a.text: The docs define process tests as testing the expected behavior of the process model, including glue code and the data flowing through it. They should run frequently and behave like unit tests — quick turnaround, no external resources.

c.text: Unit tests in the docs' typology cover programming/glue code only; they do not include the process model and data flow that this developer wants to exercise.

b.text: Integration tests run in a close-to-production environment before releases. They are not the fast, frequently-runnable scope described here.

d.text: Human-driven exploratory tests are part of the integration scope and are explicitly not the fast automated tests this developer needs.

explanation: The docs distinguish unit, process, and integration tests. Process tests are the right scope for the model + glue code + data flow combination, with no external dependencies, fast enough to run on every change.

question: Which annotation starts a process engine for a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot process test, and which JUnit version must be used?

options.text: `@ProcessEngineTest` with JUnit Jupiter.
`@ZeebeProcessTest` with JUnit 4.
`@CamundaSpringProcessTest` with JUnit 5.
`@SpringBootTest` alone, JUnit 4 or 5 both supported.

c.text: The docs prescribe `@CamundaSpringProcessTest` to start a process engine and explicitly require JUnit 5 in every test class: the `@Test` annotation must be `org.junit.jupiter.api.Test`.

b.text: `@ZeebeProcessTest` predates Camunda 8.8's Camunda Process Test; the docs' setup uses the Camunda-branded annotation, not the Zeebe-era one, and JUnit 4 is explicitly out.

d.text: `@SpringBootTest` is used alongside `@CamundaSpringProcessTest`, but on its own it doesn't start a process engine, and the docs disallow JUnit 4.

a.text: `@ProcessEngineTest` is from the Camunda 7 lineage; Camunda 8.8 introduced Camunda Process Test with `@CamundaSpringProcessTest`.

explanation: Camunda Process Test was introduced with Camunda 8.8. The docs' technical setup uses the Camunda Spring Boot Starter and the `@CamundaSpringProcessTest` annotation to start a process engine, and JUnit 5 is mandatory — the `@Test` annotation must come from `org.junit.jupiter.api`.

question: In a Camunda Process Test for an invoice-approval flow, the `ArchiveInvoiceWorker` runs as part of the test but the underlying `ArchiveService` is mocked with `@MockitoBean`. Why this split?

options.text: Mocking the worker would skip the FEEL gateway expressions, which only evaluate when the worker is mocked out.
`@MockitoBean` cannot be applied to worker classes, only to non-`@Component` service interfaces.
Job workers cannot run inside `@CamundaSpringProcessTest` and must always be replaced by a mock job worker.

Worker code is part of the process test scope, but workflow-engine-independent business services should be mocked so the test exercises the model, glue code, and data flow without calling external systems.

d.text: Per the docs: the process test should include the executable BPMN plus the glue code that logically belongs to it (workers, FEEL, client glue). Workflow-engine-independent business code such as `ArchiveService` should not run in the test, so it is mocked while the worker continues to handle input mapping and BPMN error translation.

c.text: The docs show the worker is explicitly executed as part of the test — it does input data mapping and translates a `WiredLegacyException` into a `BpmnError`. Mocking the worker entirely is a separate option only when no clean service interface exists (e.g. connectors).

a.text: FEEL gateway expressions run inside the engine regardless of whether workers are mocked; they are not gated on the worker behavior.

b.text: `@MockitoBean` works on any Spring bean, including `@Component`-annotated workers. The split here is a deliberate test-scope decision, not a Spring/Mockito limitation.

explanation: The docs draw the line at workflow-engine-independent business code. Workers, FEEL, and client glue belong inside the process test; remote service calls (here `ArchiveService`) should be mocked. That is why the worker runs and verifies its data-mapping and error-translation logic, while the archive backend is faked out with Mockito.

question: The docs warn against 'overspecifying' a process test. Which rule of thumb do they recommend you always follow when writing the assertions?

options.text: Always assert every visited element id in order so any future model change is caught immediately.
Always assert that every variable in scope still holds its initial value; deviations indicate model drift.
Always assert that the expected external effects (e.g. business service calls) really happened; pick a small set of internal-state assertions only where they truly matter.
Always assert that the process completes within a fixed wall-clock duration so performance regressions surface in tests.

c.text: The docs state this rule of thumb directly: always assert that the expected external effects of your process really took place (e.g. that business services were called as expected), and carefully choose which aspects of internal process state are important enough to warn about later.

a.text: Asserting every visited element is the textbook overspecification the docs warn against — the process definition will evolve, and such tests break for every model change rather than for real regressions.

b.text: Pinning every variable value is even more brittle and is not the docs' guidance.

d.text: Wall-clock duration is a performance concern; the docs' rule of thumb is about external effects vs internal state, not timing.

explanation: The docs advise treating external effects as the assertion bedrock — those reflect what the process is contractually doing for the outside world — and being selective about internal-state assertions so the test breaks only when something meaningful changes, not for every BPMN tweak.

question: A team is structuring the test suite for an invoice-approval process. According to the docs' divide-and-conquer guidance, how should the happy path and the exceptional/fork paths be organized into test methods?

options.text: Test only the happy path with automated tests; leave fork and error paths to integration testing later.
Combine the happy path and every exceptional path into one giant test method for full end-to-end coverage.
Cover the happy path in one consistent (big) test method, then test each fork/error path as its own separate test method.
Split the happy path across many tiny test methods so that each BPMN element has its own assertion.

c.text: The docs say to fully test the happy path in one (big) test method to keep the data flow consistent and readable, then test forks/detours and errors/exceptional paths as chunks in separate test methods.

b.text: A single giant test method conflates many scenarios, hides failures, and is the opposite of the docs' divide-and-conquer recommendation.

a.text: The docs explicitly catalogue exceptional paths (rejection, timeout auto-approval, archive system error) as automated test methods, not deferred work.

d.text: The happy path is meant to live in one consistent method to clarify the default data flow — fragmenting it loses the readability that's the whole point.

explanation: The docs' divide-and-conquer guidance is: one big test for the happy path (so the default scenario is easy to read and onboard with), then a separate test method per fork or error path (rejection, timeout auto-approval, archive system error). That gives meaningful units without overspecifying.

question: A `testApprovalTimeout` test wants to fast-forward past the boundary timer on `UserTask_ApproveInvoice` so the auto-approval branch fires. Which call on `processTestContext` does the docs use?

options.text: `Thread.sleep(Duration.ofDays(5).toMillis())`
`processTestContext.completeUserTask(byElementId("UserTask_ApproveInvoice"))`
`client.newPublishMessageCommand().messageName("timeout").send().join()`
`processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5))`

d.text: The docs' detour test for the timeout chunk advances the engine clock with `processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5))`, which makes the boundary timer fire deterministically while the user task is still active. The assertions then check `hasTerminatedElements(byId("UserTask_ApproveInvoice"))` and the auto-approval path.

a.text: `Thread.sleep` waits real wall-clock time and has no effect on the engine's internal scheduler, so the timer would never fire in the test.

c.text: A boundary timer is not driven by message correlation; publishing a message would not trigger it. The docs do not use this technique for timer detour tests.

b.text: Completing the user task drives the happy path, not the timeout detour. The point of `testApprovalTimeout` is to leave the task active and force the timer to win.

explanation: The docs' `testApprovalTimeout` example uses `processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5))` to advance the engine clock so the boundary timer on `UserTask_ApproveInvoice` fires and the auto-approval branch completes. Engine time is a separate concept from JVM wall time, so `Thread.sleep` would not help.

question: A `CamundaSpringProcessTest` unit test exercises the rejection chunk of an invoice process. The `Send invoice rejection` service task has `taskDefinition type="io.camunda:http-json:1"`. How does the docs stop the test from making a real HTTP call?

codeBlocks.stem:
// We skip HTTP for the simple unit test - mock the http connector
processTestContext.mockJobWorker("io.camunda:http-json:1").thenComplete();

options.text: Set `retries="0"` on the service task so the connector never activates a job.
Annotate the test with `@EnableWireMock` and stub `/reject` with a 200 response.
Call `processTestContext.mockJobWorker("io.camunda:http-json:1").thenComplete()` to stub the connector's job worker.
Disable the broker's REST endpoint in `application.yml` before the test boots.

c.text: The docs' unit-style detour test calls `processTestContext.mockJobWorker("io.camunda:http-json:1").thenComplete()` so the REST connector job is completed without invoking the real endpoint. This keeps the chunk test simple and offline.

b.text: `@EnableWireMock` is the integration-test technique shown later in the page, used when you want to exercise input/output mapping by mocking the endpoint instead of the worker. It is heavier than what the unit chunk test needs.

a.text: `retries="0"` only changes how many times the engine activates the job on failure; the worker would still try to hit the URL on the first activation.

d.text: Disabling broker REST endpoints does not affect outbound REST calls made by the connector job worker; it would only break inbound API traffic to the broker.

explanation: For unit-style chunk tests, the docs mock the connector job worker directly with `processTestContext.mockJobWorker("io.camunda:http-json:1").thenComplete()`. `@EnableWireMock` is reserved for the separate integration-test section where the goal is to exercise the connector's input/output mapping by mocking the REST endpoint rather than the worker.

question: For an integration test of a process whose service task uses a Camunda REST outbound connector, what does the docs recommend mocking?

options.text: Neither — integration tests must always call the real production REST endpoint.
The connector's job worker, so the connector code path never runs during the integration test.
The REST endpoint itself (e.g. with WireMock), so the connector's input and output mapping is actually exercised.
Both the endpoint and the worker together, so duplicated assertions confirm parity.

c.text: The docs say that when using the Connector framework there is logic worth testing in the connector configuration, especially the input and output data mapping, so you typically want to mock the endpoint rather than the job worker. WireMock is the example given.

b.text: Mocking the job worker is the unit-test technique for the chunk tests; in integration tests it bypasses the very mapping logic you are trying to exercise.

d.text: The docs pick one strategy per test scope, not both at once. Mocking the worker and the endpoint together is not recommended and not described.

a.text: Hitting a production endpoint from automated integration tests is not what the docs prescribe; the WireMock-based stub is the recommended pattern.

explanation: Camunda's integration-test guidance is to mock the REST endpoint (using WireMock, for example) rather than the connector's job worker, so the connector's input and output data mapping is actually exercised by the test. Mocking the worker is the lighter unit-test technique used for chunk tests.

question: According to the Camunda 8 "Working with APIs & tools" overview, which set of official clients and SDKs is offered to simplify API usage?

options.text: TypeScript, C#, and Python.
Java, Python, and Go.
Java and a REST-only client — no Node.js or Spring SDK is shipped.
Java, Spring, and Node.js.

d.text: The overview page explicitly states that the official Camunda clients and SDKs are Java, Spring, and Node.js, and that these simplify API usage and speed up development against Camunda 8 APIs.

b.text: Python and Go are not listed on the overview page as official Camunda 8 clients/SDKs. The named set is Java, Spring, and Node.js.

a.text: The overview does not name TypeScript or C# in its list of official clients; Node.js (not TypeScript) is the JavaScript-runtime client called out, and there is no C# entry on this page.

c.text: The overview names Spring and Node.js alongside Java as official clients, so saying only Java + REST is offered contradicts the page.

explanation: The "Working with APIs & tools" overview lists Java, Spring, and Node.js as the official Camunda clients and SDKs that simplify API usage and speed up development on top of the Camunda 8 APIs.

question: An order-fulfillment process needs three synchronous REST calls (payment, inventory, shipping) implemented as glue code. What is the docs' default recommendation for organizing this glue code?

options.text: Embed the REST calls in the BPMN XML as FEEL expressions on each service task so no workers are needed.
Build three separate applications — one worker per task type — even when there is no scaling or language reason to split.
Put all the glue code into one process-solution application that also deploys the BPMN model.
Run the workers inside the Zeebe broker as embedded plugins to avoid the network hop.

c.text: As a rule of thumb, the docs recommend implementing all glue code in one application — the so-called process solution — which often also deploys the BPMN model at startup. This makes the result self-contained and easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy.

b.text: The docs list splitting into multiple applications only as an exception (independent scaling/throttling or different programming languages). It is not the default.

a.text: BPMN service tasks call out to job workers; FEEL is for data, not for issuing REST calls. The docs do not propose this approach.

d.text: Zeebe runs no embedded user plugins for glue code; workers are external applications that activate jobs via the gateway API.

explanation: The docs' default recommendation is a single process solution: one application containing all the glue code (and often the BPMN itself), which is self-contained and easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy. Splitting into multiple applications is reserved for cases that need independent scaling/throttling or different programming languages.

question: A worker handles many jobs per second on a process with parallel gateways and multi-instance activities. Which configuration on the job-activation call does the docs recommend to minimize data transfer?

options.text: Set `FetchVariables` to `[]` and have the worker re-read variables later via the Tasklist API for each job.
Omit `FetchVariables` so the broker streams the full process state — this is always the fastest option.
Increase the broker's max message size so every variable fits in one payload no matter how large.
Specify a `FetchVariables` list so only the variables the handler actually needs are fetched with each job.

d.text: The docs explicitly recommend using the `FetchVariables` parameter and only fetching the variables the job handler needs. This keeps the activation payload small, reduces network bandwidth, and minimises the risk of overwriting parallel-branch variables on completion.

b.text: By default (when `FetchVariables` is omitted) the job payload contains all variables visible in the scope — possibly tens of variables of arbitrary size. The docs warn against this and recommend the opposite.

a.text: Tasklist is for human tasks, not for worker job payloads, and the docs do not propose re-reading variables out-of-band. `FetchVariables` already lets the worker request only what it needs.

c.text: Raising the message ceiling does the opposite of data minimisation; the docs' advice is to send less data, not to make room for more.

explanation: The docs say to use the `FetchVariables` parameter and only request the variables your handler needs, to minimise broker work and network bandwidth. This also reduces the surface area for race conditions on parallel paths, since completing a job writes its fetched/returned variables back to the process scope.

question: A worker invokes 20 REST requests of ~100ms each per job. With one thread, throughput tops out at ~10 jobs/s. Which approach does the docs recommend when developers are comfortable with it?

options.text: Use reactive (non-blocking) programming so the runtime overlaps I/O across many jobs without you sizing thread pools.
Process jobs strictly sequentially — the docs argue this is the most scalable approach for I/O-bound workers.
Lower the broker's polling interval to zero so the client receives jobs faster — this on its own removes the per-thread cap.
Configure your own bespoke thread pool with one thread per expected concurrent job and keep the code blocking.

a.text: The docs prefer reactive programming in most situations where parallel processing matters: the runtime/framework manages thread utilisation and you do not block a thread for the duration of each I/O call. This lifts the per-thread throughput ceiling.

b.text: Sequential processing is fine for low-volume use cases, but the docs explicitly position it as not enough when you need to scale past the per-thread cap.

d.text: Thread pools are described as workable but the docs say they typically do not recommend you configure them yourself and prefer reactive code when you need to scale past linear execution.

c.text: Activation latency does not change the fact that one blocking thread can only do one REST call at a time. Polling tuning alone does not lift the 10-jobs/s ceiling in this example.

explanation: The docs' explicit recommendation is reactive (non-blocking) programming for I/O-bound workers when developers are comfortable with it. Thread pools work but require careful sizing and library knowledge; the reactive model lets the framework/runtime overlap many in-flight I/O calls without blocking threads.

question: Workers on a large Zeebe cluster see noticeable delays — on the order of seconds — before a new job is picked up, even with long polling enabled. Which change does the docs recommend?

options.text: Have each worker poll every partition more aggressively (shorter interval) so new jobs are found faster.
Enable job streaming so jobs are pushed from the broker to workers instead of relying on per-partition long polling.
Increase the long-polling timeout so each poll waits longer for jobs to appear.
Reduce the number of partitions in the cluster so there are fewer endpoints for the worker to poll.

b.text: The docs' performance best-practice recommendation for reducing job activation latency is to enable job streaming. Long polling forces workers to periodically poll every partition; on large clusters this adds delay in the order of seconds, which job streaming eliminates.

c.text: Longer poll timeouts only change how long an idle request waits; they do not solve the per-partition polling overhead the docs call out.

a.text: More aggressive polling is precisely what the docs warn against — it increases broker load when no jobs are available without actually solving the latency on large clusters.

d.text: Reducing partition count is a major topology change with throughput and fault-tolerance trade-offs; the docs do not propose it as the latency fix here.

explanation: On large clusters, long polling forces workers to poll every partition and balance aggression with broker load, which can add delays measured in seconds. The docs' explicit performance best-practice fix is to enable job streaming, so jobs are pushed to workers as soon as they appear.

question: A Spring Boot worker calls a blocking `RestTemplate` REST endpoint and then `.join()`s on the complete command, using the default `ZeebeClient` configuration. What does the docs observe about parallelism?

options.text: Jobs run in parallel — the SDK automatically grows the thread pool to match the number of CPU cores.
Parallelism equals the broker's partition count, since the polling component subscribes one worker thread per partition.
Jobs run in parallel because `autoComplete = false` makes the SDK call complete on a background thread for you.
Jobs are processed sequentially because the default `numJobWorkerExecutionThreads` is `1`; you must raise it to scale blocking code.

d.text: The docs explicitly call out that the default thread pool size in the Java client is one. With blocking code, this limits throughput to one job at a time and you must call `numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5)` (or the Spring Boot Starter equivalent) to scale.

a.text: There is no CPU-core auto-sizing in the Java client; the default is hard-coded to one thread and you must opt in to a larger pool.

c.text: `autoComplete = false` means the SDK does not call complete for you; it does not move work off the calling thread. Parallelism is still bounded by the worker execution thread pool.

b.text: The Java client's worker execution thread pool is independent of partition count; activation may receive jobs from many partitions, but blocking handler invocations still serialize on the configured threads.

explanation: The Java client's default `numJobWorkerExecutionThreads` is `1`. With blocking code (`RestTemplate` + `.join()`), each in-flight job occupies that single thread for the full I/O round-trip, so jobs execute sequentially. To scale blocking handlers you must build the client with a larger thread pool (e.g. `.numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5)`).

question: In the Java client, why does the reactive worker example chain `.thenApply(...)` and `.exceptionally(...)` on `client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).send()` instead of calling `.join()`?

options.text: `.join()` skips the broker's retry handling, so a job is never retried after a network blip.
`.join()` blocks the worker thread until the broker acknowledges completion; chaining callbacks keeps the thread free so jobs can proceed without growing the thread pool.
`.join()` only works inside the Spring Boot Starter; the pure Java client always requires callbacks.
`.thenApply()` is required to deserialize variables into POJOs; `.join()` returns only raw JSON.

b.text: The docs note that `.join()` blocks to wait for the response, holding the worker thread for the network round-trip.
