Registering callbacks via `.thenApply(...)` / `.exceptionally(...)` keeps the reactive pipeline non-blocking so the framework can overlap many in-flight completions without you sizing a thread pool.

c.text: `.join()` is the same `CompletionStage` API regardless of how the worker is registered; it is available in both the plain Java client and the Spring Boot Starter.

a.text: `.join()` only changes when the calling thread proceeds; retries are a separate concept driven by the broker's job activation logic, not by which terminal operator you use on the future.

d.text: Variable deserialization is independent of `.join()` vs `.thenApply()`; both eventually expose the same response payload through the SDK's models.

explanation: `.join()` is a blocking terminal operator on the SDK's future — it pins the worker thread until the broker acknowledges completion. Chaining `.thenApply(...)` and `.exceptionally(...)` lets the reactive code register callbacks instead, so the thread is released immediately and the framework can keep many job completions in flight without growing a thread pool.

question: A `@JobWorker(type = "rest")` makes a blocking `RestTemplate` call and the team needs more throughput without rewriting in reactive style. Which single change does the docs suggest?

options.text: Bump `retries` on the service task definition above the default 3 so blocking jobs are reprocessed faster.
Build the client with `.numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5)` (or the equivalent Spring Boot Starter configuration) so the polling component dispatches jobs across a thread pool.
Lower the broker's job activation timeout so blocking jobs are reassigned to other workers sooner.
Set `autoComplete = true` on the `@JobWorker`, which switches the SDK to a non-blocking completion path.

b.text: The docs show exactly this fix for blocking handlers: build the `ZeebeClient` with `.numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5)` (or the equivalent Spring Boot Starter execution-threads configuration). This lifts the default-of-one thread cap so blocking REST calls can run in parallel.

a.text: Retries control failure handling, not concurrency. Raising retries does not make a single worker process more jobs per second.

d.text: `autoComplete` controls whether the SDK auto-sends the complete command; it does not change the threading model. Blocking code still serializes on the same execution threads.

c.text: Shortening the activation timeout causes jobs to be re-activated by other workers and increases broker load; it does not raise throughput on the original worker.

explanation: The docs' direct fix for scaling blocking Java workers without rewriting in reactive style is to enlarge the worker execution thread pool — for example with `ZeebeClient.newClientBuilder().numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5).build()` or the equivalent Spring Boot Starter configuration. Throughput then scales roughly with the number of threads, bounded by the I/O latency.

question: A Node.js worker uses `zbc.createWorker({ taskType, taskHandler })` with `axios.get(...).then(...)` inside the handler. A teammate proposes rewriting it with `const r = await axios.get(...)` to "block on the response". What does the docs say will happen?

options.text: The `await` form will block Node.js's only thread and serialize jobs to one at a time.
The Node.js SDK refuses to register a handler that uses `async/await`; the test will throw at startup.
It only works if you also configure a thread pool, because Node.js requires one for concurrent job activation.
Even the seemingly blocking `await` form is still executed in a non-blocking fashion — JavaScript cannot truly block — so the worker scales the same way.

d.text: The docs make this explicit: reactive programming is so deep in JavaScript that it is impossible to write truly blocking code, and code that looks blocking is still executed non-blockingly. Node.js workers scale well via the event loop.

a.text: `await` does not block the Node.js event loop; it suspends the async function and lets the loop service other callbacks. The docs note JavaScript cannot really block.

b.text: The SDK accepts ordinary `async` handlers; there is no such restriction described in the docs.

c.text: Node.js needs no specific worker thread pool — the Camunda 8 Node.js client itself uses reactive programming internally and relies on the event loop.

explanation: The Node.js section is unequivocal: JavaScript cannot really block, so even a `await axios.get(...)` style handler is still executed by the event loop and the worker scales without any thread-pool tuning. The Camunda 8 Node.js client itself is reactive internally.

question: When configuring a C# worker with `.HandlerThreads(3)`, what does that number actually control according to the docs?

options.text: The number of broker partitions the worker subscribes to, one OS thread per partition.
The number of times each job will be retried before failing, overriding the service-task `retries` attribute.
The exact number of OS threads the SDK pins to the worker for its lifetime.
The degree of parallelism allowed to the Dataflow TPL — implemented as a mix of event loop and threads — not a literal OS-thread count.

d.text: The docs describe `HandlerThreads` as a naming legacy: the C# client uses the Dataflow Task Parallel Library (TPL), so the value really configures the degree of parallelism allowed to TPL. Internally it is a mixture of event loop and threading — an implementation detail of TPL.

c.text: The docs explicitly say it is not a literal OS-thread count; the implementation is a mixture of event loop and threading driven by TPL.

a.text: Handler threads are unrelated to partition subscription; the partition fan-out is an internal broker/client concern, not exposed by `HandlerThreads`.

b.text: Retry counts are configured on the service task definition (or via the fail command), not via the worker builder's `HandlerThreads` setting.

explanation: In the C# client, `HandlerThreads(n)` is a naming legacy. The implementation uses the Dataflow Task Parallel Library (TPL), so the value controls the degree of parallelism allowed to TPL — internally a mixture of event loop and threading — rather than a literal OS-thread count. This is a good foundation for scaling the worker.

question: What is the result of the FEEL expression above when `orders` is an empty list `[]`?

codeBlocks.stem: every order in orders satisfies order.total > 0

options.text: true
false
An error, because the list is empty.
null

a.text: FEEL's `every` quantifier returns `true` for an empty list — the universal quantifier over an empty domain is vacuously true.

b.text: `some` over an empty list returns `false`, but `every` returns `true`.

d.text: FEEL `every` always returns a boolean; it does not produce `null` for empty lists.

c.text: An empty list is a valid input to `every`; FEEL does not raise an error here.

explanation: FEEL's `every` quantifier returns `true` over an empty list (vacuous truth). The corresponding `some` quantifier returns `false` in the same situation.

question: Given the Spring Boot worker declaration above, what is your responsibility inside the method?

codeBlocks.stem:
@JobWorker(type = "X", autoComplete = false)
public void doWork(JobClient client, ActivatedJob job) { ... }

options.text: Explicitly call `client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).send()` (or send a fail / throw-error command).
Return a variables map — the SDK still completes the job for you.
Nothing — the SDK polls the broker to know when the job is finished.
Throw an exception when the work is done to signal completion.

a.text: With `autoComplete = false` the SDK does not call complete for you. You own the job lifecycle and must send a complete, fail, or throw-error command via the `JobClient`. This is useful for async work that outlives the method invocation.

b.text: Returning a map only matters when `autoComplete = true`; with it disabled, the SDK ignores the return value.

d.text: Throwing on success would be interpreted as a failure and decrement retries.

c.text: The broker does not poll workers; workers poll the broker via `ActivateJobs`, and with `autoComplete = false` the worker must explicitly complete.

explanation: Disabling `autoComplete` means the SDK no longer wraps the method with a complete-on-return behaviour. The worker must explicitly call complete, fail, or throw-error on the `JobClient` so the broker advances or retries the job.

question: A BPMN diagram with an embedded Camunda Form is deployed, and Tasklist is opened. How does Tasklist obtain and render that form for each assigned user task?

options.text: Tasklist fetches the form schema from the bpmn-io `form-js` GitHub repository on first render.
Tasklist imports the embedded form schema from the deployment and uses it to render the form on every task assigned to it.
Tasklist requests the form as pre-rendered HTML from Camunda Modeler each time a task is opened.
Tasklist defers form handling to a runtime API call that the user task itself performs after assignment.

b.text: The Camunda Forms reference states that after deploying a diagram with an embedded form, Tasklist imports the form schema and uses it to render the form on every task assigned to it.

a.text: Tasklist does not pull form schemas from the bpmn-io GitHub repository at runtime; `form-js` is the rendering library, not a remote source of schemas.

c.text: Tasklist renders forms client-side via `form-js` using the deployed schema; Modeler does not serve pre-rendered HTML at runtime.

d.text: The reference does not describe a runtime API call from the user task to obtain the form; the embedded form schema is imported at deployment time.

explanation: Per the Camunda Forms reference page, deploying a BPMN diagram with an embedded form causes Tasklist to import the form schema and use it to render the form on every task assigned to it. Rendering is powered by the open-source bpmn-io `form-js` library, but the schema itself lives with the deployment, not in an external repository.

question: A Camunda Form is rendered as part of a user task. Which of the following statements about how a form field's `Key` attribute participates in data binding is NOT correct?

options.text: The `Key` is only used to map submitted data back to the process variable; initial form values come from a separate property.
A simple `Key` such as `username` maps directly to a process variable named `username`.
Inside a group, the group's path is prefixed to each child field's `Key` to form the full path.
A path-style `Key` such as `user.info.age` produces nested output data under `user.info`.

b.text: The data binding page describes a simple key as a variable name that directly matches a process variable, with the example `key: "username"`.

d.text: The docs show that a path-style key such as `user.info.age` results in nested output JSON under `user.info`.

a.text: This is the false statement and therefore the correct choice for a NOT question. The docs explicitly state the `Key` serves a dual purpose: mapping data to the field on initial form load and mapping the field's data back to the process variable on submission.

c.text: Per the groups section, a group's path acts as a prefix to the key of each child field within that group, extending the full path.

explanation: The form-data-binding documentation states that the `Key` attribute has a dual purpose: it maps process data into the field on initial form load, and it maps the field's submitted value back to the corresponding process variable on submission. Treating `Key` as a write-only binding misses half of its documented role and is the false statement here.

question: A number form field has its `Key` configured as `user.info.age`, and the user submits the value `21`. Which JSON best represents the resulting output data for this field?

options.text: A three-level nested object under `user` and `info`.
An array containing one nested `user` object.
A single flat key with the dotted path as its name.
An object with an empty `user` and a sibling `info.age` key.

a.text: The data binding docs show that a path key such as `user.info.age` set to value `21` produces a nested object: each dot segment becomes a level of nesting and the leaf segment holds the value.

c.text: Path keys are interpreted, not used verbatim as a flat key; the dotted segments are not preserved as a single literal key in the output.

d.text: The docs nest each segment in turn; you do not get a partial nesting where one segment becomes a flat dotted suffix under another.

b.text: A path key produces a nested object, not an array; arrays in Camunda Forms output are produced by dynamic lists, not by a path key alone.

explanation: The data binding page demonstrates that defining a path key like `user.info.age` and entering `21` yields the nested object shape `{ user: { info: { age: 21 } } }`. Path keys exist precisely to express nested structures from a single field rather than producing flat keys or arrays.

question: In Camunda Forms data binding, what is the key structural difference between a `group` path and a `dynamic list` path?

options.text: Groups and dynamic lists both produce arrays, but only dynamic lists may be nested.
Both produce objects; the only difference is that dynamic lists may share their path with groups, while groups may not.
Groups produce a nested object under their path; dynamic lists produce an array of objects under their path.
Groups produce arrays and dynamic lists produce single objects keyed by index.

c.text: The docs describe groups as extending a path prefix over nested object fields, while dynamic lists are bound to arrays and output an array of objects under their own path.

a.text: Groups do not output an array; the docs use them to model nested object structures, not lists of items.

d.text: It is dynamic lists that produce arrays of objects, not groups; the docs are explicit that dynamic lists are bound to arrays.

b.text: The sharing rule is the opposite: groups may share paths if their fields do not conflict, whereas dynamic lists cannot share paths with other dynamic lists or groups.

explanation: Per the data-binding page, groups apply their path as a prefix to child field keys, building a nested object under that path. Dynamic lists are bound to arrays and emit an array of objects under the list's own path. That object-vs-array distinction is the core structural difference, and it also drives the rule that dynamic lists cannot share paths with other dynamic lists or groups.

question: A form group has its `path` set to `user.info`. Three child fields with keys `surname`, `name`, and `age` are submitted as `"Inco"`, `"Gnito"`, and `29`. Which JSON describes the resulting output data?

options.text: An array of single-field objects under `user.info`.
The three field values as siblings of `info` under `user`.
All three fields at the root, with dotted prefixes baked into their keys.
All three fields nested under `user.info`.

d.text: The docs show that a group with path `user.info` and child keys matching field labels yields a nested object with `surname`, `name`, and `age` under `user.info`.

c.text: The group's path becomes nesting, not a literal dotted prefix on each flat key; the output is a nested object, not a flat one.

a.text: Groups produce nested objects, not arrays; arrays are produced by dynamic lists, not by groups.

b.text: The full path is `user.info.<childKey>`, so `surname`, `name`, and `age` sit under `info`, not as siblings of `info` under `user`.

explanation: The data-binding page documents that a group's path acts as a prefix to each child field's key. With path `user.info` and child keys `surname`, `name`, and `age`, the submission produces a nested object with those three properties under `user.info`, mirroring the docs' worked example.

question: A Select form field uses a FEEL expression that returns the shorthand `[{"value": "fr"}, {"value": "de"}]` with no `label` property on any option. Which value types does the form accept in this shorthand?

options.text: `string`, `number`, or `boolean` only.
Only `string` values, since the value also serves as the label.
Numeric types only, because labels must be strings and FEEL coerces values to numbers.
Any non-null type, including objects and lists.

a.text: The supported-types section states that when no `label` is provided, the `value` parameter is restricted to `string`, `number`, and `boolean`. The label-less shorthand falls into this case.

d.text: Any non-null type is allowed only when a `label` is provided. The shorthand omits the label, so this broader rule does not apply.

b.text: Even without a label, the docs allow `number` and `boolean` values in addition to `string`; the shorthand is not restricted to strings only.

c.text: There is no rule that FEEL coerces option values to numbers; the docs explicitly include `string` and `boolean` alongside `number` for the no-label case.

explanation: The supported-types section of the options page distinguishes two cases. With a `label`, the `value` can be any non-null type. Without a `label` (`[{value: ...}]` or `["fr", ...]`), `value` is restricted to `string`, `number`, or `boolean` because the value itself is then also used as the display label.

question: A Radio form field is configured with Dynamic Options and `Input values key` set to `languageData`. What must the `languageData` process variable contain for the radio options to render correctly?

options.text: A FEEL expression string that Tasklist evaluates each time the form opens.
A single object whose keys are option labels and whose values are the option values.
An array of option objects, each with a `label` (string) and a `value`, or one of the shorthand forms.
An XML fragment with `<option>` elements; Tasklist parses it on render.

c.text: The Input values key path expects the variable to be an array of options, each defining its `label` and `value`. The shorthand forms `[{value: ...}]` or `["fr", ...]` are also accepted.

a.text: FEEL expressions belong in the FEEL-expression option source, not in the Input values key path; the Input values key reads data directly, it does not evaluate strings.

b.text: Options must be an array of option objects, not a single map of label-to-value entries; the docs show JSON arrays as the expected shape.

d.text: Camunda Forms does not parse XML for option sources; the docs require JSON arrays of option objects.

explanation: The Dynamic Options configuration's Input values key maps a form variable to the option source. The expected format is an array of option objects with a `label` and a `value`, or one of the documented shorthand forms. FEEL expression strings belong in the FEEL-expression source, not in the Input values key, which reads the variable's value directly.

question: You are configuring a Select form field's options in Camunda Modeler. Which sources does Camunda Forms support for populating that list of options?

options.text: Only static options defined directly on the form schema.
Only a SQL query against an external database configured on the form element.
Only a REST endpoint that Tasklist calls each time the form is rendered.
Static options on the form schema, dynamic options driven by a process variable via `Input values key`, or a FEEL expression that returns an array of options.

d.text: The options page lists three sources: static configuration on the form schema, process-data driven via `Input values key`, and a FEEL expression that returns the option array.

a.text: Static is one of three supported sources; the docs explicitly cover dynamic and FEEL-based options as well.

c.text: The Forms documentation does not provide a REST-endpoint option source; options come from the schema, process data, or a FEEL expression.

b.text: Camunda Forms has no SQL option source; options come from the schema, process data via `Input values key`, or a FEEL expression.

explanation: The Configuring form field options sources page documents exactly three sources for option-style fields like Radio and Select: static options on the form schema, dynamic options pulled from process data via an `Input values key`, and a FEEL expression that returns the option array. REST endpoints and SQL queries are not documented option sources.

question: How can the column headers of a Table form field be defined via the `Headers source` property?

options.text: Only via an expression; statically defined headers are not supported.
Statically as a list of items (each with a `Label` and `Key`), or dynamically via an expression that returns a list of `{ label, key }` objects.
By inferring header names automatically from the first row returned by the `Data source`.
Only statically as a fixed list of header labels on the form schema.

b.text: The table data-binding page lists exactly two options for `Headers source`: a static List of items with `Label` and `Key`, or an Expression returning a list of `{ label, key }` objects.

d.text: The expression-based dynamic source is explicitly supported alongside the static list-of-items source, so static-only is incorrect.

a.text: List of items is the first option documented for `Headers source`; expressions are not the only way to define table headers.

c.text: The docs do not describe header inference from the `Data source`; you must explicitly define headers either statically or via an expression.

explanation: The `Headers source` property of the Table form field accepts either a static List of items, where you define each column's `Label` and `Key` directly, or an Expression that returns a list of objects with `label` and `key` properties. Inferring headers from the `Data source` is not part of the documented behaviour.

question: What input does the `Data source` property of a Table form field accept?

options.text: A URL pointing to an external JSON endpoint that Tasklist fetches on render.
An expression that returns a single object whose properties become rows.
Only an expression that evaluates to a list of objects, one object per table row.
Either a static JSON literal or an expression returning a list.

c.text: The Data source section explicitly states the field accepts only expressions, and the expression must return a list of objects, where each object represents one row.

d.text: The docs say the `Data source` field accepts only expressions; a static JSON literal is not the documented input form for this property.

b.text: The expression must return a list of objects (each object a row), not a single object whose properties expand into rows.

a.text: Camunda Forms does not fetch table data from a URL via this property; the property is an expression that returns the row list directly.

explanation: Per the Data source section, the Table form field's `Data source` accepts only expressions, and the expression must evaluate to a list of objects where each object represents one row and has one property per declared column. External fetches and static literals are not documented input forms.

question: Inside a Feelers template, what can appear between the double curly braces of an insert such as `{{ ... }}`?

options.text: JavaScript expressions; FEEL is evaluated separately on form submission only.
Only a single variable name; no FEEL operators or function calls are permitted.
Plain text only, with no expression evaluation at all.
Any valid FEEL expression; its evaluated value is inserted at that position.

d.text: The Variables/inserts section states you can use any valid FEEL expression within the double braces, with `{{if isAdmin then "an admin" else "a user"}}` as an example of a FEEL conditional in an insert.

b.text: Feelers inserts are not limited to bare variable names; the docs explicitly show a full FEEL `if ... then ... else ...` expression inside `{{ ... }}`.

a.text: Feelers does not use JavaScript; the docs say expressions inside double braces are FEEL.

c.text: The braces are evaluated, not treated as plain text; the docs show `{{username}}` being replaced by the variable's value.

explanation: The Variables/inserts section makes the rule explicit: anything inside `{{ ... }}` may be any valid FEEL expression. The worked example shows both a simple variable reference and a FEEL conditional in the same template line, confirming that the brace contents are evaluated as FEEL rather than treated as plain text or restricted to identifiers.

codeBlocks.stem: Hello {{username}}, you are {{if isAdmin then "an admin" else "a user"}}.

question: A Feelers template loops over `nodes`, where each node object already has a property literally named `parent`. Inside the loop, which accessor reaches the outer-scope `root` value?

options.text: `this.parent.root`
`parent.root`
`_parent_.root`
`__parent.root`

c.text: The Additional details section gives this exact case: when the data already uses `parent` as a key, the underscore-wrapped `_parent_` accessor disambiguates from the property and resolves to the outer scope. The same rule applies to `_this_`.

b.text: `parent.root` would resolve to the node's own `parent` property (which has no `root` field), not the outer scope; the docs call out this collision explicitly.

a.text: There is no `this.parent` chain in Feelers; `this` references the loop element itself, and chaining `parent` from it does not invoke the loop's outer-scope accessor.

d.text: The alternative accessor uses a single underscore on each side (`_parent_`), not a double underscore prefix.

explanation: When loop data shadows the loop accessor names (`this`, `parent`), Feelers provides underscore-wrapped alternatives `_this_` and `_parent_` that unambiguously refer to the loop element and the outer scope. The Additional details example uses `_parent_.root` precisely because each node object already carries a `parent` field.

question: A Camunda Forms checkbox is bound via its `Key` to a process variable whose current value is the string `"yes"`. With no `Default value` configured, how does the checkbox initially render?

options.text: Checked, because any non-empty string coerces to `true`.
Displays an inline validation error and prevents the form from loading until the variable is corrected.
Falls back to the previous user's submitted state stored in Tasklist.
Unchecked, because non-boolean data is treated as `false` by default.

d.text: The Datatypes section of the checkbox page states that checkboxes are bound to `boolean` data, and any other datatype is treated as `false` by default. A string value such as `"yes"` therefore renders the checkbox unchecked.

a.text: Camunda Forms does not perform JavaScript-style truthy coercion of non-empty strings; the docs are explicit that non-boolean data is treated as `false`.

b.text: The docs describe non-boolean data being treated as `false`, not raising a validation error that blocks the form from rendering.

c.text: There is no fallback to a previous user's state; binding always reflects the current process variable, with non-boolean values defaulting to `false`.

explanation: The checkbox element's Datatypes note says it must be bound to boolean data and that any other datatype is treated as `false` by default. A string variable such as `"yes"` does not satisfy the boolean type expectation, so the field renders as unchecked rather than checked or erroring.

question: An Expression form field has its `Target value` set to a FEEL expression and its `Compute on` property set to `only on form submission`. As the user types into the rest of the form, when does the expression re-evaluate?

options.text: Whenever the user changes focus between fields, regardless of the `Compute on` setting.
On every keystroke, since FEEL expressions always re-evaluate when any dependency changes.
Once on initial render and then never again until the process instance ends.
Only when the form is submitted; the result is not recomputed on intermediate input changes.

d.text: The Expression field docs describe `Compute on` as choosing between recomputing whenever the result changes or only on form submission. With `only on form submission` selected, the expression evaluates at submission time, not during typing.

b.text: Per-change recomputation corresponds to the other `Compute on` setting (`whenever the result changes`), not `only on form submission`.

c.text: The docs do not describe a one-shot render-only evaluation tied to the process instance lifecycle; `Compute on` selects between change-driven and submission-time evaluation.

a.text: Focus changes are not the trigger described in the docs; the trigger is either a change in the computed result or form submission, depending on `Compute on`.

explanation: The Configurable properties of the Expression field include `Compute on`, which selects when the FEEL expression is evaluated: either whenever the result changes, or only on form submission. Choosing `only on form submission` defers evaluation until submit, so intermediate user input does not trigger re-evaluation.

question: A user task uses a Camunda Forms number field. Which of the following statements about the number element is NOT true according to the documentation?

options.text: A number field can only be bound to numeric process data; string variables are rejected outright.
The `Prefix` and `Suffix` appearance options add an appendage before or after the input.
`Read only` can be set dynamically using a FEEL expression.
Validation supports `Required`, `Minimum`, and `Maximum` rules on the field's value.

c.text: The Configurable properties section says `Read only` can be dynamically set using a FEEL expression, so this statement is true.

d.text: The Validation section lists exactly `Required`, `Minimum`, and `Maximum` for the number field, so this statement is true.

b.text: The Appearance options include `Prefix` and `Suffix`, which add an appendage before or after the input, so this statement is true.

a.text: This is the false statement and therefore the correct choice for a NOT question. The Datatypes note explicitly says the field can be bound to numeric data or to strings that can be parsed to numeric data; string variables are not rejected.

explanation: The number element's Datatypes note documents that the field can be bound to numeric data or to strings parseable to numeric data (as per JavaScript's `parseInt`). Claiming that string variables are rejected outright contradicts the docs, which is what makes that option the correct answer to a `NOT true` question.

question: A number field with **Key** `quantity`, **Minimum** `1` and **Maximum** `100` is on a Camunda Form. The user enters `0` and clicks submit. What happens?

options.text: The form submits but the number field becomes read-only.
The form submits and `quantity` is stored as `0`.
The form does not submit; a validation error is shown.
The form submits and `quantity` is auto-clamped to `1`.

b.text: Validation blocks submission when the value violates the configured Minimum, so `0` is never stored on the form variable.

d.text: The number element does not silently clamp out-of-range values; it surfaces a validation error and waits for the user to correct the input.

c.text: Per the docs, when Minimum is set the form only submits when the value is at least `n`. A value below the Minimum produces a validation error and blocks submission.

a.text: Read only is a separate configurable property and is not toggled as a side effect of a failed Minimum/Maximum validation.

explanation: The Minimum and Maximum validations on a number field gate form submission: the form only submits when the value satisfies the configured bound. A value below Minimum displays a validation error and the form is not submitted; the field is not silently clamped or made read-only.

question: Which of the following is NOT a configurable property of the number element in the Camunda Forms element library?

options.text: Decimal digits
Locale
Increment
Serialize to string

a.text: Decimal digits is listed in the docs as a configurable property — it defines the maximum number of digits after the decimal.

c.text: Increment is listed in the docs and defines the increment between valid field values.

b.text: Locale is not listed as a configurable property of the number element. The documented appearance options for formatting are Prefix and Suffix, not a Locale setting.

d.text: Serialize to string is listed in the docs and enables unlimited precision digits on the field's output.

explanation: The documented configurable properties of the number element are Field label, Field description, Key, Default value, Decimal digits, Increment, Read only, Disabled, Hide if, Columns, Serialize to string, the Validation group (Required, Minimum, Maximum) and the Appearance group (Prefix, Suffix). Locale is not on that list.

question: What is the effect of enabling the **Serialize to string** property on a Camunda Forms number element?

options.text: It enables unlimited precision digits in the field's output value.
It converts the field into a read-only display element.
It exposes a regular-expression pattern matcher on the field.
It restricts user input to integer values only.

a.text: The docs state that Serialize to string configures the output format and enables unlimited precision digits — useful when the precision of native numeric types is not enough.

d.text: Restricting to integers is closer to the default behaviour described under Datatypes; it is not what Serialize to string toggles.

c.text: The number element exposes no regex pattern matcher; the only validation properties are Required, Minimum and Maximum.

b.text: Making the field non-editable is the role of the separate Read only property, not Serialize to string.

explanation: Serialize to string changes how the number field's value is serialised on output: instead of being constrained by the precision of a native numeric type, the value is emitted as a string that can carry unlimited precision digits. Read only and validation bounds (Required, Minimum, Maximum) are configured by other properties.

question: On a Camunda Forms select element, the **Default value** property is available only when which condition is met?

options.text: The bound data type is `string`.
The options source is **static**.
The select has **Validation > Required** enabled.
The select is marked **Searchable**.

d.text: Searchable lets the user search entries via keyboard but is independent of whether a Default value can be configured.

b.text: The docs explicitly note that Default value is only available for static options sources, because the option set must be known at design time to bind a default.

c.text: Required only enforces that the user must pick a value at submission; it does not unlock the Default value property.

a.text: Selects can be bound to any datatype (strings are recommended); the binding type does not control whether Default value is editable.

explanation: On a select element, Default value can only be pre-configured when the Options source is static, because Camunda must know the option set at design time to bind a default. When the Options source is dynamic (resolved at runtime from an expression), the docs state Default value is not available.

question: A select element is bound via **Key** `priority` to an existing process variable whose value is `"urgent"`, but the configured options source only defines `low`, `medium` and `high`. What does the user see when the form renders?

options.text: The bound value `urgent` is ignored and no option is pre-selected.
The form fails to render and an error is shown to the user.
The first defined option (`low`) is pre-selected as a fallback.
A new option `urgent` is added to the dropdown at render time.

b.text: Form rendering does not fail when the bound value has no matching option; the docs describe an explicit ignore-on-mismatch behaviour.

d.text: The select does not synthesise new options from the bound data; the option set comes from the configured Options source.

a.text: Per the Datatypes section, the select correlates the bound data value with the appropriate option from the options source. If no matching option is found, the data is simply ignored.

c.text: There is no documented fallback to the first option; the field renders with no pre-selection when the value does not match.

explanation: The select correlates the bound data value with its options. When the value does not match any option in the configured Options source — here `urgent` against `low`/`medium`/`high` — the docs say the data is simply ignored, so the dropdown renders with no pre-selection.

question: A developer adds a table to a Camunda Form and wants to populate its rows from a static list of three hard-coded items. How must the table's **Data source** field be configured?

options.text: As an expression that returns the list (e.g. a FEEL list literal).
As plain text — paste the JSON array directly into the field.
As templating syntax with a `{{#loop}}` block enumerating each item.
Via the **Headers source** field — Data source is optional for static rows.

b.text: The Data source field accepts an expression only — plain text is not supported here, unlike on many other form-element fields.

c.text: Templating syntax is not the binding mechanism for the table's Data source; the docs state it can only be an expression.

a.text: The docs state the Data source can only be an expression, so a static list must be written as an expression (typically a FEEL list literal) that returns the rows.

d.text: Headers source defines the columns, not the rows; Data source is the row binding and it is required, not optional.

explanation: Unlike many other Camunda Forms fields that accept plain text, expression or templating, the table's Data source field accepts an expression only. To bind a static set of rows, encode them as a FEEL expression (for example a list literal) that returns the desired list.

question: A FEEL expression bound to the **Text** property of a text component evaluates at runtime to a string containing `<script>alert('xss')</script>` followed by some Markdown. What does the rendered form show?

options.text: All Markdown formatting is stripped and only the literal script tag remains visible.
The `<script>` element is dropped during rendering and does not execute; the surrounding Markdown still renders.
An alert dialog pops up when the form is rendered to the user.
The form refuses to render and shows a validation error to the user.

c.text: The text component sanitises potentially harmful HTML before rendering, so an injected `<script>` does not execute and no alert is shown.

b.text: The docs state that to ensure safety and prevent cross-site scripting, potentially harmful HTML elements will not be rendered. The script tag is therefore dropped from output, while the surrounding Markdown is still processed by the renderer.

d.text: Validation errors are a property of input fields (e.g. number, text field), not of the read-only text component, which silently sanitises its output.

a.text: Markdown formatting is not stripped because of a script tag; the renderer continues to process GitHub-flavored Markdown and safe HTML — only harmful elements are dropped.

explanation: The text component's Text property is processed by a Markdown renderer that supports basic HTML and GitHub-flavored Markdown, but the docs explicitly say potentially harmful HTML elements are not rendered. That built-in sanitisation drops `<script>` (and similar) tags from the output while leaving the safe Markdown intact, providing XSS protection without breaking the rest of the text.

question: A text component's **Text** property uses the template snippet shown. When the form variable `user.hobbies` is the list `["chess", "hiking"]`, how does the iteration block render?

options.text: As an unordered list with two items: `chess` and `hiking`.
Nothing renders until a FEEL expression is wrapped around the loop block.
As a single line containing the literal `["chess", "hiking"]`.
As the literal template source `{{#loop user.hobbies}}* {{this}}{{/loop}}`.

a.text: The `{{#loop ...}}` block iterates the list, the leading `*` produces a Markdown bullet for each iteration, and `{{this}}` is replaced by the current list item — yielding two bullets `chess` and `hiking`.

c.text: Template syntax expands the loop into per-item output; the raw list literal is not what the user sees.

d.text: The text component evaluates template syntax before rendering, so the source template string is not displayed literally to the user.

b.text: FEEL is not required to wrap a template block; the template syntax is rendered directly by the text component.

explanation: The text component supports template syntax. `{{#loop user.hobbies}}* {{this}}{{/loop}}` iterates the bound list, emits a Markdown bullet `*` for each item, and `{{this}}` is substituted with the current value. The resulting Markdown is then rendered by the text component as an unordered list.

question: After editing a Camunda Form in Web Modeler, what does the author need to do to save their changes?

options.text: Re-deploy the form to the cluster so the change is persisted.
Press `Ctrl+S` to commit the change to the current deployment.
Nothing — Web Modeler autosaves every change the author makes.
Click the **Save** button at the top right of the form editor.

d.text: There is no manual Save button in Web Modeler for forms; the docs state you don't have to do anything to save your form.

b.text: A Ctrl+S keystroke is not required to persist changes — Web Modeler is autosaving in the background.

c.text: The docs explicitly state that to save your form in Camunda 8 you don't have to do anything; Web Modeler will autosave every change you make.

a.text: Deployment is a separate step that pushes the saved form to the cluster so it can be executed; it is not how the form is saved in Web Modeler.

explanation: Web Modeler autosaves every change made to a form, so no explicit save action is required from the author. Deployment is a separate concern: it pushes the latest saved form (and any linked diagrams) to the Camunda 8 cluster so it can be executed.

question: A user task in a Camunda 8.8 BPMN diagram is linked to a Camunda Form in Web Modeler. The form designer updates the form's schema. What must the diagram author do for the updated form to be used the next time the diagram is deployed?

options.text: Detach and then re-attach the form binding before clicking Deploy.
Nothing — the diagram always resolves to the latest form and the form is auto-deployed with it.
Manually re-link the form to the user task each time the form changes.
Edit a JSON configuration on the user task to point at the new form version.

c.text: Manual re-linking on every form change is exactly what the 8.4 improvement removed from the workflow; it is no longer required.

d.text: Editing a JSON configuration is not required — the docs explicitly call out that no manual JSON configuration is needed for linked forms anymore.

b.text: Since the 8.4 improvements, diagrams always have the latest form updates, no manual re-link or JSON configuration is needed, and linked forms are automatically deployed with the diagram.

a.text: Detach/re-attach is not part of the documented workflow; the link automatically resolves to the latest form version.

explanation: The 8.4 improvements to linked-form handling in Web Modeler — still in effect in 8.8 — mean a diagram's linked form always reflects the latest saved form, no manual re-linking or JSON configuration is needed, and linked forms are automatically deployed together with the diagram.

question: A form designer places a submit button into a Camunda Form's schema, then links the form to a user task. When the user opens that task in Tasklist, where does the submit button appear?

options.text: It is moved to the top of the form so the user sees it first.
It is hidden — Camunda controls when the user can complete the task.
It is converted into a Cancel button next to the form.
Inline in the rendered form, exactly where it was placed in the editor.

d.text: The docs state that any submit button in the form schema is hidden when the form is used through a user task.

b.text: When using Camunda Forms with a user task, any submit button present in the form schema is hidden so that Camunda can control when a user is allowed to complete the task.

a.text: Camunda Forms does not relocate submit buttons in user tasks; it hides them entirely so Tasklist's Complete action governs completion.

c.text: The submit button is not converted to a cancel button; it is simply not rendered.

explanation: When a Camunda Form is rendered as part of a user task, any submit button defined in the form schema is suppressed. Tasklist exposes its own Complete action instead, so the platform can govern when task completion is permitted (e.g. only after validations pass) rather than letting the user submit the form directly.

question: In Web Modeler, how do you link an existing Camunda Form to a user task on a BPMN diagram?

options.text: Add an `extensionElement` to the BPMN XML by hand pointing at the form's id.
Select the user task, click the blue overlay with the link icon on its right side, navigate to the form, and click **Link**.
Drag the form from the project sidebar and drop it onto the user task on the canvas.
Open the user task's properties panel and type the form's filename into a free-text field.

c.text: Drag-and-drop from the project sidebar onto the canvas is not the documented linking workflow for forms in Web Modeler.

d.text: There is no free-text filename field for linking; the link is established via the blue link overlay on the user task itself.

b.text: The docs describe selecting the user task, clicking the blue overlay with the link icon on its right side to open the navigation menu, navigating to the desired form and clicking the blue Link button — after which the overlay stays visible to indicate a linked form.

a.text: Hand-editing BPMN XML is not required; Web Modeler manages the link through the user-task overlay.

explanation: Linking is a UI gesture in Web Modeler: select the user task, click the blue link-icon overlay that appears on its right side to open the navigation menu, navigate to the form, and click the blue Link button. Once linked, the overlay remains visible on the task as an indicator.

question: A BPMN diagram in Web Modeler has two user tasks, each linked to a different Camunda Form. The author clicks the blue **Deploy** button on the diagram. What gets deployed?

options.text: Only the form linked to the first user task in flow order.
The BPMN diagram together with both linked Camunda Forms.
Only the BPMN diagram; the forms must be deployed separately afterwards.
Nothing — Deploy only validates the diagram in Web Modeler.

c.text: Linked Camunda Forms are deployed together with the diagram; a separate form deployment step is not required.

b.text: The docs state that linked forms will automatically be deployed along with the diagram when the author clicks Deploy.

a.text: All linked Camunda Forms on the diagram are deployed, not just the one on the first user task.

d.text: Deploy actually deploys the diagram to the engine; it is not just a validation step inside Web Modeler.

explanation: Clicking Deploy in Web Modeler pushes the diagram to the Camunda 8 cluster together with every Camunda Form linked to its user tasks (and none start events). The author does not need a separate deploy action per form.

question: A user task asks the user to upload a contract PDF that downstream service tasks will process. Which Camunda Forms component is intended for this use case?

options.text: The **Document preview** component.
A **Table** bound to a list of file metadata.
A **Text field** with a custom file-path validation.
The **Filepicker** component.

d.text: The docs point at the Filepicker form component as the way to let users upload files in a Camunda Form, including building a form for document upload to a BPMN process.

a.text: Document preview displays and allows download of an existing document; it does not collect a new upload from the user.

c.text: A text field captures only string input; it cannot transport file binaries into the process via a form submission.

b.text: A table renders rows from a data source; it is not a file-upload control.

explanation: Camunda Forms exposes a dedicated Filepicker component for collecting user-uploaded files (such as a contract PDF) and feeding them into the BPMN process via document handling. The companion Document preview component handles the opposite direction — displaying and downloading an existing document.

question: How is a Camunda Form associated with a user task in Camunda 8?

options.text: By a runtime API call from Tasklist after the user opens the task.
By writing HTML in a documentation annotation on the task.
By a form key / form ID on the user task; the form may be embedded in the deployment or linked by ID.
It must always be embedded inline in the BPMN XML.

d.text: Inline embedding is not required. Forms are referenced from the user task by key or id and can be deployed independently.

c.text: User tasks reference a form by key or id. The form itself can be deployed alongside the BPMN (embedded form) or referenced by id (linked form). Tasklist renders the referenced form at runtime.

b.text: Documentation annotations are descriptive metadata and do not render a form.

a.text: Tasklist does not associate forms at runtime; the link is part of the deployed user task definition.

explanation: A user task references a Camunda Form by a form key or form id. The form can be embedded in the same deployment or linked by id; Tasklist renders the referenced form when the user opens the task.

question: A new user opens Operate but the dashboard shows no deployed processes or running instances. Per the basic-navigation guide, what prerequisite must be met before this section's steps apply?

options.text: Optimize must be installed and connected to the Zeebe Elasticsearch exporter first.
The user must be assigned the `operate-admin` role in Identity before any process appears.
A process must be deployed to Zeebe and at least one process instance must exist.
Tasklist must be running and have at least one claimed user task before processes are listed.

c.text: The page's opening note states the section (and the next on variables and incidents) assumes you have deployed a process to Zeebe and created at least one process instance.

b.text: The docs prerequisite is a deployed process and a running instance, not an Identity role assignment.

a.text: Optimize is only suggested elsewhere for performance visualization; it is not a prerequisite for the basic-navigation steps.

d.text: Tasklist activity is unrelated to whether Operate's dashboard lists deployed processes.

explanation: The basic-navigation guide opens with a note that the section assumes a process has been deployed to Zeebe and at least one instance has been created; otherwise it points the user to the guides section to get started with Camunda 8.

question: An operator opens the **Processes** tab in Operate after clicking a deployed process name in the **Process Instances by Name** panel. Which operation can be performed directly from a row's **Operations** column on a single running instance?

options.text: Migrate the running instance to a newer process definition version inline.
Cancel the running process instance via the cancel icon under **Operations**.
Edit the BPMN XML of the running instance from the Operations column.
Restart a completed run from the start event using a re-run icon.

b.text: The Processes view explicitly says you can cancel a single running process instance by clicking the cancel icon under **Operations** next to the process.

a.text: Migration is a separate Operate feature reached from the process page header, not the per-row Operations icon described here.

c.text: Operate does not let an operator edit BPMN XML; editing belongs to Modeler.

d.text: Operate has no restart-from-start action; completed instances are immutable history.

explanation: On the **Processes** view reached via the **Process Instances by Name** panel, the documented per-row action under **Operations** is a cancel icon that cancels a single running instance. Migration and modification are separate features and BPMN editing is a Modeler task.

question: Beyond inspecting a single instance via its **Process Instance Key**, which Camunda component does the Operate user guide recommend for visualizing the **performance** of process instances?

options.text: Tasklist.
Optimize.
Modeler.
Console.

b.text: The Inspect a process instance section ends by recommending Optimize for visualizing the performance of process instances.

a.text: Tasklist is for working on user tasks, not for process performance dashboards.

c.text: Modeler is for designing BPMN/DMN, not for runtime performance analytics.

d.text: Console manages clusters and deployments; it does not provide performance visualizations of instances.

explanation: Operate exposes instance history and variables on the instance detail page, but the docs explicitly direct users to Optimize when the goal is to visualize the performance of process instances.

question: After confirming the deletion of a finished process instance from Operate's **Processes** page, where is the progress of the delete operation visible per the docs?

options.text: In the **Migrations** tab next to the process definition.
In a toast notification at the bottom-left corner of the dashboard.
In the **Operations** panel on the right side of the screen.
In the **Audit Log** view under Identity.

c.text: Step 4 of the documented flow says the deleted instance is visible in the **Operations** panel on the right side of the screen.

b.text: The docs do not describe a bottom-left toast for delete progress; the Operations panel is the documented location.

d.text: Identity's audit log is unrelated to Operate's per-operation progress UI.

a.text: The **Migrations** area concerns process migrations, not finished-instance deletes.

explanation: The Processes-page delete flow ends with the operator viewing the deleted process instance entry in the right-side **Operations** panel. That panel is the documented place to track progress of operations performed in Operate.

question: On Operate's **Processes** page an operator wants to delete a completed process instance, but no **Delete** button appears next to any row in the list. Which step does the docs say is missing?

options.text: Filter by **Tenant ID** so per-tenant operations become available.
Switch to the **Active** + **Incidents** filter combination to expose delete actions.
Apply the **Finished Instances** filter by checking the appropriate box on the **Processes** page.
Apply a **Process Instance Key** range filter to scope the delete.

c.text: Step 1 of the documented flow says you must apply the **Finished Instances** filter by checking the appropriate box before delete is available per instance.

b.text: Active and Incidents filters reveal running or incidented instances, not finished ones, so they do not expose the per-row Delete button described here.

a.text: Tenant filtering is a separate scoping concern; it is not the documented prerequisite for the Delete button to appear.

d.text: There is no documented Process Instance Key range filter that unlocks deletion in this flow.

explanation: The Delete button on the Processes page only applies to finished instances, so the documented first step is checking the **Finished Instances** filter. Without that filter applied, the list shows running instances which do not expose the per-row delete action.

question: After clicking the **Delete** button next to a finished process instance on Operate's **Processes** page, what does the docs say must happen before the delete is performed?

options.text: The operator must wait for the documented 24-hour soft-delete grace period.
The operator must confirm the operation by clicking **Delete** in the confirmation prompt.
The operator must enter a justification comment that is written to an audit log.
The operator must re-authenticate via a step-up MFA challenge in Identity.

b.text: Step 3 of the documented Processes-page flow is to confirm the delete operation by clicking **Delete** in the prompt.

d.text: The docs do not describe a step-up MFA requirement for finished-instance deletion.

c.text: There is no documented justification or audit-comment requirement in this Operate flow.

a.text: There is no 24-hour grace period in the documented flow; deletion happens once the confirmation prompt's Delete is clicked.

explanation: Operate's documented delete flow has three operator clicks: apply the Finished Instances filter, click Delete on the row, then confirm by clicking Delete in the prompt. There is no MFA, justification, or grace period documented for this action.

question: From the **Processes** page, an operator has applied the **Finished Instances** filter and clicked a **Process Instance Key**. According to the docs, what is the next step to delete that instance from the **instance detail** page?

options.text: Use the cancel icon under **Operations** in the Process Instances by Name panel.
Click the **Delete** button on the instance detail page, then confirm by clicking **Delete**.
Run a `zbctl cancel instance` command against the broker for that key.
Submit a deletion job to Optimize and wait for the next ETL cycle.

b.text: After navigating to the instance detail page, the documented next steps are clicking the **Delete** button and then confirming the delete operation by clicking **Delete**.

a.text: The cancel icon in the Process Instances by Name panel cancels running instances; it is not the documented delete path on the instance detail page.

c.text: Deletion of a finished instance is performed in Operate's UI; the docs do not describe a `zbctl` command for this flow.

d.text: Optimize is for performance analytics, not for performing or queuing Operate instance deletions.

explanation: On the instance detail page the documented flow is: click **Delete**, then confirm by clicking **Delete** in the confirmation dialog. The same source notes that, once confirmed, the instance is deleted and cannot be accessed again.

question: After confirming the deletion of a finished process instance from its **instance detail** page in Operate, which statement matches the caution that the docs include?

options.text: The instance is now deleted and you may not access it again.
The instance is moved to a soft-delete bin and is recoverable for 30 days.
The instance is hidden but remains visible with a **Deleted** tag until the next history retention cycle.
The instance is exported to cold storage and remains queryable through Optimize.

a.text: The documented caution after confirming the delete reads: use caution as the process instance is now deleted and you may not access it again.

b.text: There is no documented soft-delete bin or 30-day recovery window; the docs treat the deletion as immediate and final.

d.text: The docs do not describe a cold-storage export of deleted instances to Optimize.

c.text: There is no documented Deleted tag or retention-cycle visibility for the instance after confirmation.

explanation: Operate's instance-detail delete flow ends with an explicit caution that the process instance is now deleted and may not be accessed again. There is no documented soft-delete, grace period, or archived view for the instance after confirmation.

question: Which sequence matches the documented steps for deleting a finished process instance from its **instance detail** page in Operate?

options.text: Open the **Operations** panel → select **Bulk delete** → tick the instance → click **Apply**.
Open the instance modification dialog → cancel all tokens → click **Delete instance**.
Apply the **Finished Instances** filter → click the **Process Instance Key** → click **Delete** → confirm by clicking **Delete**.
Click **Cancel** on the instance detail page → choose **Soft cancel** → confirm.

c.text: The documented steps are: apply the Finished Instances filter, navigate to the instance detail page by clicking the Process Instance Key, click Delete, then confirm by clicking Delete.

a.text: The Operations panel shows the progress of operations but is not where the deletion is initiated; there is no documented Bulk delete in this flow.

b.text: Modification cancels tokens or sets variables on a running instance; it is not the documented path for deleting a finished instance from the detail page.

d.text: Cancel applies to running instances, and there is no documented Soft cancel action for finished-instance deletion.

explanation: Operate's instance-detail delete sequence is fixed: filter for Finished Instances, click the Process Instance Key to open the detail page, click Delete, and confirm with the Delete button in the prompt. Cancel, Bulk delete, and modification are different features that do not apply here.

question: Before clicking the **Delete** button at the top right to delete a process definition from Operate's **Processes** page, what does the docs say you must do first?

options.text: Stop the Zeebe broker that originally accepted the deployment.
Mark the process definition as `archived: true` in `application.yaml`.
Cancel the deployment in Modeler's deployment history view.
Filter by process name and version so a specific process version is selected.

d.text: Step 1 of the documented flow is to select a specific process version on the Processes page by filtering by process name and version before the Delete button at the top right applies.

c.text: Modeler does not have a deployment-cancellation step that gates Operate's delete; the delete is initiated in Operate directly.

a.text: Stopping a broker is not part of the documented process-definition deletion flow.

b.text: There is no `archived: true` marker in the documented flow; deletion is performed from Operate's UI.

explanation: Operate deletes a specific process definition version, so the documented first step on the Processes page is to filter by process name and version to select that version. Only then does the top-right Delete button delete the intended definition.

question: A process definition version has 3 running instances. An operator filters the **Processes** page by that name and version and clicks **Delete** at the top right. According to the docs, what is the result?

options.text: The running instances continue independently of the deleted definition.
The running instances are migrated automatically to the next available version.
The running instances are soft-cancelled with a 7-day grace period before final removal.
Deletion is not possible until the running instances are cancelled or resolved.

d.text: The docs explicitly note that the selected process definition version must have no running instances, otherwise it is not possible to delete the definition; running instances must first be cancelled or resolved.

b.text: Deleting a process definition does not trigger automatic migration of running instances to another version.

a.text: Running instances cannot continue without their process definition; the delete is blocked while instances are still running.

c.text: There is no documented 7-day soft-cancel grace period; the delete is simply blocked while running instances exist.

explanation: Operate refuses to delete a process definition version that has running instances. The docs direct the operator to first cancel or resolve running instances from the process instances list or instance detail page, then retry the deletion.

question: A process definition version is successfully deleted from Operate's **Processes** page after all its instances have completed. According to the docs, what happens to the deleted definition's **finished process instances**?

options.text: They are archived to Tasklist as read-only artifacts.
They are preserved indefinitely in Operate's history for compliance reporting.
They are exported to Optimize and only removed from Operate.
They are deleted from the application along with the process definition.

d.text: The note on the page states that deleting a process definition permanently removes it and that all the deleted definition's finished process instances will be deleted from the application.

b.text: The docs say the finished instances are removed, not preserved indefinitely for compliance.

c.text: Deleting a process definition does not move finished instances to Optimize; they are deleted.

a.text: Tasklist holds user tasks, not full process-instance history; finished instances are deleted, not archived to Tasklist.

explanation: The docs warn that deleting a process definition is permanent: all of its finished instances are deleted from the application, and any decision and process instances referenced by those instances are deleted too. There is no archival or compliance-preservation step.

question: A process definition that contains user tasks is deleted from Operate's **Processes** page. According to the docs, what is the impact on **Tasklist**?

options.text: The user tasks remain in Tasklist but are flagged as orphaned.
The user tasks are migrated automatically to the latest deployed version of the process.
The user tasks are moved to a Tasklist `archived` tenant for retention.
The user tasks defined by that process definition are deleted from Tasklist.

d.text: The impact list on the docs page explicitly states that, if a process definition contains user tasks, they will be deleted from Tasklist when the definition is deleted.

a.text: The docs say the user tasks are deleted from Tasklist, not flagged as orphaned and retained.

b.text: Deleting a process definition does not trigger any documented automatic migration to another version's user tasks.

c.text: There is no documented Tasklist archived tenant; the user tasks of the deleted definition are deleted.

explanation: Deleting a process definition cascades into Tasklist: any user tasks that belong to that definition are deleted from Tasklist. The same delete also removes the deleted definition's finished process instances and any decision and process instances they referenced.

question: Which statement about deleting a decision definition from Operate's **Decisions** page is NOT true according to the docs?

options.text: All other decision tables and literal expressions that are part of the DRD are also deleted.
Only the selected decision definition is removed; other decision tables and literal expressions in the same DRD are left intact.
Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents.
Deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD that contains this decision definition.

b.text: This is the false statement and therefore the correct answer for a negative-kind question: the docs say deleting a decision definition deletes the whole DRD that contains it, so the other tables and literal expressions are NOT left intact.

d.text: True per the docs: deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD that contains this decision definition.

a.text: True per the docs: all other decision tables and literal expressions that are part of the DRD are also deleted.

c.text: True per the docs: deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents.

explanation: Operate's decision-definition deletion is DRD-scoped. The docs warn that deleting one decision definition deletes the entire DRD, including all other decision tables and literal expressions in that DRD, and that removing the only existing version could trigger process incidents. Any statement that the other DRD elements survive the delete is false.

question: In Operate, what does the docs warn could be a consequence of deleting the **only existing version** of a decision definition from the **Decisions** page?

options.text: The DRD is preserved with a placeholder decision table for backward compatibility.
Running process instances are migrated automatically to a no-op DMN decision.
The decision is automatically redeployed from the last deployment artifact.
It could result in process incidents.

d.text: The note on the page states that deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents.

b.text: There is no documented automatic migration to a no-op DMN decision; the docs warn about incidents instead.

a.text: Deleting a decision definition deletes the entire DRD; the docs do not describe a placeholder decision table.

c.text: The decision is not automatically redeployed; the deletion is final and may cause incidents at evaluation time.

explanation: Operate warns that removing the only version of a decision definition leaves processes that call that decision without a target, which could result in process incidents at runtime. The same delete also removes the entire DRD that contains the decision.

scenario: On the Operate Decisions page, an operator filters by a specific decision name and version, clicks Delete, confirms the checkbox, and clicks Delete in the modal. The targeted decision definition belongs to a DRD that also contains two other decision tables and one literal expression.
question: After the delete operation runs from the Operate Decisions page, what happens to the sibling decision tables and the literal expression in the same DRD?

options.text: The selected version is removed but earlier versions of the same decision remain available so the DRD can still be evaluated.
The delete is rejected until every other decision definition in the DRD has been deleted individually first.
The entire DRD is deleted, so all other decision tables and the literal expression that are part of the DRD are deleted along with the targeted decision.
Only the selected decision definition is removed; the other decision tables and the literal expression in the DRD are preserved.

d.text: The docs warn explicitly that deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD, so sibling decision tables and literal expressions are not preserved.

c.text: The Operate docs state that deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD that contains it, and all other decision tables and literal expressions that are part of that DRD are also deleted.

a.text: The delete acts on the DRD, not just a single version, so other versions in the DRD do not survive the operation.

b.text: Operate does not require deleting other decision definitions first; it deletes the whole DRD in one operation when you confirm the delete.

explanation: The Operate Decisions delete flow operates at the DRD level: removing a decision definition removes the DRD that contains it, which takes every other decision table and literal expression in that DRD with it. The docs also call out that deleting the only existing version can cause process incidents in instances that still reference the decision.

question: Which capability is part of Camunda Console as described in the introduction page?

options.text: Resolve runtime incidents on an active process instance.
Assign and complete BPMN user tasks for end users.
Manage API clients used to interact with the orchestration cluster and its components such as Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist, and the REST API.
Edit FEEL expressions in a deployed DMN literal expression.

a.text: Resolving incidents on running process instances is an Operate capability, not a Console feature listed on the introduction page.

c.text: The Console introduction lists managing API clients to interact with the orchestration cluster and its components (Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist, REST API) as a core capability.

d.text: Editing FEEL expressions in a DMN literal expression is done in Modeler; Console does not edit deployed decision logic.

b.text: Working on user tasks belongs to Tasklist; Console does not assign or complete user tasks.

explanation: The Console introduction enumerates cluster lifecycle and platform-level management: creating and deleting clusters, managing API clients, alerts, IP allowlists, the organization, and using the Administration API. Incidents are an Operate concern, FEEL editing is in Modeler, and user tasks are handled in Tasklist.

question: How does the Camunda 8 documentation describe the role of Camunda Console?

options.text: The management application for the included Camunda 8 products such as Modeler, Tasklist, Operate, and Optimize.
A web-based BPMN modeler for authoring and versioning process diagrams.
A monitoring and troubleshooting tool for Zeebe process instances.
A job worker runtime that executes service tasks and connectors at scale.

a.text: The Console introduction states that Camunda Console is the management application for the included products such as Modeler, Tasklist, Operate, and Optimize.

b.text: Authoring BPMN diagrams is the role of Modeler, not Console.

c.text: Monitoring and troubleshooting Zeebe process instances is the role of Operate, not Console.

d.text: Console does not execute service tasks or connectors; it manages clusters and access to them.

explanation: Console sits above the individual Camunda 8 products as the management plane. The introduction page describes it as the management application for Modeler, Tasklist, Operate, and Optimize, where you manage clusters, API clients, alerts, IP allowlists, and the organization.

question: A team needs to restrict access to a Camunda 8 cluster so that only requests from their office IP range can reach it. Which Camunda 8 component do they use, per the Console introduction?

options.text: Camunda Operate, by adding a filter on the Processes view.
Camunda Console, by managing IP allowlists for the cluster.
Camunda Modeler, by editing the cluster connection settings in the BPMN file.
Camunda Tasklist, by changing the user permissions on each task.

b.text: The Console introduction lists managing IP allowlists to restrict access to clusters as one of Console's capabilities.

a.text: Operate filters affect what the user sees in the UI; they do not restrict who can reach the cluster.

c.text: Modeler is a process authoring tool and does not control cluster network access.

d.text: Tasklist permissions control task visibility for users; they do not enforce cluster-level IP restrictions.

explanation: Restricting cluster access by IP range is a Console concern. The introduction page calls out 'Manage IP allowlists to restrict access to clusters' as one of the things you do with Camunda Console.

question: Which statement best matches how the Camunda 8 documentation introduces Tasklist?

options.text: A ready-to-use application for working on BPMN user tasks, with the option to build a fully custom application using the Orchestration Cluster API.
A modeling tool for authoring user task forms outside of BPMN.
A monitoring tool that visualizes incidents and process variables for active process instances.
A Camunda 7 compatibility layer for migrating legacy human workflows.

a.text: The Tasklist introduction describes it as a ready-to-use application for user tasks, and explicitly says you are not limited to the Tasklist UI and can extend or build custom applications using the Orchestration Cluster API.

c.text: Monitoring incidents and process variables on active instances is Operate's role, not Tasklist's.

b.text: Tasklist is not a form modeler; forms are authored in Modeler and surfaced in Tasklist.

d.text: Tasklist is a Camunda 8 application, not a Camunda 7 compatibility or migration layer.

explanation: Tasklist is positioned in the docs as the out-of-the-box UI for human workflows tied to BPMN user tasks, while leaving room for fully custom task applications built on the Orchestration Cluster API.

question: A team wants to build a fully custom web UI for BPMN user tasks instead of using Tasklist's built-in interface. Which integration path do the Tasklist docs recommend?

options.text: Build the custom application against the Orchestration Cluster API, since the docs explicitly say you are not limited to the Tasklist user interface.
Replace user tasks in the BPMN model with service tasks that call the custom UI via REST.
Fork and self-host the Tasklist UI source and inject custom React components.
Run a separate Camunda 7 engine alongside Camunda 8 to expose the task list over SOAP.

c.text: The docs do not direct users to fork the Tasklist UI; they point to the Orchestration Cluster API for custom applications.

a.text: The Tasklist introduction explicitly says you are not limited to the Tasklist user interface and can extend or build a fully custom application using the Orchestration Cluster API.

b.text: Removing user tasks from the BPMN model defeats the purpose of using user tasks; the docs do not propose this workaround.

d.text: Camunda 8 Tasklist is independent of Camunda 7; the docs do not suggest running a legacy engine to deliver tasks.

explanation: The Tasklist docs deliberately separate the out-of-the-box UI from the underlying API. Teams that want a custom UI build directly against the Orchestration Cluster API, which exposes the same user-task data Tasklist uses.

question: A BPMN process reaches a user task that gets assigned to a specific user. According to the Tasklist introduction, how does that user learn there is work for them to do?

options.text: Operate raises an incident against the user task until it is claimed and completed.
Tasklist sends a webhook to the assignee's external system on every assignment.
Zeebe pushes a gRPC stream notification directly to the user's browser.
The user is notified in Tasklist when they are assigned a task, and can then run the work required for that task.

b.text: The introduction does not describe an automatic outbound webhook on assignment; the notification happens inside Tasklist itself.

d.text: The Tasklist introduction says that as you model and run BPMN processes, users are notified in Tasklist when they are assigned a task and run the work required for the specific task.

a.text: An unclaimed user task is not an incident; Operate uses incidents for runtime errors, not for waiting work.

c.text: Zeebe does not push gRPC streams to browsers; Tasklist itself is the notification surface for assigned user tasks.

explanation: Tasklist is the notification surface for BPMN user tasks. The introduction explicitly ties the notification to assignment: when a user is assigned a task in the running BPMN process, they are notified in Tasklist and can then complete the work.

scenario: An operator started a batch retry from Operate. In the Operations panel they click the operation's ID; the operation becomes a filter on the processes list, and one process instance row in the list is now highlighted red.
question: How does the operator see the specific error message for the failed operation on that instance?

options.text: Open the Zeebe broker logs on disk and grep for the operation ID.
Re-run the batch retry; failed-operation messages are only emitted after a second attempt.
Open Console and look up the operation in the cluster's audit log.
Expand the red-highlighted process instance row in the processes list; the expanded row shows the error message tied to the selected operation.

c.text: Failed-operation details are surfaced in Operate's processes view, not in a Console audit log.

d.text: The docs state that if an operation fails the related process instance row is marked red, and the operator can expand the row to see the error message related to the selected operation.
