b.text: The docs do not require a re-run; the error message is available immediately by expanding the red row.

a.text: Operate exposes the error in the UI; pointing operators to raw Zeebe logs is not the documented workflow.

explanation: When an operation fails on an instance, Operate marks the instance row red in the filtered processes list. Expanding that row reveals the error message for the specific operation that was selected in the Operations panel, so operators investigate failures without leaving the processes view.

question: In Operate, an operator wants to filter the processes list down to only the instances affected by a specific operation they started earlier. Which workflow does the docs describe?

options.text: Manually edit the URL query string to add the operation ID parameter.
Use the global search bar in the top navigation to type the operation ID.
Go to the Processes view, click the Expand Operations icon to open the operations panel, and click the operation's ID so it is applied as a filter on the processes list.
Open Console and select the operation from the cluster's Operations tab.

c.text: The docs walk through exactly this flow: open the Processes view, expand the operations panel via the Expand Operations icon, and click an operation ID to set it as a filter on the processes instances list.

b.text: The docs do not describe a top-nav search by operation ID; the documented entry point is the operations panel on the Processes view.

a.text: Editing query strings by hand is not the documented workflow; the UI sets the filter when the operation is selected.

d.text: The operations panel for monitoring operation status lives in Operate, not in Console.

explanation: Operate centralizes operation monitoring in the Processes view. Expanding the operations panel and clicking an operation ID applies that operation as a filter on the instances list, scoping subsequent inspection to instances touched by that operation.

question: An operator opens the Operations panel on the Operate Processes view and the panel shows: "No operations have been created yet." What does this message indicate?

options.text: Operate is paused and operations cannot be listed until it is resumed from Console.
The user is missing the operate:read role and operations are hidden until it is granted.
The Zeebe exporter is misconfigured and is dropping operation events.
No operations such as canceling, retrying, migrating, or modifying process instances have been started yet for this cluster, so there is nothing for the panel to display.

c.text: The docs treat this message as an empty-state, not an error; it is not described as an exporter or configuration problem.

d.text: The docs include this exact note: if you don't have any operations the operations panel will show "No operations have been created yet." It is the empty-state for a cluster on which no operations have been performed.

b.text: The docs do not link this message to a missing role; it is shown whenever no operations exist.

a.text: There is no pause-state concept tied to this message in the docs; it simply reflects the absence of operations.

explanation: Operate uses this message as the empty-state for the Operations panel. Cancel, retry, migration, and modification all create operations that show up here; until at least one operation has been started, the panel reports that none have been created yet.

question: After an operator selects an operation in the Operations panel on the Processes view, what change appears in the process instances list?

options.text: The list becomes read-only until the selected operation finishes.
The list groups rows by BPMN flow node so operators can drill into specific elements.
The list switches to a BPMN diagram view that highlights tokens affected by the operation.
A new column named Operation State is added to the processes instances list, showing the current state of the operation for each affected instance.

c.text: Selecting an operation does not replace the list with a diagram; the list stays as a list with an added column.

d.text: The docs state that after an operation is selected, a new column named Operation State is added to the processes instances list, indicating the current state of the operation.

b.text: Selecting an operation does not regroup the list by flow node; that is not part of the documented behavior.

a.text: The list is not made read-only by selecting an operation; only the Operation State column is added.

explanation: Selecting an operation in the panel both filters the processes list and augments it with an Operation State column that surfaces the per-instance status of the selected operation, so operators can scan completion and failure at a glance.

question: After a downstream system outage, an on-call engineer needs to retry or cancel many process instances at the same time. Which Camunda 8 component does the documentation point to for this?

options.text: Camunda Console, by restarting the orchestration cluster.
Camunda Operate, which supports retrying or canceling many process instances at once.
Camunda Tasklist, by reassigning all of the affected user tasks.
Camunda Modeler, by redeploying the BPMN model to clear the affected instances.

d.text: Redeploying a model does not retry or cancel running instances; Modeler is for authoring BPMN, not bulk operations.

b.text: The Operate introduction explicitly lists 'Retry or cancel many process instances at once' as a key Operate capability for monitoring and troubleshooting.

c.text: Tasklist works on user tasks, not on bulk retry or cancel of full process instances.

a.text: Restarting a cluster from Console does not retry or cancel specific instances; that is an Operate workflow.

explanation: Operate is the monitoring and troubleshooting tool for Zeebe process instances. Among its key capabilities, the introduction calls out resolving incidents, updating variables, and retrying or canceling many process instances at once, which is exactly what bulk recovery needs.

question: Which Operate capability is described in the introduction as moving many active process instances at once so execution can continue?

options.text: Process instance modification, which is performed per single active instance.
Batch move of active process instances, which the introduction lists as letting execution continue on many instances at once.
Resolve incident, which automatically advances tokens past the failing element.
Delete finished instances, which targets instances that have already terminated.

a.text: The introduction describes process instance modification as a way to keep one active instance going, not as a bulk move across many instances.

b.text: The introduction explicitly lists 'Batch move active process instances to allow execution to continue' as a distinct Operate capability.

d.text: Deleting finished instances acts on completed instances; it does not move active ones to a new state.

c.text: Resolving an incident addresses a failure; it does not, by itself, move tokens of many instances to allow execution to continue.

explanation: Operate distinguishes single-instance modification from batch move: modification fixes one active instance, while batch move applies a move across many active instances at once so they can continue execution.

question: Which of the following is listed in the Operate introduction as a capability of Operate?

options.text: Resolving incidents on active process instances and updating process variables.
Defining BPMN element templates for connectors.
Authoring and validating DMN decision tables in a visual editor.
Issuing and rotating API client credentials for the orchestration cluster.

a.text: The Operate introduction names resolving incidents and updating process variables as key operations Operate can carry out on running process instances.

c.text: Authoring DMN decision tables is a Modeler capability; Operate runs on top of Zeebe but does not author decision logic.

d.text: Managing API clients is a Console capability, not an Operate one.

b.text: BPMN element templates for connectors are defined in Modeler, not Operate.

explanation: Operate's introduction page lists a focused set of runtime operations: resolving incidents, updating variables, bulk retry/cancel, delete finished instances and resources, modify, and batch move. Authoring artifacts and managing credentials belong to Modeler and Console respectively.

question: Under the Operate trial license described in the Availability section, what restriction applies to its use?

options.text: Operate may be used only in non-production environments, but there is no restriction on the length of the evaluation or on the available feature set.
The trial requires an active paid Camunda support contract before Operate can be downloaded.
The trial expires after a fixed 30-day evaluation period.
Only a reduced subset of Operate features is enabled during the trial.

c.text: The docs explicitly state there is no restriction on the length of the evaluation period under the trial license.

d.text: The docs explicitly state there is no restriction on the available feature set under the trial license.

a.text: The Availability section says there are no restrictions on evaluation length or feature set under the trial license, as long as Operate is used in non-production environments only. Production use is available with support in the Camunda 8 offering.

b.text: The trial license does not require a paid support contract; it is provided for non-production evaluation.

explanation: Operate's trial license is generous on time and features but strictly limited to non-production environments. Production use is covered separately by the Camunda 8 offering, which includes support.

question: A faulty gateway expression caused hundreds of active `orderProcess` instances to enter the wrong branch and pile up at `Reject order`. Which Operate feature lets an operator move all of them to `Approve order` in one operation?

options.text: Process instance batch modification, using the Move action on the selected instances.
Process instance modification, applied separately to each affected instance.
Process instance migration, mapping `Reject order` to `Approve order` in a new version.
Bulk cancellation followed by re-creating instances at the desired flow node.

a.text: The docs describe batch modification as the way to select multiple process instances and move them to the correct flow node in a single operation when execution entered the wrong branch.

b.text: Single-instance process instance modification is the alternative when only one active instance needs adjustment; it is not the single-operation move across many instances.

c.text: Migration changes the process definition version of instances; it is not the tool for moving tokens to a different flow node within the same version.

d.text: Cancelling and re-creating is not the documented approach; batch modification exists exactly so operators can move existing tokens without losing the instances.

explanation: When the wrong branch was taken or data was corrupted across many instances, Operate's process instance batch modification lets you select multiple instances and move them to the correct flow node in a single operation. Single-instance modification is for one instance and also offers extra options beyond move; migration is a separate feature that targets a different definition version.

question: After selecting instances and clicking **Move**, an operator clicks **Continue** on the information modal. Which UI change in Operate confirms that batch modification mode is now active?

options.text: The diagram is replaced by a read-only history view until the modification finishes.
A red incident overlay appears, signalling that the selected instances are paused.
A modal dialog stays open over the canvas until the operator types `MOVE` to confirm.
A blue border appears around the view, including a blue banner at the top and apply/exit buttons at the bottom.

d.text: Per the docs, entering batch modification mode is represented by a blue border, a blue banner at the top, and two buttons at the bottom for applying or exiting modifications.

a.text: The diagram is not replaced with history; the regular process instance list view remains, just framed by the blue batch-mode chrome.

c.text: There is no MOVE typing step. A confirmation modal is used only earlier to switch into batch mode, not while it is active.

b.text: Incident overlays are unrelated; batch modification mode uses blue chrome, not a red incident state indicator.

explanation: Operate visually flags batch modification mode by drawing a blue border around the working area, showing a blue banner at the top, and exposing apply/exit buttons at the bottom of the process instance list view, so operators can clearly tell that they are about to commit changes to many instances at once.

question: An operator opens the process instance list view but the **Move** action is not visible in the toolbar. According to the Operate batch modification guide, what causes **Move** to become available?

options.text: Switching to incident view so that only incident instances are listed.
Opening the Operations panel and starting a new batch operation manually.
Selecting one or more process instances in the list — the toolbar then appears with the Move action enabled.
Choosing a target version of the process definition from the filters panel.

c.text: The docs state that as instances are selected in the list view, the process instance toolbar appears and the Move action becomes available.

d.text: Picking a target version belongs to migration, not to enabling the Move toolbar action for batch modification.

a.text: Filtering by incident state is unrelated to whether the Move toolbar action is shown.

b.text: The Operations panel monitors progress after a batch modification is started, not the way it is initiated.

explanation: Operate exposes the Move action through the process instance toolbar, and the toolbar only appears once instances are selected in the list view of the chosen flow node. Without a selection, there is nothing to move and no toolbar.

question: While in batch modification mode, an operator realizes the wrong instances were selected. How can they drop the entire current selection without leaving batch modification mode?

options.text: Click **Exit** below the process instance list view.
Reload the browser tab so Operate forgets the selection.
Click **Apply Modification** without choosing a target flow node.
Click **Discard** in the process instance toolbar, or clear the current selection in the list.

d.text: The docs state that all currently selected instances can be discarded either by clicking Discard in the toolbar or by clearing the current selection — both keep the operator in batch modification mode.

a.text: Clicking Exit below the list view leaves batch modification mode entirely, which is the opposite of staying in the mode.

b.text: Reloading is not a documented control; relying on it risks losing the unsaved batch-mode context as well.

c.text: Apply Modification would attempt to commit the change, not clear the selection.

explanation: Operate documents two equivalent ways to discard a selection while staying in batch modification mode: clicking the Discard option in the process instance toolbar, or clearing the current selection. Clicking Exit, by contrast, leaves the mode altogether.

question: An operator wants to use Operate's batch modification to move several `orderProcess` instances stuck at `Check payment` to a different flow node. What is the very first step the docs prescribe?

options.text: On the process you want to modify, select the flow node that contains the instances you intend to move.
Click **Apply Modification** below the process instance list view to enter batch mode.
Deploy a new version of `orderProcess` to act as the migration target.
Open the Operations panel and pre-create a new batch operation entry.

a.text: The Operate guide explicitly starts the workflow with selecting the flow node containing the process instances you intend to move on the process you want to modify.

b.text: Apply Modification is the final commit step; it only becomes available later, once the target flow node has been chosen.

d.text: The Operations panel shows progress after a batch modification has been started; it is not how operators enter or initiate the workflow.

c.text: Deploying a new version is part of migration, a separate Operate feature; batch modification reroutes tokens within existing instances.

explanation: Batch modification in Operate is rooted at a source flow node: the operator first selects the flow node containing the instances to move, then selects instances in the list view that appears, and only after that does the Move action become available. Apply Modification, the Operations panel, and migration are all later or unrelated steps.

question: Inside Operate's batch modification mode, after the operator has selected the target flow node to move the instances to, which control commits the move?

options.text: **Continue** on the information modal at the top of the page.
**Confirm**, after typing the word `MOVE` into a confirmation box.
**Migrate**, which then opens the migration view.
**Apply Modification**, located below the process instance list view.

d.text: The docs say that once the target flow node is chosen and all intended selections are made, you click Apply Modification below the process instance list view to commit the move.

c.text: Migrate enters Operate's migration view, which targets a different process version; it is not the commit step for a batch move.

a.text: Continue on the information modal merely switches the UI into batch modification mode; it does not commit anything.

b.text: No confirmation typing step is documented for batch modification; the MIGRATE keyword belongs to migration, not batch move.

explanation: Operate's batch move workflow ends at Apply Modification. After the operator selects the target flow node and is happy with the selected instances, clicking Apply Modification below the process instance list view starts the move. Continue, Migrate, and any typed confirmation belong to other workflows.

question: After Apply Modification kicks off a batch move in Operate, where does the operator track progress and inspect how many process instances have actually been modified?

options.text: In Tasklist, under an Operations tab dedicated to batch jobs.
In the Zeebe broker log, by tailing the batch modification trace.
In the operations panel entry for that batch, by clicking its **Instances** link.
On the BPMN diagram, by hovering over each migrated token in turn.

c.text: The docs say the operator is directed to the instances list view where the batch modification progress can be monitored in the operations panel, and the Instances link on that entry reveals how many instances have been modified.

b.text: Operators are not expected to read Zeebe broker logs to monitor a batch modification; Operate provides the operations panel for that purpose.

a.text: Tasklist is a user-task UI; it does not surface Operate's batch modification operations.

d.text: There is no per-token hover counter on the diagram for batch progress; counts live in the operations panel entry.

explanation: Once a batch modification is started, Operate routes the user to the instances list view and reports progress in the operations panel. Each batch shows up as an entry there, and clicking the entry's Instances link reveals exactly how many process instances have already been modified.

question: According to Operate's batch modification docs, which element type can be used as the source flow node of a **Move** modification?

options.text: A boundary event attached to an activity.
A subprocess element itself.
A service task with running tokens.
A start event of the process.

c.text: The non-supported list excludes start events, boundary events, events attached to event-based gateways, subprocesses themselves, and elements with multiple running scopes — a plain service task is not on that list, so it is a supported source for Move.

d.text: Start events are explicitly listed under elements that do not support Move modifications.

a.text: Boundary events are explicitly listed under elements that do not support Move modifications.

b.text: Move is explicitly not possible for a subprocess itself, per the non-supported modifications section.

explanation: The docs enumerate what Move cannot operate on: start events, boundary events, events attached to event-based gateways, the subprocess element itself, and elements with multiple running scopes. Tasks like a service task with active tokens are not on that exclusion list and can therefore be moved.

question: Which process instance states qualify for Operate's process instance migration?

options.text: Any state — active, completed, and cancelled — all migrate together.
Completed instances only, so their history can be re-played on a new version.
Cancelled instances only, so they can be revived on the target version.
Running instances only — that is, instances in an active or incident state.

d.text: The docs state explicitly that it is only possible to migrate running process instances, meaning instances in an active or incident state.

b.text: Completed instances are immutable history and are not part of any migration plan.

c.text: Cancelled instances are not running and are excluded from migration.

a.text: Only running (active or incident) instances qualify; completed and cancelled are explicitly ignored.

explanation: Process instance migration in Operate operates only on running instances. The docs make this concrete: instances that are active or in an incident state can be migrated; everything else is excluded from the migration plan and silently ignored.

question: An operator reviewing the migration summary in Operate clicks **Confirm**. A text box appears. What must they type in before clicking **Confirm** again to actually start the migration?

options.text: The word `MIGRATE`.
The numeric id of the target version.
The string `CONFIRM`.
The bpmnProcessId of the source process.

a.text: The docs are explicit: after clicking Confirm to review, the operator must enter the word MIGRATE into the text box, then click Confirm again to start the migration operation.

d.text: Operate does not require the source bpmnProcessId as a confirmation token; the source process is already selected in the filters panel.

b.text: Typing the target version number is not the documented confirmation step.

c.text: The confirmation keyword is MIGRATE, not CONFIRM; the latter is only the button label.

explanation: Operate guards a migration with a typed confirmation: the operator clicks Confirm, types the word MIGRATE into the text box, and clicks Confirm again. This intentional friction prevents accidental large-scale changes when many running instances are about to be re-routed to a new process version.

question: What three areas make up Operate's migration view once the operator clicks **Migrate**?

options.text: BPMN editor canvas, DMN test tab, and a deployment log.
Source process diagram, retry policy editor, and a job history viewer.
Source process diagram (top left), target process diagram (top right), and the flow node mapping panel (bottom).
Tasklist panel, broker metrics, and the FEEL playground.

c.text: The docs describe the migration view as having three areas: the source process diagram (top left), the target process diagram (top right), and the flow node mapping (bottom panel).

b.text: There is no retry policy editor or job history viewer in the migration view; migration is about mapping flow nodes between two versions.

d.text: Tasklist, broker metrics, and FEEL playground are entirely different tools, not parts of Operate's migration view.

a.text: BPMN editing happens in Modeler, not in Operate's migration view.

explanation: Operate splits the migration view into three coordinated panes: the source process diagram on the top left, the target process diagram on the top right, and the flow node mapping panel underneath. The mapping panel is where the operator pairs source flow nodes with their target counterparts.

question: An operator selects a mix of `orderProcess` instances in Operate for migration to a new version. Which subset of that selection will be silently excluded from the migration plan?

options.text: Instances currently in an incident state, because incidents must be resolved first.
Instances still actively waiting at user tasks for human input.
Instances of the latest deployed version of `orderProcess`.
Completed and canceled instances — only active or incident instances are part of the plan.

d.text: The docs say it is only possible to migrate running process instances (active or incident state); all other process instances will not be part of the migration plan and will be ignored.

a.text: Incident instances are explicitly considered running and are included; they do not need to be resolved before migration.

b.text: Active instances waiting at user tasks are still running and are part of the migration plan.

c.text: There is no rule that excludes instances of the latest version; eligibility is by run state, not by version recency.

explanation: Operate keeps migration safe by considering only running instances — those in an active or incident state. Anything completed or cancelled is treated as immutable history, dropped from the migration plan, and silently ignored, even if the operator selected it.

question: While mapping source flow nodes in Operate's migration view, the dropdown for a particular source element offers no matching target flow nodes from the target process. What is the most likely reason according to the docs?

options.text: The element belongs to a different BPMN namespace than the rest of the process.
The element's id was changed between versions, so Operate cannot link them.
The target process version is older than the source, so backward mappings are hidden.
Migration of that element type is not currently supported by Zeebe, so the mapping is not offered.

d.text: The docs note that it is currently only possible to map elements with migration supported by Zeebe, which is why some source elements have no usable target in the dropdown.

a.text: BPMN namespace differences are not the documented filter; Zeebe support is.

b.text: Operate maps flow nodes the operator picks, not by id matching; an id change alone does not hide the dropdown.

c.text: There is no documented rule that hides options based on whether the target version is older; eligibility is gated by Zeebe support for the element type.

explanation: Operate's migration view filters the mapping dropdowns to flow node types that Zeebe currently supports for migration. When an element does not yet have migration support in Zeebe, the dropdown simply does not let the operator choose a target for it, regardless of ids or version order.

question: An operator wants to migrate running instances of `orderProcess` from version 1 to version 2 in Operate. What must they choose in the filters panel before clicking **Migrate**?

options.text: Only the target process version — Operate infers the source from the current selection.
An incident filter so that only broker-side errors are migrated.
A retry policy that governs how migrated jobs are re-executed.
The source process (`orderProcess`) and the specific source version (version 1) of those instances.

d.text: The docs say to select a specific process and version from the filters panel, which becomes the source process version that instances are migrated from.

a.text: Selecting only a target version is not the first step; the source process and source version come first, in the filters panel.

c.text: Operate's migration view does not configure retry policies; that is unrelated to picking the source filter.

b.text: An incident-only filter is not required; migration applies to running instances, which includes both active and incident states.

explanation: Operate's migration starts in the filters panel by pinning the source: pick the process (`orderProcess`) and the version (1) whose running instances you intend to migrate. Only then do you select instances and click Migrate to open the migration view, where the target process and version are chosen.

question: An operator is preparing a process instance migration in Operate for `orderProcess`. Which of the following statements about that migration is NOT correct?

options.text: Completed and canceled instances in the selection are also included in the migration plan.
Before the migration starts, the operator must type the word `MIGRATE` into the confirmation text box.
Only running instances (active or in an incident state) are part of the migration plan.
The migration view shows a source process diagram, a target process diagram, and a flow node mapping panel.

a.text: This is the false statement: the docs say completed and canceled instances are not part of the migration plan and are ignored, even if they appear in the selection.

c.text: This is true — only running instances, meaning active or incident, are migrated.

b.text: This is true — Operate requires typing MIGRATE in the confirmation box before clicking Confirm again to start the migration.

d.text: This is true — the migration view is composed of the source diagram, the target diagram, and the flow node mapping panel.

explanation: The only false statement is the claim that completed and canceled instances are included; the docs make clear that the migration plan covers only running instances (active or incident). The other three options restate documented behavior about the migration view layout and the MIGRATE typed confirmation.

question: A single process instance is stuck on one service task and the operator wants execution to continue at a downstream task in that same instance. Which Operate capability is the documented fit?

options.text: Process instance modification on that single instance to move the token to the downstream task.
Redeploy the BPMN with a higher version and migrate just that instance forward.
Roll the instance back to the previously completed activity using Operate's history view.
Process instance batch modification, because it is the canonical way to recover a stuck instance.

d.text: Batch modification is for cases where many instances ended up in the wrong branch and you want to fix them together, not for a single stuck instance.

c.text: Operate cannot roll an instance back to a previously completed activity; history is read-only.

a.text: Process instance modification on the single instance is what the docs prescribe when execution is stuck and you want to skip or repeat activities for that instance.

b.text: Migration changes the process definition version of running instances; it is not how a stuck instance resumes at a different activity.

explanation: Per the Operate modification docs, you modify an active instance to allow execution to continue, for example to skip or repeat activities. For one stuck instance use process instance modification; for the same fix across many instances in the wrong branch, use process instance batch modification instead.

question: How does an operator enter modification mode on a process instance in Operate, and what visible UI changes confirm it?

options.text: Open the Tasklist entry for the instance; a Modify drawer slides out from the side.
Click the modify icon in the process instance header; a blue banner appears at the top and apply / discard buttons appear at the bottom.
Click the migrate icon; modification controls appear in the variables panel.
Right-click the running flow node in the diagram and choose Modify; the diagram is locked until the operator commits.

a.text: Tasklist is for user tasks and does not provide a Modify drawer; modification mode is not entered from there.

b.text: The docs describe clicking the modify icon in the process instance header to enter modification mode, after which a blue banner appears at the top and two buttons (apply, discard) appear at the bottom.

d.text: Operate does not expose a right-click `Modify` action on flow nodes for entering modification mode.

c.text: The migrate icon enters the separate process instance migration feature, not modification mode.

explanation: Modification mode is entered via the modify icon on the process instance header. The blue banner at the top and the apply / discard buttons in the footer are the documented visual confirmation that modification mode is active.

question: In modification mode, the operator wants to cancel every running token on the `Send notification` service task in a single staged modification. Which exact action does this?

options.text: Select `Send notification` in the diagram, then click `Cancel instance` from the dropdown.
Apply the modifications first, then remove each token individually from the instance history view.
Click `Apply Modifications` in the footer and choose a `Cancel All Tokens` shortcut from the modal.
Right-click each running token in the instance history and choose `Terminate token`.

b.text: Cancel modifications are staged before being applied; applying first and then removing tokens is not the documented workflow.

a.text: The docs prescribe selecting the flow node and clicking `Cancel instance` from the dropdown to cancel all running tokens on that flow node as a pending modification.

c.text: `Apply Modifications` opens the summary modal but offers no `Cancel All Tokens` shortcut there.

d.text: Operate has no documented `Terminate token` right-click action in the instance history.

explanation: Selecting the flow node in the diagram and choosing `Cancel instance` from the dropdown stages a single pending modification that cancels every running token on that node, which is then visible in the instance history until the operator applies the batch.

question: The operator needs to spawn a new token at `Validate order` in a running instance without disturbing the token currently waiting at `Receive payment`. Which modification does this?

options.text: Cancel the instance and recreate it; the new instance will begin at `Validate order`.
Select `Validate order` in the diagram, then click `Add` from the dropdown.
Choose `Move instance` on `Receive payment` and pick `Validate order` as the target.
Edit the BPMN diagram in Operate to add another incoming sequence flow into `Validate order`.

c.text: Move cancels the source token while adding at the target; the scenario requires keeping the token at `Receive payment` active.

d.text: BPMN authoring is done in Modeler; Operate does not let you edit the diagram to add tokens at runtime.

a.text: Cancelling the instance destroys all existing state; that is not how `Add token` works.

b.text: Selecting the target flow node and clicking `Add` from the dropdown stages an `Add token` modification at that node without touching other tokens — exactly what the docs describe.

explanation: An `Add token` modification stages a new token at the selected flow node and leaves existing tokens untouched, in contrast to `Move`, which cancels the source token and adds at the target. The action is initiated by selecting the flow node and choosing `Add` from the dropdown.

question: According to the Operate modification docs, what is a `Move instance` modification on a flow node equivalent to?

options.text: A combined `Cancel` of the source flow node's tokens plus `Add` of new tokens at the target flow node.
Editing the process definition so the source flow node's outgoing sequence flow now points to the target.
Cancelling the entire process instance and starting a brand-new instance that begins at the target flow node.
Rolling the instance back so the source flow node becomes ACTIVE and the target becomes COMPLETED.

a.text: The docs explicitly state that the move operation is equivalent to the combination of `Cancel` and `Add` modifications, achievable in a single move step.

d.text: Modification cannot roll history backwards; completed nodes stay completed.

c.text: Move never terminates the whole instance — it relocates tokens within the existing instance.

b.text: Modifications change a running instance's state, never the deployed process definition.

explanation: Per the docs, the move operation on a flow node combines a `Cancel` of all running tokens on the source with an `Add` of new tokens on the target, packaged as one modification. The operator can therefore do in one step what would otherwise require two separate modifications.

question: Which statement about adding a variable to a new scope during Operate's modification mode is FALSE?

options.text: The operator first selects the new scope from the instance history, then clicks `Add Variable` in the variables panel.
Adding variables to new scopes is only possible during modification mode and only on scopes that were newly generated by the modification.
Once valid Name and Value fields are filled and the operator blurs out of the field, the variable is added to pending modifications.
The new variable is written to live process state immediately on add, before the operator clicks `Apply Modifications`.

a.text: True — the docs describe selecting the new scope from the instance history before clicking `Add Variable`.

c.text: True — blurring out of the last edited field with valid values is exactly what adds the variable to pending modifications.

d.text: False — the variable is added to pending modifications, not live state. It only takes effect once the operator applies the modifications.

b.text: True — the docs scope this feature to new scopes generated during modification mode.

explanation: Adding a variable to a new scope during modification mode is staged as a pending modification; the variable is not part of the live process state until the operator applies the modifications batch. The other three statements correctly describe the documented workflow.

question: Which sequence correctly adds a variable to a newly generated scope during modification mode in Operate?

options.text: Apply the pending modifications first, then open the new scope and add the variable via the variables panel.
Right-click the new scope in the diagram, choose `Inject Variable`, and the variable is committed at once.
Set the variable on the parent process scope; child scopes inherit it automatically once modifications are applied.
Select the new scope in the instance history, click `Add Variable` in the variables panel, fill in Name and Value, then blur out of the last edited field.

b.text: The docs describe no right-click `Inject Variable` action, and changes are pending, not committed on the spot.

d.text: Selecting the new scope in the instance history, clicking `Add Variable`, entering Name and Value, then blurring out is the documented four-step sequence that adds the variable to pending modifications.

a.text: Applying ends modification mode; the docs explicitly add variables while still in modification mode.

c.text: Operate does not document automatic inheritance of variables from parent to child scopes for this feature.

explanation: Adding a variable to a new scope is a four-step flow in modification mode: select the new scope in the instance history, click `Add Variable` in the variables panel, fill Name and Value, blur out of the field. With valid values, the new variable lands in pending modifications.

question: An existing scope already holds the variable `orderValue`, and the operator wants to correct its value through Operate's modification mode. Which steps queue an `Edit Variable` modification?

options.text: Open the variable in the JSON / Diff viewer and click `Replace Value` to apply the change immediately.
Select the existing scope in the instance history, edit the value in the variables panel, then blur out of the field with a valid value.
Cancel the running token on the flow node first, then edit the value on the new scope created by the cancel.
Delete the existing variable on the scope, then add it again with the new value as a pending modification.

b.text: The docs say to select the existing scope, edit the variable value in the variables panel, then blur out with a valid value — that queues the `Edit Variable` modification.

a.text: The JSON / Diff viewer is for inspecting an added or edited variable in the summary modal, not for committing edits directly.

d.text: Operate has a dedicated edit flow for existing variables; a delete-and-readd workflow is not documented.

c.text: Cancelling the token is unrelated; editing existing variables on an existing scope does not require cancelling anything.

explanation: Editing an existing variable on an existing scope is done in-place via the variables panel: select the scope in instance history, change the value, blur out with a valid value. The change is queued as an `Edit Variable` pending modification until the operator applies the batch.

question: Within Operate's pending-modifications summary modal, which action is available to the operator?

options.text: Save the current set of pending modifications as a reusable template for other instances.
Reorder pending modifications by drag and drop to control the apply sequence.
Delete an individual pending modification via its delete icon, or view an added / edited variable in a JSON / Diff viewer.
Schedule the modifications to apply at a future timestamp instead of immediately.

b.text: The modal does not offer drag-and-drop reordering of pending modifications.

d.text: Apply takes effect on click; the modal does not support deferred scheduling.

c.text: The docs list deleting a pending modification with the delete icon and viewing an added or edited variable in a JSON / Diff viewer as available actions in the summary modal.

a.text: Templates of pending modifications are not a documented feature of the summary modal.

explanation: The summary modal opened via `Apply Modifications` lets the operator delete a pending modification, view an added or edited variable in a JSON / Diff viewer, cancel and stay in modification mode, or apply the modifications and exit modification mode.

question: After staging several pending modifications, the operator clicks `Apply` in the summary modal and the modification operation is created successfully. What happens next?

options.text: The modifications are queued and only applied once the next milestone in the instance completes.
The instance pauses until the operator acknowledges each modification individually in a side panel.
A success notification is shown and the changes are reflected on the instance in a short time.
The instance is restarted from the start event with the pending modifications replayed in order.

d.text: Applying modifications does not restart the instance; existing state is preserved aside from the modifications themselves.

c.text: The docs describe a success notification on the successful apply, followed by the changes being reflected on the instance in a short time.

b.text: There is no per-modification acknowledgement step after Apply in Operate.

a.text: There is no documented dependency on a future milestone before modifications take effect.

explanation: When `Apply` succeeds, Operate shows a success notification and the staged modifications are reflected on the instance shortly afterwards. The apply itself ends modification mode and dispatches the modification operation.

question: A flow node nested in a multi-instance body currently has 800 running tokens. The operator chooses `Cancel instance` on that flow node in modification mode. How many tokens does a single such modification cancel?

options.text: All 800 — Operate cancels every running token on the selected flow node in one operation.
100 — Operate processes tokens in fixed chunks of one hundred per apply.
Zero — modifications on multi-instance bodies are unsupported.
500 — the per-modification cap is 500 tokens; repeat the modification on the same flow node to handle the rest.

a.text: The docs state a technical limit of 500 affected tokens per cancel or move on a single multi-instance-nested flow node.

c.text: Modifications on multi-instance bodies are supported, subject to the 500-token-per-operation cap.

d.text: Up to 500 tokens are affected per cancel / move modification; the docs recommend repeating the modification on the same flow node until all tokens are handled.

b.text: There is no documented 100-token chunking; the documented cap is 500.

explanation: For cancel or move on a flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body, the docs cap the number of affected tokens at 500 per modification in this version. The operator can repeat the modification on the same flow node until all tokens are handled, or fall back to the Orchestration Cluster API with a custom filter.

question: When more than 500 tokens must be canceled or moved on a single flow node nested in a multi-instance body, which approach does the Operate documentation recommend?

options.text: Increase the per-operation token limit in Operate's settings, then re-issue the same modification.
Cancel the entire process instance and recreate it with execution starting at the target flow node.
Modify individual element instances via the instance history, or use the Orchestration Cluster API with a custom filter.
Schedule the modification at off-peak hours so Operate processes more tokens per apply.

a.text: The 500-token cap is a technical limit in this version, not a tunable Operate setting.

d.text: Scheduling has no effect on the per-modification token cap.

b.text: Cancelling and recreating destroys instance state and is not the recommended workaround.

c.text: The docs point to single-instance modification (select specific element instances in the instance history) or the Orchestration Cluster API with a custom filter as the recommended way to go beyond 500 tokens.

explanation: Beyond the 500-token-per-modification cap, the docs recommend either single-instance modification by selecting individual element instances in the instance history, or using the Orchestration Cluster API (`modify-process-instance`) with a custom filter for more granular targeting.

question: A multi-instance body has 1200 running tokens on one nested flow node. Which statement about a single `Cancel instance` modification on that flow node is FALSE?

options.text: At most 500 of the 1200 tokens are affected by one modification because of a technical limit in this version.
All 1200 tokens are canceled in a single apply because Operate enforces no per-modification token cap.
The operator can repeat the modification on the same flow node until all 1200 tokens have been handled.
The Orchestration Cluster API can be used with a custom filter as an alternative path for the remaining tokens.

a.text: True — 500 is the documented per-operation cap for cancel / move on a flow node nested in a multi-instance body.

c.text: True — the docs recommend repeating the modification on the same flow node until all tokens are handled.

d.text: True — the docs explicitly call out the Orchestration Cluster API with a custom filter as an alternative.

b.text: False — Operate caps affected tokens at 500 per cancel / move modification in this version, so a single apply cannot handle all 1200.

explanation: The 500-token cap on cancel / move modifications on a multi-instance-nested flow node is the key technical limit operators must work around, either by repeating the same modification on the flow node or by using the Orchestration Cluster API with a custom filter.

question: An operator is using modification mode to stage `Add token` and `Move tokens to` actions on various elements of a running instance. Which statement about non-supported modifications is FALSE?

options.text: `Add token` and `Move tokens to` are not possible on a start event.
`Add token` is not possible on a regular service task that has a single running scope.
`Move tokens from` is not possible on a subprocess itself.
`Add token` and `Move tokens to` are not possible on a boundary event.

a.text: True — the docs list start events as elements that block `Add token` / `Move tokens to`.

d.text: True — boundary events are explicitly called out as blocking `Add token` / `Move tokens to`.

c.text: True — `Move tokens from` is explicitly blocked on a subprocess itself per the docs.

b.text: False — a service task with a single running scope is a normal `Add token` target. The block applies to elements with multiple running scopes, not single-scope ones.

explanation: Operate blocks `Add token` and `Move tokens to` on start events, boundary events, events attached to event-based gateways, and elements with multiple running scopes; it also blocks `Move tokens from` on a subprocess itself. A regular service task with a single running scope is not in that blocked list.

question: An `order-process.bpmn` instance was created via `zbctl` with the payload below. The XOR gateway in the model requires an integer `orderValue` for the routing evaluation to happen properly. Per the Operate guide on resolving incidents and updating variables, what should the operator do?

options.text: Update `orderValue` to an integer value in Operate, then resolve the incident on the XOR gateway.
Cancel the instance — incidents from gateway evaluation cannot be recovered without redeploying.
Redeploy `order-process.bpmn` with the gateway condition rewritten to compare strings so the existing value matches.
Re-run the same `zbctl` command; Zeebe coerces string numbers to integers on the second attempt.

b.text: Cancellation is unnecessary — this entire guide exists because the recovery path is updating the variable in Operate, not destroying the instance.

c.text: Rewriting the BPMN to accept the wrong-typed payload is the opposite of what the guide teaches; the guide fixes the variable to match the model.

a.text: The page teaches exactly this flow: when `orderValue` was set as a string but the XOR gateway needs an integer, update the variable in Operate and resolve the resulting incident.

d.text: Zeebe does not silently coerce string numbers to integers for FEEL comparisons; re-running zbctl with the same payload reproduces the same problem.

explanation: The Operate guide walks through the case where an `order-process` instance is created with `orderValue` as the string `"99"` even though the XOR gateway requires an integer for proper evaluation. The fix is to update the variable to the correct type in Operate and resolve the resulting incident, not to cancel, redeploy, or simply re-create the instance.

question: In the Operate guide's `order-process.bpmn` example, why does supplying `orderValue` as the string `"99"` cause a problem at the XOR gateway?

options.text: The variable name must be `orderId`, not `orderValue`, for the XOR gateway to evaluate.
The model requires `orderValue` as an integer for the XOR condition; comparing a string to an integer fails.
XOR gateways accept only Boolean variables; numeric types are not evaluated at all.
`zbctl` always serialises variables as strings, so the gateway never receives integers regardless of input.

b.text: The guide says the `order-process.bpmn` model required `orderValue` as an integer so the XOR gateway evaluation routes the instance; passing a string causes a type comparison failure.

a.text: `orderId` is used as the correlation identifier later in the flow, not as the value the XOR gateway compares against.

d.text: `zbctl` passes the JSON types as supplied. Quoting `99` makes it a string, but an unquoted `99` would be an integer.

c.text: XOR gateways evaluate FEEL expressions on many types, including numerics. The issue here is type mismatch, not a numeric-vs-Boolean restriction.

explanation: The guide's `order-process.bpmn` example needs `orderValue` so the XOR gateway can route the instance. When the value is supplied as a string but the gateway condition expects an integer, the comparison cannot evaluate and the engine raises an incident on the gateway.

question: An instance of `order-process.bpmn` is created with the `zbctl` command shown. According to the guide, what happens when this instance reaches the XOR gateway that branches on `orderValue`?

options.text: `zbctl` rejects the create command up front because `orderValue` is not an integer.
The default sequence flow out of the XOR fires silently, so no incident is reported.
The instance routes to the high-value branch because `"99"` coerces to a number above zero.
An incident is raised because the gateway cannot compare a string `orderValue` to an integer threshold.

d.text: The guide describes exactly this case: `orderValue` is present but set as a string, so the XOR gateway cannot compare it to the integer threshold and the engine raises an incident.

c.text: FEEL does not silently coerce strings to numbers for `>=` comparisons; the comparison fails with a type error rather than matching a branch.

a.text: `zbctl` accepts the JSON payload as written and creates the instance; the failure surfaces later when the gateway evaluates.

b.text: A failed gateway evaluation creates an incident on the gateway; the engine does not silently take a default flow when the condition fails to evaluate.

explanation: The guide demonstrates that creating an instance with `orderValue` as a string causes the XOR gateway condition `orderValue>=100` to fail with a type-comparison error, producing an incident that an operator must resolve before the instance can progress.

question: According to the guide, which variable must every instance of `order-process.bpmn` carry so the XOR gateway evaluation routes the instance properly?

options.text: `orderValue`
`orderId`, which the gateway reads directly
A Boolean `paymentReceived` flag set at instance creation
A correlation key matching the deployment name `order-process`

a.text: The guide states explicitly that every process instance created for `order-process.bpmn` requires an `orderValue` so the XOR gateway evaluation happens properly.

b.text: `orderId` is the correlation identifier for the message catch event later in the flow, not the variable the XOR gateway evaluates.

c.text: The guide does not require a Boolean `paymentReceived` at creation; the payment-received state is signalled later by a published message.

d.text: Correlation keys are used for message correlation; they are not what the XOR gateway evaluates to route the instance.

explanation: The guide is explicit: every `order-process.bpmn` instance requires an `orderValue` so the XOR gateway can route the token. Without that variable (or with the wrong type), the gateway condition cannot evaluate and an incident is raised.

question: Which statement is NOT true about the steps the guide uses to advance the example instance to its XOR gateway with `zbctl`?

options.text: The `payment-received` message is published with `--correlationKey="1234"`.
The `payment-received` message is published with `--correlationKey="99"`, matching the `orderValue` value.
Once the message is correlated, Operate shows the instance has an incident that must be fixed before it progresses.
A worker for the `Initiate Payment` task is registered with `zbctl create worker initiate-payment`.

b.text: False — the guide uses `--correlationKey="1234"`, not `"99"`. The value `99` is the `orderValue` the gateway compares, not the correlation key.

d.text: True — the guide registers an `initiate-payment` worker with `zbctl --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat`.

a.text: True — the guide explicitly publishes the message with `--correlationKey="1234"`.

c.text: True — the guide says you should observe an incident in Operate after these commands, which must be fixed before the instance can progress.

explanation: The guide uses `--correlationKey="1234"` (option c) and registers the `initiate-payment` worker (option b); once the message correlates, Operate shows an incident on the gateway (option d). Option a is false because it confuses `orderValue` (`99`, the gateway input) with the correlation key.

question: After running the `zbctl` command shown, which BPMN element in the guide's `order-process` model receives the published message?

options.text: A `Payment Received` message start event that begins a new instance.
The `Initiate Payment` service task waiting on its worker.
The `Payment Received` intermediate message catch event.
The XOR gateway, which evaluates `orderValue>=100`.

c.text: The guide states that the published `payment-received` message is correlated with the running instance so it can advance past the `Payment Received` intermediate message catch event.

a.text: A message start event would create a new instance; here the message is correlated to an existing running instance via its correlation key, so it goes to an intermediate catch event.

b.text: `Initiate Payment` is a job-worker task completed by `zbctl create worker initiate-payment`, not by a message correlation.

d.text: The XOR gateway evaluates a FEEL condition on `orderValue`; it does not subscribe to messages.

explanation: Per the guide, `--correlationKey="1234"` correlates the `payment-received` message to a running instance and advances its token past the intermediate message catch event named `Payment Received`. Message start events create new instances; the XOR gateway is a separate downstream element.

question: After the `initiate-payment` worker is registered and the `payment-received` message is correlated, which statement about the instance's state in Operate is FALSE?

options.text: The instance shows an incident that must be fixed before it can progress.
The instance has completed successfully because the XOR gateway routed it to `Ship Without Insurance`.
The `payment-received` message has been correlated using the key `1234`.
The `Initiate Payment` job has been completed by the registered worker.

b.text: False — the guide explicitly says you will observe an incident on the instance after these steps; the instance has not completed and is blocked at the XOR gateway.

a.text: True — the guide states you should now observe the process instance has an incident, which means there is a problem with process execution that must be fixed before the instance can progress.

d.text: True — the worker registered with `zbctl create worker initiate-payment --handler cat` activates and completes the `Initiate Payment` job before the message is published.

c.text: True — the message is published with `--correlationKey="1234"` and correlates to the instance created with `orderId: "1234"`.

explanation: After both `zbctl` commands the instance is stuck at the XOR gateway with an incident, not completed. The worker completes `Initiate Payment`, the message correlates with key `1234`, and Operate displays the incident exactly as the guide describes.

question: The `initiate-payment` worker completes its job and `payment-received` is correlated to the instance with key `1234`. Why does Operate then display an incident rather than letting the instance progress?

options.text: The `initiate-payment` worker exhausted its retries running the `cat` handler.
Two tokens arrived at the XOR gateway, violating its exclusive-merge semantics.
The `payment-received` message was rejected because no subscription was active at the time of publish.
The XOR gateway downstream of the message catch event cannot evaluate `orderValue>=100` because `orderValue` was created as a string.

d.text: The instance was created with `orderValue` as the string `"99"`. After the token advances past the message catch event, it reaches the XOR gateway, which raises an incident because it cannot compare a string to an integer threshold.

c.text: The message correlates successfully — the catch event's subscription is active once the token reaches it; the incident appears at the XOR gateway, not on the correlation.

a.text: The `cat` handler simply echoes the payload and completes the job; the worker does not fail or exhaust retries in this example.

b.text: Only one token is moving through the flow; XOR gateways evaluate one token at a time and do not raise incidents from merge semantics here.

explanation: Once the message is correlated, the token reaches the XOR gateway, which evaluates `orderValue>=100`. Because `orderValue` was supplied as a string (`"99"`) at creation, the gateway condition fails with a type-comparison error and Operate surfaces the failure as an incident on the gateway.

question: Which statement about the guide's procedure for advancing the example instance to its XOR gateway is NOT correct?

options.text: Once the `payment-received` message is published, the instance bypasses the `Initiate Payment` task automatically.
Completing `Initiate Payment` requires a registered worker that activates and completes the job.
The correlated message advances the token past the `Payment Received` intermediate message catch event.
The published `payment-received` message must correlate with the running instance via its correlation key.

a.text: False — the message catch event sits downstream of `Initiate Payment`; correlating a message does not bypass an upstream service task. The guide registers a worker to complete `Initiate Payment` before publishing the message.

b.text: True — the guide creates a worker with `zbctl create worker initiate-payment --handler cat` to complete the `Initiate Payment` job.

d.text: True — `zbctl publish message ... --correlationKey="1234"` correlates the message to the instance whose correlation value is `1234`.

c.text: True — the guide states the published message is correlated with the instance so it can advance past the `Payment Received` intermediate message catch event.

explanation: Publishing a message correlates to a waiting subscription; it does not skip an upstream service task. The guide therefore first registers the `initiate-payment` worker to complete that task, then publishes `payment-received` with `correlationKey="1234"` to advance the token past the intermediate catch event toward the XOR gateway.

question: An Operate incident on the example instance shows the error message in the stem. Per the guide, what action resolves it?

options.text: Cancel the instance and recreate it with a corrected payload from `zbctl`.
Edit `orderValue` in Operate to remove the quotation marks so its value becomes an integer.
Click Retry first; Operate auto-coerces the variable type before re-evaluating the gateway.
Redeploy the BPMN model with `orderValue` declared as a String input.

b.text: The guide says to click the edit icon next to `orderValue`, remove the quotation marks (so `"99"` becomes `99`), then save with the checkmark — turning the string into an integer.

a.text: The guide resolves the incident in place by editing the variable; it does not cancel and recreate the instance.

d.text: The model already expects an integer at the XOR gateway; declaring `orderValue` as a String would not fix the type-mismatch condition.

c.text: Operate does not auto-coerce variable types on retry. The guide requires editing the variable first, then initiating a retry.

explanation: Per the guide, the resolution is to edit `orderValue` in Operate by clicking the edit icon, removing the quotation marks so the value becomes an integer, and saving with the checkmark. Operate does not coerce types automatically, and the variable can be fixed in place on a running instance.

codeBlocks.stem: Expected to evaluate condition 'orderValue>=100' successfully, but failed because: Cannot compare values of different types: STRING and INTEGER

question: After editing `orderValue` in Operate from `"99"` to `99` and saving the change, what final step does the guide require to actually move the instance past the XOR gateway?

options.text: Restart the Zeebe broker so the blocked job is unlocked and re-activated.
Save the variable change; Operate re-evaluates the gateway on its next polling cycle.
Initiate a retry of the process instance from the top right corner of the page or below Operations.
Republish the `payment-received` message so the gateway re-evaluates.

c.text: The guide states the last step is to initiate a retry of the process instance, and notes two places where this can be done: in the top right corner of the page and below Operations.

d.text: The message catch event was already satisfied earlier; re-publishing the message is not what re-evaluates the failing gateway.

b.text: Operate does not retry failed evaluations automatically on a polling cycle; the operator must trigger the retry.

a.text: Restarting the broker is not part of the resolution flow described in the guide.

explanation: Per the guide, editing the variable alone is not enough — the operator must initiate a retry of the process instance to re-evaluate the failing element. The retry control is available in the top right of the instance page and below the Operations panel.

question: Which of the following is NOT a way the guide describes for resolving a variable-related incident in Operate?

options.text: Modify the FEEL condition on the XOR gateway from within Operate.
Edit an existing variable's value and save the change with the checkmark icon.
Initiate a retry of the process instance after fixing the variable.
Add a variable to the instance if one is missing altogether.

a.text: False — Operate does not let you edit BPMN model expressions like a gateway condition. Model changes are made in Modeler and redeployed; Operate fixes runtime state.

b.text: True — the guide walks through clicking the edit icon, changing the value, and clicking the checkmark to save.

d.text: True — the guide explicitly notes you can also add a variable if it is missing from a process instance altogether.

c.text: True — the guide's final step is initiating a retry of the process instance from the page header or the Operations panel.

explanation: Operate supports editing variable values, adding missing variables, and retrying instances — but it does not let operators rewrite BPMN model expressions like gateway conditions. Model edits belong in Modeler.

question: According to the guide, where on the process instance page can a user initiate a retry of an incident-blocked instance in Operate?

options.text: Via `zbctl --insecure resolve incident`; the Operate UI has no retry control.
Only via the global Operations menu on the Operate dashboard.
From the Modeler deployment dialog, after redeploying the process.
In the top right corner of the page, and below the Operations panel.

d.text: The guide states there are two places on the process instance page where you can initiate a retry: in the top right corner of the page and below Operations.

b.text: Retry is initiated on the process instance page itself, not from a global dashboard menu.

c.text: Modeler is for editing and deploying models; instance-level retry is an Operate action on the running instance.

a.text: The guide describes resolving the incident through the Operate UI, not via a CLI command.

explanation: The guide is explicit that the retry control appears in two places on the process instance page: the top right corner and below the Operations section. Both trigger a re-evaluation of the failing element after the variable has been fixed.

question: An operator inspects an incident in Operate and finds that the variable required to evaluate a downstream element is missing from the instance entirely, not merely typed wrong. Per the guide, how should this be resolved?

options.text: Wait for the configured retry interval; Zeebe re-injects unmapped variables from input mappings automatically.
Cancel and recreate the instance with a complete payload; Operate cannot add variables to a running instance.
Edit the BPMN file in Modeler to back-fill a default for the variable, then redeploy the process.
Add the missing variable to the running instance in Operate, then initiate a retry of the process instance.

d.text: The guide notes that as well as editing a variable, you can also add a variable if one is missing from a process instance altogether — and the final step is still to initiate a retry of the instance.

b.text: Cancel-and-recreate is unnecessary; the guide explicitly supports adding a missing variable to a running instance from Operate.

c.text: A model-level default would apply to new instances, not the already-running instance whose incident must be resolved now.

a.text: Zeebe does not automatically re-derive missing variables; incidents on missing variables wait for an operator action plus a retry.

explanation: Per the guide, Operate's variable-modification flow handles both editing existing variables and adding missing ones. In either case the final step is to initiate a retry of the instance so the failing element re-evaluates with the corrected state.

question: After the variable incident is resolved and the instance advances to `Ship Without Insurance`, which `zbctl` invocation does the guide use to complete the process instance?

options.text: Complete a single job by job key, without registering a worker.
Publish a `ship-without-insurance` message with the instance's correlation key.
Resolve an incident on `ship-without-insurance` from the CLI.
Register a job worker for `ship-without-insurance` with a `cat` handler.

d.text: The guide shows `./bin/zbctl --insecure create worker ship-without-insurance --handler cat`, which registers a worker that activates and completes the job, allowing the instance to finish.

a.text: `zbctl complete job` exists but is not what the guide uses; the guide demonstrates the end-to-end flow by registering a worker.

b.text: `Ship Without Insurance` is a job-worker task, not a message catch event; publishing a message would not complete it.

c.text: There is no incident on `Ship Without Insurance` at this point; the earlier gateway incident has already been resolved.

explanation: The guide completes the example instance by registering a worker for the `ship-without-insurance` job type with a simple `cat` handler. The handler echoes the job payload to satisfy the worker contract, the job completes, and the token reaches the end event.

question: After the variable incident is fixed and the instance is retried, the token reaches the `Ship Without Insurance` task but the instance does not finish. Per the guide, why?

options.text: `Ship Without Insurance` is a job-worker task; until a worker is registered for that job type, no worker activates and completes the job.
A boundary timer must elapse before the engine completes the service task.
Operate must be configured with a 'complete-on-arrival' rule for terminal tasks.
`zbctl resolve incident` is needed because every job-worker task starts in an error state.

a.text: The guide completes the instance by creating a worker for `ship-without-insurance` with `zbctl --insecure create worker ship-without-insurance --handler cat`. Until such a worker activates and completes the job, the token stays parked at the task.

c.text: Operate has no 'complete-on-arrival' rule; job-worker tasks are completed by registered workers.

b.text: A service task is not gated by a timer; the engine waits for a worker to activate and complete the job.

d.text: There is no incident on `Ship Without Insurance` here; the earlier gateway incident was already resolved, and job-worker tasks do not start in error states.

explanation: Job-worker tasks in Zeebe are completed by external workers. Until a worker is registered for the `ship-without-insurance` job type, the job sits waiting. The guide demonstrates this by creating the worker with `zbctl create worker ship-without-insurance --handler cat`, after which the instance completes.

question: The underlying issue that caused incidents on many process instances has been resolved, but the affected instances remain stuck. What should an operator do in Operate to unstick them all at once?

options.text: Wait for the engine to auto-retry incidents after the default backoff window.
Redeploy the BPMN model with a new version so incidents are dropped.
Create a selection of the affected instances and apply a batch retry.
Cancel each instance and start fresh ones manually from Tasklist.

c.text: Operate supports batch operations on a selection of process instances: tick the boxes for the affected instances and choose the retry operation. This is exactly the docs' example of a microservice outage being resolved.

d.text: Tasklist starts new instances and completes user tasks; it does not retry stuck instances after their incident's root cause is fixed.

b.text: Redeploying creates a new process definition version but does not resolve incidents on already-running instances.

a.text: Incidents in Camunda 8 do not auto-resolve; an operator must explicitly retry the affected jobs or instances.

explanation: When many instances share the same incident root cause and that cause is later resolved, the instances stay stuck until they are retried. Operate's selection-and-batch-retry workflow is the docs-recommended path: check the boxes for the affected instances, pick the retry operation, and watch progress in the Operations panel.

question: In Camunda Operate's batch-operations workflow, what is a `selection`?

options.text: A saved filter on the Operate dashboard, scoped to the current user.
A collection of process definitions bundled together for deployment.
A subset of BPMN elements highlighted in the diagram view of a single instance.
A set of process instances on which you can carry out a batch retry or batch cancellation.

d.text: The docs define a selection as a set of process instances on which you can carry out a batch retry or batch cancellation, created by ticking the boxes next to the instances you want.

a.text: Operate's filters narrow what is shown in the list, but the docs reserve the term 'selection' for the batched set of instances acted on together.

c.text: A selection in this context is about whole process instances, not BPMN elements inside one instance's diagram.

b.text: Deploying process definitions is a Modeler / API concern; Operate selections are a runtime construct over running instances.

explanation: A selection in Operate is the set of process instances you check off in the list so you can apply a batch operation to them. The docs name two such operations explicitly: batch retry and batch cancellation. The workflow is: tick the boxes, pick the operation, confirm, then watch the Operations panel.

question: After confirming a batch retry on a selection in Operate, where can an operator see the current status of all operations?

options.text: A toast notification that disappears after a few seconds with no permanent record.
The Zeebe broker's stdout, which is the only place batch progress is logged.
The Operations panel.
The Incidents tab of any one process instance in the selection.

c.text: The docs state that after confirmation you see the Operations panel with the current status of all operations.

d.text: The Incidents tab shows incidents for a single instance and does not aggregate batch-operation status across the selection.

a.text: Operate persists batch progress in the Operations panel, not in a transient toast notification.

b.text: Broker logs are an implementation detail; the user-facing status surface is the Operations panel in Operate.

explanation: Operate's batch workflow ends by surfacing progress in the Operations panel. From there an operator can confirm whether each retry or cancellation in the selection succeeded, without needing to inspect individual instances or broker logs.

question: A user task is deployed without a candidate user or group assignment in the BPMN. How can a user start working on it from Tasklist, and what if they change their mind?

options.text: The task is invisible to every user until an Operate operator runs a migration.
Self-assign the task from the queue; if they no longer intend to work on it, they can unassign it.
Tasklist auto-assigns the task to the first user who opens the Tasks page that day.
Only the BPMN author can claim it by re-editing the model in Modeler and redeploying with an assignee.

b.text: The docs state that user tasks can be automatically assigned in the BPMN process or self-assigned from Tasklist, and the user can unassign the task if they do not intend to work on it.

a.text: Unassigned tasks are visible in the Tasks page so users can pick them up; they are not hidden until an operator intervenes.

d.text: Assignment happens at runtime through Tasklist or the API; redeploying the model is not required to claim a task.

c.text: Tasklist does not silently auto-assign on first open; users must self-assign explicitly when the BPMN didn't assign anyone.

explanation: Tasklist supports two assignment paths: automatic assignment defined in the BPMN process (to a user or group) and self-assignment by the user from the Tasks page. A self-assigned task can also be unassigned if the user later decides not to work on it, putting it back into the queue for someone else.

question: Which of the following is shown directly on each task tile in the Tasklist Tasks queue preview?

options.text: The Zeebe partition ID that owns the process instance.
Due date.
The hostname of the worker that last polled the job.
The full BPMN XML of the user task element.

b.text: The docs list the attributes shown on each queue tile and Due date is one of them, alongside task name, process name, optional context description, assignee, priority, creation date, and follow-up date.

d.text: BPMN XML is a Modeler/deployment artefact and is not surfaced on the Tasks queue tile.

c.text: Worker hostnames are an integration concern and do not appear on the queue tile.

a.text: Zeebe partition IDs are an internal broker detail not exposed in the Tasklist queue preview.

explanation: The Tasks queue tile previews a fixed set of attributes: task name, process name, optional context description, assignee, priority, creation date, due date, and follow-up date. Internal infrastructure details such as BPMN XML, worker hostnames, or Zeebe partition IDs are not part of this preview.

question: A new Tasklist user opens the Tasks page and asks where to find task filters, the task queue, and the details of the currently selected task. How are these laid out on the page?

options.text: Filters and the queue of tasks are on the left side; details of the selected task are on the right.
The queue is on the left, and task details and filters share a stacked panel on the right.
Filters sit in a top horizontal bar, with the queue and details splitting the bottom area evenly.
Details are on the left and the queue is on the right, mirroring Operate's instances view.

a.text: The docs state that on the left side of the page you can see task filters and the queue of tasks, while details of the selected task are displayed on the right.

c.text: There is no horizontal-bar filter strip with a split lower region; filters live alongside the queue on the left.

b.text: Filters and the queue share the left side; the right side is reserved for the selected task's details.

d.text: This inverts the actual layout; Tasklist puts the queue on the left and the details on the right.

explanation: Tasklist's Tasks page uses a two-column layout: filters and the queue of available tasks are on the left, and details of the task currently selected from the queue are shown on the right. This lets the user keep scrolling the queue while inspecting one task at a time.
