question: A user opens a task in Tasklist whose definition has no associated Camunda Form. What does the task detail page show in place of the form, and can they still complete the task?

options.text: It displays the task variables, and the user can still complete the task.
It redirects to Operate to set variables there before the task can be completed.
It shows an empty page with a disabled Complete button until a form is deployed.
It shows the raw BPMN XML of the user task element for inspection only.

a.text: The docs say that if the task doesn't have a form, Tasklist will display task variables, and the task page is still the place a user reviews and completes the task.

c.text: Tasklist does not disable completion just because the task has no form; the variables view replaces the form.

d.text: Tasklist does not show BPMN XML on the task detail page; it shows variables when there is no form.

b.text: Setting variables and completing a task without a form is done in Tasklist itself, not by jumping to Operate.

explanation: Task completion in Tasklist usually happens via a Camunda Form, but when no form is attached, the same detail page falls back to showing the task variables. The user can review those variables and still complete the task from Tasklist without leaving for Operate or Modeler.

question: From a task's detail page in Tasklist, what does switching to the `Process` tab display?

options.text: A visual representation of the BPMN diagram the task is part of.
A flat list of every process definition deployed in the cluster.
Operate's instance-modification panel scoped to the parent process instance.
The deployment YAML of the process application as packaged for Zeebe.

a.text: The docs say the Process tab provides a visual representation of the BPMN diagram the task is part of, helping users see what happened earlier and what's coming next.

d.text: Tasklist's Process tab is a BPMN diagram preview, not a YAML deployment descriptor.

c.text: Modifying tokens or variables on a running instance is an Operate feature; Tasklist's Process tab only visualises the diagram.

b.text: The Process tab is scoped to the task's own process definition, not a directory of every deployed definition.

explanation: Tasklist's Process tab on the task detail page renders the BPMN diagram of the parent process. The intent is to help the assignee understand how this individual user task fits into the larger workflow — what activities came before and what comes after — without leaving Tasklist for Operate or Modeler.

question: Using the ordering control in the Tasklist Tasks queue, which attribute can a user arrange tasks by?

options.text: The process definition version number of the parent process.
Priority.
The candidate group name in alphabetical order.
The number of variables attached to each task.

b.text: The docs list the supported sort keys explicitly: date of creation, due date, follow-up date, or priority. Priority defines a task's urgency relative to others.

d.text: Variable count is not one of the four sort keys the ordering control offers.

c.text: Candidate group is not listed among the ordering options; the supported keys are creation, due, follow-up, and priority.

a.text: The version number of the parent process definition is not a Tasklist ordering option.

explanation: Clicking the order icon in the Tasks queue lets a user arrange tasks by date of creation, due date, follow-up date, or priority. The follow-up date is the latest time to start a task, the due date is the deadline to finish it, and priority signals relative urgency.

question: Camunda 8 frames process automation around three aspects: Design, Automate, and Improve. A team already uses Modeler, Tasklist, and Operate, and now wants to analyse bottlenecks in their running processes. Which component primarily supports the `Improve` aspect?

options.text: Modeler.
Operate.
Optimize.
Tasklist.

c.text: The docs explicitly position Optimize beyond Design and Automate as the component that leverages process data to analyse areas for improvement, including bottlenecks.

d.text: Tasklist is for reviewing and completing user tasks, which is part of the Automate aspect, not Improve.

a.text: Modeler is where process flows are designed; it covers the Design aspect rather than continuous Improve.

b.text: Operate is for viewing and analysing individual process instances at runtime, not for the longitudinal improvement analytics Optimize provides.

explanation: Camunda 8 splits process automation into Design, Automate, and Improve. Modeler covers Design; Tasklist and Operate together cover Automate. Optimize is the dedicated Improve component, offering business intelligence over historical process data so stakeholders can spot bottlenecks and target areas for improvement.

question: Who is Camunda Optimize primarily geared toward, according to the docs?

options.text: Business stakeholders at Camunda enterprise customers, who collaboratively access reports and share process intelligence.
Zeebe broker operators tuning JVM heap and partition counts.
BPMN modelers authoring new processes in Desktop Modeler.
End users who claim and complete user tasks in Tasklist.

a.text: The docs say Optimize is geared toward business stakeholders and offers business intelligence tooling for Camunda enterprise customers, who collaboratively access reports and share process intelligence.

b.text: Broker operations and JVM tuning belong to platform administration, not to Optimize's business intelligence audience.

c.text: Modelers author processes in Modeler; Optimize is not aimed at design-time users.

d.text: Task workers use Tasklist; Optimize is not aimed at the people completing individual tasks.

explanation: Optimize is the Improve-side component for business stakeholders at Camunda enterprise customers. It provides BI over process execution data so they can collaboratively analyse bottlenecks, share reports and dashboards, and find concrete areas to improve in business processes.

question: A platform team is worried that enabling Optimize will slow down running process instances. According to the docs, how does Optimize collect process data, and where does it store it?

options.text: It makes REST API calls to the Camunda server, collects new historical data, and stores it in its own Elasticsearch database.
It tails the broker's stdout logs and parses each log line for process events.
It plugs directly into the Zeebe partition state and writes results back to the broker's data store.
It scrapes the Operate web UI on a schedule with a headless browser to extract metrics.

a.text: The docs say Optimize makes REST API calls to the Camunda server, collecting new historical data and storing it in its Elasticsearch database, so users can analyse without affecting runtime.

c.text: Optimize does not write into Zeebe's partition state; it consumes data via REST and stores it in its own database.

d.text: Optimize ingests via REST, not by scraping another product's UI.

b.text: Log scraping is not Optimize's data-collection mechanism; it pulls via the documented REST API.

explanation: Optimize is designed to leave runtime performance alone. As instances execute, it polls the Camunda server over REST for new historical data and stores that data in its own Elasticsearch database. Reports and dashboards then read from Elasticsearch, so analysis happens independently from the live engine.

question: A business analyst wants to see, overlaid on the BPMN diagram, where instances spend the most time so they can target a bottleneck. Which Optimize feature do they reach for?

options.text: Heatmaps for instance durations on the process diagram.
Tasklist's Tasks queue ordering by priority.
Operate's process-instance modification panel.
The Zeebe broker's built-in Prometheus metrics endpoint.

a.text: The docs invite users to review heatmaps for instance durations and visualise activity instances compared to total process instances inside Optimize.

c.text: Operate's modification panel adds or cancels tokens on a single running instance; it is not a duration heatmap.

b.text: Tasklist ordering surfaces individual tasks to a worker; it does not visualise instance durations on the diagram.

d.text: Broker metrics describe infrastructure health, not per-activity instance durations on a BPMN diagram.

explanation: Optimize's process performance dashboards include heatmaps that colour BPMN elements by metrics such as instance duration. Overlaying durations directly on the diagram is how analysts spot which activity is the bottleneck and where to focus improvement work.

question: Beyond traditional business intelligence tooling, what additional capability does Camunda Optimize provide?

options.text: It guides users toward continuous process improvement by understanding their goals, facilitating rapid identification of constraints in their system.
It hosts an in-browser IDE for editing BPMN, DMN, and Form files alongside dashboards.
It performs token-level modification of running process instances, the same as Operate.
It grants direct write access to Zeebe state so analysts can compensate completed instances.

a.text: The docs say Optimize goes beyond traditional BI by guiding users toward continuous process improvement based on their goals and facilitating rapid identification of constraints within an organisation's system.

d.text: Optimize is an analytical layer over historical data; it does not mutate Zeebe state or compensate instances.

b.text: Authoring BPMN, DMN, and Forms is Modeler's job, not Optimize's.

c.text: Token-level modification of running instances is an Operate feature; Optimize is for analysis, not runtime intervention.

explanation: Optimize is positioned as more than a generic BI tool. By understanding the user's improvement goals it points them at the constraints holding their processes back, complementing the historical reports and dashboards. Runtime intervention stays in Operate, while design stays in Modeler.

question: Which operation can be performed in Camunda Operate's process-instance modification feature?

options.text: Change the process definition version of a running instance in place.
Add a new token to any flow node in the diagram.
Roll a completed instance back to a previous state.
Edit the BPMN model and redeploy it from within Operate.

b.text: Process-instance modification in Operate lets an operator add a token to any flow node, cancel a specific token, or set variables on a running instance.

d.text: BPMN editing is in Modeler, not Operate.

c.text: Operate cannot roll back a completed instance; history is immutable.

a.text: Changing the process definition version is a separate Operate feature called migration, not modification.

explanation: Operate's process-instance modification adds and cancels tokens and updates variables on a running instance. Editing the BPMN model is a Modeler task, migration (changing a definition version) is a separate Operate feature, and history is immutable.

question: A modeler deploys an ad-hoc sub-process containing a start event, two service tasks `Task A` and `Task B`, and an end event. Which constraint does this model violate?

options.text: Ad-hoc sub-processes must use a parallel gateway after the start event to activate inner elements.
Ad-hoc sub-processes must contain only service tasks; user and other task types are not allowed.
Ad-hoc sub-processes must not contain start events or end events; only activities (and optional sequence flows between them) are allowed.
Ad-hoc sub-processes must have exactly one outgoing sequence flow from their start event.

d.text: An ad-hoc sub-process is not allowed to have a start event in the first place, so a rule about its outgoing sequence flow does not apply.

a.text: There is no rule that an ad-hoc sub-process must begin with a parallel gateway. Inner elements are activated externally, not by token flow from a start event.

c.text: The docs list two constraints: an ad-hoc sub-process must have at least one activity, and it must not have start events or end events. Inner elements are not connected to start or end events and can be executed multiple times, in any order, or skipped.

b.text: No task-type restriction is documented for ad-hoc sub-processes. The constraint is about start and end events, not about which kinds of activities may appear.

explanation: Ad-hoc sub-processes are a special kind of embedded subprocess marked with the `~` tilde. Their inner elements are not connected to start or end events; each element can be executed multiple times, in any order, or skipped. The two documented constraints are that the sub-process must contain at least one activity, and that it must not contain start events or end events.

question: An ad-hoc sub-process is handled internally by Zeebe with `completionCondition` set as shown and `cancelRemainingInstances` set to `false`. Five inner activities have been activated; the third one completes and the condition evaluates to `true`. What happens to the two activities still running?

options.text: They keep running, and the ad-hoc sub-process completes only after both remaining instances finish.
They are terminated immediately and the ad-hoc sub-process completes right away.
They are paused until a future inner-element completion re-evaluates `completionCondition`.
An incident is raised because `cancelRemainingInstances` must be `true` when a `completionCondition` is defined.

b.text: Immediate termination of remaining instances is the behavior when `cancelRemainingInstances` is `true` (the default), not when it is set to `false`.

a.text: When `cancelRemainingInstances` is `false`, the ad-hoc sub-process waits for the completion of all active instances before completing, even after the `completionCondition` has been met.

d.text: Both `true` and `false` are valid settings for `cancelRemainingInstances`; setting it to `false` does not cause an incident.

c.text: There is no pause-and-re-evaluate behavior. Once the condition is true, the sub-process either cancels the remaining instances or waits for them, depending on `cancelRemainingInstances`.

explanation: An ad-hoc sub-process evaluates its `completionCondition` after every inner element completes. When the condition becomes true, the `cancelRemainingInstances` attribute decides what happens to still-active inner instances: `true` (the default) terminates them and completes the sub-process immediately; `false` waits for all active instances to complete before the sub-process completes.

question: An ad-hoc sub-process is handled by a job worker. The worker tries to complete its current job with an `adHocSubProcess` job result that both activates new inner elements and signals that the `completionCondition` is fulfilled. What does the docs say about this?

options.text: Zeebe activates the new elements first, then completes the ad-hoc sub-process once they finish.
Both operations succeed and the activated elements run as fire-and-forget after the sub-process completes.
The `completionCondition` always takes precedence, so the newly activated elements are silently dropped.
The job worker cannot fulfill the completion condition and activate new elements at the same time; these must be separate job completions.

a.text: There is no documented behavior where Zeebe runs the activations first and then completes. The two actions are mutually exclusive in a single job result.

c.text: The docs do not describe silently dropping activations; they state the worker simply cannot combine the two actions in one job completion.

b.text: An ad-hoc sub-process cannot have inner elements running after it completes — the sub-process either cancels remaining instances or waits for them. Fire-and-forget post-completion is not supported.

d.text: Per the docs: the job worker can activate more elements or fulfill the completion condition, but it cannot fulfill both the completion condition and activate new elements at the same time.

explanation: In a job-worker-handled ad-hoc sub-process, the worker receives a job and decides the next step: activate one or more inner elements, or fulfill the completion condition. These are mutually exclusive in a single job completion. There is only one active ad-hoc sub-process job at any time, and the job may be recreated during execution; completing it may also result in a `NOT_FOUND` rejection.

question: An ad-hoc sub-process defines `outputCollection` `results` and `outputElement` `= result`. Where is the `results` variable visible after the ad-hoc sub-process completes?

options.text: It is created in the root process scope from the start, before any inner flow runs.
It is created as a local variable of the ad-hoc sub-process and is propagated to the parent scope when the sub-process completes.
It is only readable by the next service task immediately downstream and disappears after that task completes.
It exists only inside the ad-hoc sub-process and is discarded when the sub-process completes.

d.text: While `results` does start as a local variable of the ad-hoc sub-process, it is not discarded on completion — it is propagated to the parent scope through normal variable propagation.

b.text: The docs state that `outputCollection` is created as a local variable of the ad-hoc sub-process and updated whenever an inner flow completes, then propagated to the parent scope when the sub-process completes.

a.text: The variable is not created in the parent or root scope up front; it lives as a local of the ad-hoc sub-process and is only propagated outward at completion.

c.text: Variable propagation is not limited to the next downstream task. Once propagated to the parent scope, `results` follows normal scope-visibility rules and persists in that scope.

explanation: An ad-hoc sub-process can collect inner-flow outputs by defining `outputCollection` (the collected list's variable name) and `outputElement` (the FEEL expression evaluated per inner-flow completion). The `outputCollection` variable is created as a local variable of the sub-process, updated as inner flows complete, and propagated to the parent scope on completion.

question: A job worker reads the `adHocSubProcessElements` variable to decide which inner element of an ad-hoc sub-process to activate. Which structure does each entry in this list provide?

options.text: `id`, `name`, `lane`, and `assignee` — the user-facing assignment fields.
Only the BPMN XML fragment for each activatable element, as a single string per entry.
`elementId`, `elementType`, `taskDefinitionType`, and `retries`.
`elementId`, `elementName`, `documentation`, `properties`, and `parameters` defined via the `fromAi` FEEL function.

c.text: `taskDefinitionType` and `retries` are not part of the `adHocSubProcessElements` entry schema. The entries describe activatable inner elements, not job-worker task definitions.

a.text: User-task assignment fields like `lane` or `assignee` are not in the entry structure. The variable is about activatable inner elements in general, not specifically user tasks.

d.text: Per the docs, each entry includes `elementId`, `elementName`, `documentation`, `properties`, and `parameters` (the latter being parameters defined using the `fromAi` FEEL built-in function).

b.text: The variable provides structured metadata fields, not raw BPMN XML. Workers consume named fields to decide which elements to activate.

explanation: When an ad-hoc sub-process is activated, Zeebe creates a special `adHocSubProcessElements` variable in its scope containing a list of activatable inner elements. Each entry exposes `elementId`, `elementName`, `documentation`, `properties`, and `parameters` (declared via the `fromAi` FEEL function). The docs explicitly warn that this variable must not be updated by application code.

question: A modeler wants to use a transactional subprocess in a Camunda 8.8 process. According to the BPMN coverage page's Subprocesses section, what is the status of this element?

options.text: Transactional subprocesses are listed in the Subprocesses section but are not implemented in Camunda 8.8.
Transactional subprocesses are implemented and behave like an embedded subprocess with an automatic compensation handler attached to each inner task.
Transactional subprocesses are implemented but require a connector to be deployed for the transaction boundary.
Transactional subprocesses are implemented and use the ad-hoc marker to coordinate commit and rollback.

a.text: The BPMN coverage page lists transactional subprocesses among the subprocess types but, unlike embedded, call activity, event subprocess, and ad-hoc subprocess, it does not carry the `implemented` marker.

b.text: There is no automatic compensation behavior provided by transactional subprocesses in Camunda 8.8 — the symbol is shown for reference but not executed.

c.text: Connectors are not used to implement transactional subprocess semantics; the element itself is not supported.

d.text: The ad-hoc marker is a separate, supported feature for ad-hoc sub-processes. It is not Camunda's implementation of transactional subprocesses.

explanation: The Subprocesses section of the BPMN coverage page enumerates five subprocess types: embedded subprocess, call activity, event subprocess, transactional subprocess, and ad-hoc subprocess. Only embedded, call activity, event subprocess, and ad-hoc subprocess are marked as implemented in Camunda 8.8. Transactional subprocess is shown without the `implemented` marker, meaning the engine does not execute it.

question: A team is choosing between using a `business rule task`, a `script task`, and an `undefined task` in a Camunda 8.8 process. According to the Tasks section of the BPMN coverage page, which statement is correct?

options.text: Only the script task is supported in Camunda 8.8; business rule task and undefined task were removed from coverage.
All three task types — business rule task, script task, and undefined task — are listed as implemented in Camunda 8.8.
Only the business rule task is supported; script tasks and undefined tasks are display-only symbols.
None of these task types is supported; they are documented but not executable by Zeebe.

c.text: Script tasks and undefined tasks are both shown with the `implemented` marker in the Tasks section, alongside the business rule task.

b.text: The Tasks section of the BPMN coverage page shows service, user, receive, send, business rule, script, manual, and undefined tasks all as implemented in Camunda 8.8. The only task variant in the section that is not marked as implemented is the receive-task-instantiated variant.

a.text: Business rule tasks and undefined tasks remain part of the supported task set; they are not removed from coverage.

d.text: All three task types are explicitly marked as implemented in the BPMN coverage page, so they can be executed.

explanation: The Tasks section of the BPMN coverage page lists each task symbol with an `implemented` CSS class when the engine supports it. Service, user, receive, send, business rule, script, manual, and undefined tasks are all marked as implemented in Camunda 8.8. The receive-task-instantiated variant is the only task in the section shown without the implemented marker.

question: A modeler wants to use a `complex gateway` in a Camunda 8.8 process. According to the Gateways section of the BPMN coverage page, what is its implementation status?

options.text: Complex gateways are implemented and behave like an inclusive gateway whose join condition is configurable via FEEL.
Complex gateways are listed in the Gateways section but are not implemented in Camunda 8.8.
Complex gateways are implemented and execute as event-based gateways at runtime.
Complex gateways are implemented but require a job worker to evaluate the gateway condition externally.

a.text: Complex gateways do not have configurable join semantics in Camunda 8.8; the symbol is shown in the coverage page without the `implemented` marker.

d.text: No job worker mechanism for complex gateways exists; the engine does not execute them at all.

c.text: Complex gateways and event-based gateways are listed as separate symbols. The event-based gateway is implemented; the complex gateway is not.

b.text: The Gateways section shows exclusive, parallel, event-based, and inclusive gateways with the `implemented` marker. The complex gateway is listed but does not have the marker, so it is not implemented.

explanation: The Gateways section of the BPMN coverage page lists five gateway types. Exclusive, parallel, event-based, and inclusive gateways are all marked as implemented in Camunda 8.8. The complex gateway is shown only for reference and is not executable by the engine.

question: A modeler wants to attach the standard `loop` marker to a service task in Camunda 8.8 so the task repeats. According to the Markers section of the BPMN coverage page, what is the loop marker's status?

options.text: The loop marker is implemented and repeats the task until a `loopCondition` evaluates to `false`.
The loop marker is implemented and behaves exactly like a sequential multi-instance marker.
The loop marker is listed in the Markers section but is not implemented in Camunda 8.8.
The loop marker is implemented under the hood by attaching the ad-hoc marker to the task.

a.text: There is no `loopCondition` semantics in Camunda 8.8 for the standard loop marker; the symbol is shown in the coverage page but without the `implemented` marker.

b.text: Sequential multi-instance and the loop marker are listed as separate symbols. Sequential multi-instance is implemented; the loop marker is not.

c.text: The Markers section shows multi-instance parallel, multi-instance sequential, compensation, and ad-hoc markers as implemented. The loop marker is the only one in that section that is not marked as implemented.

d.text: The ad-hoc marker is a separate, implemented marker for ad-hoc sub-processes. It does not substitute for an unsupported loop marker.

explanation: The Markers section of the BPMN coverage page lists five markers: multi-instance parallel, multi-instance sequential, loop, compensation, and ad-hoc. Multi-instance (both variants), compensation, and ad-hoc are marked as implemented in Camunda 8.8. The standard loop marker is shown for reference but is not implemented; modelers needing repetition typically use a multi-instance activity instead.

question: A modeler wants to use a throwing timer intermediate event in Camunda 8.8 to insert a `PT30M` delay in the middle of a process. According to the Events section of the BPMN coverage page, is this supported?

options.text: No — the Events coverage table for the Timer row does not include a Throw or End variant; only Start (normal and event-subprocess interrupting/non-interrupting), Catch, and Boundary (interrupting/non-interrupting) variants are implemented.
Yes — the timer throw event is implemented and equivalent to a generic `Pause` activity.
Yes — the timer throw event is implemented and is configured with an ISO 8601 cycle like `R1/PT30M`.
No — timer events of any kind are not supported in Camunda 8.8; only message events can drive time-based waits.

a.text: The Timer row in the events coverage table shows implemented cells only for the Start, Event Subprocess (interrupting and non-interrupting), Catch, and Boundary (interrupting and non-interrupting) columns. The Throw and End cells for the Timer row are empty, meaning these variants are not implemented.

b.text: There is no generic `Pause` activity in BPMN, and the timer throw event is not implemented in Camunda 8.8.

c.text: ISO 8601 cycle expressions like `R1/PT30M` are used by implemented timer variants (start, catch, boundary), not by a throw timer event — which does not exist as an implemented element.

d.text: Timer events are supported as start, catch, and boundary variants (interrupting and non-interrupting). The only gap is throw and end variants.

explanation: The Events section of the BPMN coverage page is a matrix of event types versus positions (Start, Event Subprocess interrupting/non-interrupting, Catch, Boundary interrupting/non-interrupting, Throw, End). For the Timer row, only Start, Event Subprocess variants, Catch, and Boundary variants are marked as implemented. The Throw and End cells are empty, so a throwing timer intermediate event or a timer end event cannot be used to delay flow in Camunda 8.8. Delays are typically modelled with a timer intermediate catch event.

question: A modeler wants to attach a non-interrupting error boundary event to a service task in Camunda 8.8 so the task can continue while a handler runs in parallel. According to the Events section of the BPMN coverage page, is this supported?

options.text: No — error boundary events are not supported in Camunda 8.8 at all; teams must use a regular boundary timer to detect errors.
Yes — every error event variant (start, catch, boundary interrupting and non-interrupting, throw) is implemented for the Error row.
No — in the Error row of the events coverage table, only the interrupting variants (event subprocess, boundary, and end) are implemented; the non-interrupting boundary column is empty.
Yes — non-interrupting error boundary events are the only error variant supported; interrupting error boundary events are not implemented.

d.text: The relationship is reversed: the interrupting error boundary event is implemented; the non-interrupting one is not.

c.text: The Error row of the events coverage table shows implemented cells only for the Event Subprocess (interrupting) column, the Boundary (interrupting) column, and the End column. The Start, Event Subprocess non-interrupting, Catch, Boundary non-interrupting, and Throw cells for Error are empty.

b.text: The Error row is sparse: most cells are empty. Start, Catch, non-interrupting variants, and Throw are all unimplemented for errors.

a.text: Interrupting error boundary events are supported. A timer boundary is not the appropriate substitute for catching a business error.

explanation: In the BPMN coverage events table, the Error row only marks three cells as implemented: the interrupting event subprocess start event, the interrupting boundary event, and the end event. Non-interrupting error boundary events are not supported in Camunda 8.8; error handling on a still-running activity is not available. To react to an error you must either let the boundary event interrupt the activity or model the recoverable flow differently.

question: A developer argues that a Camunda 8.8 BPMN process diagram is just a picture and that the actual runtime semantics live in a separate file shipped alongside it. Based on the BPMN primer, what is the correct view?

options.text: The BPMN diagram is a binary `.bpmn` file, and the executable steps are defined in a separate `.zeebe` companion file that the engine reads at deployment.
A BPMN process is a single XML document with a visual representation; the XML contains all the information needed by workflow engines such as Zeebe, and the diagram is one view of the same artifact.
The diagram is a PNG image embedded inside the BPMN file, and Zeebe parses the pixels at runtime to reconstruct the flow.
The diagram and the XML are stored separately and only the diagram is required at runtime; the XML is for documentation only.

b.text: The primer states that a BPMN process is an XML document that has a visual representation. The XML contains all the information needed for engines and modeling tools, while the diagram contains just enough information to be quickly understood by humans — they are two views of the same source artifact.

a.text: BPMN is XML, not a binary format, and there is no separate `.zeebe` companion file. Zeebe-specific properties are added inside the BPMN XML via the `zeebe:` namespace.

c.text: The diagram is not stored as pixels; the BPMN XML includes `bpmndi` diagram-interchange elements that describe shape positions vector-style.

d.text: The XML is the executable source of truth; the diagram alone cannot drive the engine. The primer specifically calls the dual XML/diagram nature the source of BPMN's power.

explanation: BPMN 2.0 is an industry standard for process modeling and execution. A BPMN process is a single XML document with a visual representation. The XML carries every detail the engine and modeling tools need, and the diagram conveys enough for non-technical stakeholders to understand the flow. The primer calls this duality the reason BPMN works as source code and documentation in one artifact.

question: A non-technical stakeholder asks why Camunda 8.8 uses BPMN 2.0 rather than an in-house diagram format. Which statement best reflects the BPMN primer's framing of the notation's purpose?

options.text: BPMN 2.0 is an industry standard for both process modeling and execution, with a visual representation intended to be understood by non-technical stakeholders as well.
BPMN 2.0 is only a documentation notation; it cannot be executed by a workflow engine and is shown to stakeholders for review only.
BPMN 2.0 is a binary serialization format optimized for engine throughput rather than human readability; non-technical reviewers see a separate rendered report.
BPMN 2.0 is a Camunda-only diagram format that requires a developer to interpret each symbol on behalf of business users.

d.text: BPMN 2.0 is an open industry standard, not a Camunda-only format, and the primer specifically emphasizes that the diagram is understandable by non-technical people.

b.text: The primer states BPMN is for both modeling and execution; Zeebe interprets the BPMN XML directly. It is not documentation-only.

a.text: The primer opens by describing BPMN 2.0 as an industry standard for process modeling and execution, and stresses that the visual representation is meant to be understood quickly by humans, including non-technical people.

c.text: BPMN is XML, a human-readable text format. It is not a binary format, and no separate rendered report substitutes for the diagram.

explanation: BPMN 2.0 is an industry standard for both process modeling and execution. The primer highlights the duality of BPMN: a single XML artifact carries the executable definition while the visual diagram conveys enough meaning for non-technical stakeholders to read and reason about the process. This is why the same BPMN file serves as source code and documentation.

question: An interrupting event is attached to the border of a subprocess in a Camunda 8.8 process. The subprocess currently has three active inner activities. The boundary event triggers. According to the BPMN primer, what happens?

options.text: The boundary event is queued and only fires after every active inner activity reaches a completion or wait state.
Only the inner activity closest to the boundary event is interrupted; the other two continue to completion.
The boundary event has no effect on the subprocess itself; it only routes the token after the subprocess completes normally.
The subprocess is interrupted regardless of which inner element is currently active; all of its active inner activities are torn down.

b.text: The primer states the interruption is at the level of the subprocess as a whole. Selective interruption of one inner activity is not how events on a subprocess border behave.

a.text: There is no queueing of the event behind inner activity completion. The whole point of an event on a subprocess border is that it can interrupt regardless of the current inner state.

c.text: An event attached to a subprocess border is part of the interruption mechanism, not a post-completion router. Routing after normal completion is what outgoing sequence flows do.

d.text: Per the primer: when the event is triggered, the subprocess is interrupted, regardless of which of its elements is currently active. All active inner work in that subprocess is ended.

explanation: Subprocesses are element containers that allow defining common functionality, including the ability to attach an event to their border. The primer explicitly states that when such an event is triggered, the subprocess is interrupted regardless of which inner element is currently active. The trigger applies to the subprocess as a whole, not to a single inner activity.

question: A process instance's token reaches a parallel gateway with three outgoing sequence flows. According to the BPMN primer, what happens at the gateway?

options.text: The parallel gateway generates new tokens by activating all three outgoing sequence flows in parallel.
The token waits at the gateway until all three downstream activities have already completed; only then does it pass.
The token is consumed and only the first downstream activity that registers a job worker is executed.
The gateway chooses one of the three outgoing sequence flows based on process data, the same way an exclusive gateway does.

d.text: Choosing one outgoing flow based on data is the behavior of an exclusive gateway, not a parallel gateway. Parallel gateways do not evaluate data conditions for routing.

a.text: The primer states that the parallel gateway generates new tokens by activating multiple sequence flows in parallel. Each outgoing flow advances independently from that point.

b.text: Parallel gateways do not wait for downstream completion before forwarding the token; the join behavior on the receiving side is what waits, not the splitting side.

c.text: There is no behavior in which the gateway picks the first worker to register. All outgoing flows are activated; each one independently triggers its downstream elements.

explanation: Gateways route tokens in more complex patterns than plain sequence flow. The primer contrasts two gateway behaviors: an exclusive gateway chooses one outgoing sequence flow based on data, while a parallel gateway generates new tokens by activating multiple outgoing sequence flows in parallel. A three-way parallel split therefore produces three concurrent tokens.

question: A process instance enters a business rule task and the internal DMN decision engine fails to evaluate the decision. What happens at the task?

options.text: The task completes and propagates a `null` result variable so the process can continue.
An incident is raised at the business rule task; when the incident is resolved, the decision is evaluated again.
The process instance is automatically cancelled with an error end event.
The token routes via a default outgoing sequence flow modelled on the task.

b.text: An unsuccessful decision evaluation raises an incident at the business rule task. When the incident is resolved, the engine re-evaluates the decision.

a.text: Failed evaluation does not silently complete the task with a null result; it raises an incident so the cause can be addressed.

c.text: An evaluation failure does not trigger an error end event automatically. It surfaces as an incident on the task itself.

d.text: Business rule tasks do not branch on a default flow when evaluation fails; the failure becomes an incident.

explanation: When the DMN decision engine cannot evaluate the decision at a business rule task, the engine raises an incident on the task. Once the incident is resolved (for example, after fixing the data or the DMN), the decision is evaluated again from that point.

question: A business rule task is configured with the extension element shown and no `bindingType` attribute. Which version of the called decision is evaluated when the task is activated?

options.text: The version that was deployed together with the currently running version of the process.
The latest deployed version of the decision at the moment the business rule task is activated.
The version annotated with the version tag `latest`.
An incident is raised because `bindingType` is a required attribute.

b.text: If the `bindingType` attribute is not specified, `latest` is used as the default — the latest deployed version at the moment the task is activated.

a.text: That is the behaviour of `bindingType="deployment"`, which must be set explicitly. Without `bindingType`, `latest` is used.

c.text: `versionTag` matches a tag annotation on a deployed decision, not the literal string `latest`. The default `latest` simply takes the most recent deployment.

d.text: `bindingType` is optional. When it is omitted, the engine applies the `latest` default rather than raising an incident.

explanation: A `<zeebe:calledDecision>` without a `bindingType` falls back to the `latest` binding, so the engine resolves the most recently deployed version of the decision whenever the business rule task is activated.

question: On a business rule task with a called DMN decision, when are the input mappings applied to transform variables for the decision engine?

options.text: Only once, on the initial activation of the task.
After the decision is evaluated, just before the `resultVariable` is merged.
On activating the task, and again before each re-evaluation when an incident at the task is resolved.
Only when an output mapping is also defined at the same task.

a.text: Input mappings are not a one-shot operation. They are also reapplied whenever an incident at the task is resolved, before the decision is re-evaluated.

c.text: Input mappings are applied on activating the business rule task, and again when an incident at the task is resolved before the decision is re-evaluated. This can affect the new decision result.

b.text: Input mappings run before the decision evaluation, not after it. Post-evaluation handling is governed by the `resultVariable` and output mappings.

d.text: Input and output mappings are independent. Input mappings still run on activation and on incident resolution even if no output mapping is configured.

explanation: Input mappings on a business rule task run on activating the task (before the decision is evaluated) and are reapplied whenever an incident at the task is resolved. Because mappings re-execute on resolution, a fresh set of variables can change the decision result.

question: Which statement about a business rule task implemented with a job worker is NOT true?

options.text: The job `type` can be a static value or a FEEL expression prefixed with `=` that evaluates to a string.
It is defined using the `zeebe:taskDefinition` extension element with a job `type`.
The engine also evaluates a DMN decision internally before creating the job for the worker.
It behaves exactly like a service task; the only differences are the visual task marker and the semantics for the model.

b.text: True. A job worker implementation is configured via `zeebe:taskDefinition`, the same element used by service tasks.

d.text: True. Business rule tasks with a job worker behave exactly like service tasks; only the task marker and the modelling semantics differ.

a.text: True. The job `type` accepts either a static value or a FEEL expression prefixed with `=` that resolves to a string at runtime.

c.text: False. A business rule task with a job worker implementation does not evaluate a DMN decision; it creates a job and waits for the worker, just like a service task.

explanation: With a job worker implementation, a business rule task behaves like a service task: it creates a job of the configured `type` and waits for a worker to complete it. The internal DMN engine is bypassed; only the called-decision implementation evaluates DMN.

question: A business rule task is modelled with the XML shown. Which binding type do the docs say is applied to `shipping_box_size`?

options.text: No binding type is applied; the deployment is rejected because `bindingType` is required.
`latest`, because no `bindingType` attribute is specified on `<zeebe:calledDecision>`.
`deployment`, because the decision was deployed together with the process.
`versionTag`, with `latest` interpreted as the tag value.

b.text: The chunk labels this XML as 'no explicit binding type (`latest` is used implicitly)', so the engine treats the called decision as bound by `latest`.

c.text: `deployment` requires `bindingType="deployment"` on `<zeebe:calledDecision>`. The shown XML omits the attribute, so `latest` is used implicitly.

d.text: `versionTag` requires both `bindingType="versionTag"` and a separate `versionTag` attribute; the literal string `latest` is not interpreted as a tag value.

a.text: `bindingType` is optional. When omitted, the docs note that `latest` is used implicitly rather than rejecting the deployment.

explanation: The XML representation in the docs labels this first example as 'no explicit binding type (`latest` is used implicitly)'. The engine therefore applies the `latest` binding to the called decision when the task is activated.

codeBlocks.stem:
<bpmn:businessRuleTask id="determine-box-size" name="Determine shipping box size">
  <bpmn:extensionElements>
    <zeebe:calledDecision decisionId="shipping_box_size" resultVariable="boxSize" />
  </bpmn:extensionElements>
</bpmn:businessRuleTask>

question: A child process referenced by a call activity defines a none start event and a message start event. When the call activity is activated, which start event instantiates the child instance?

options.text: The first start event in the BPMN XML order, regardless of type.
Both start events are activated in parallel, creating two execution branches in the child.
The none start event; any other start event types in the child process are ignored.
The message start event, because messages take priority over a none start event.

c.text: A call activity always activates the child at its none start event; the docs explicitly state that other start event types in the called process are ignored.

d.text: Message start events are used for message-correlated process instantiation, not by a call activity. The call path always targets the none start event.

b.text: Only one start event runs when a call activity instantiates the child — the none start event. Other start events do not fan out a second branch.

a.text: Selection is by start-event type (none), not by XML declaration order. Even if the message start event appears first, it is ignored.

explanation: When a call activity invokes another process, a new instance of the referenced process is created and activated at its none start event. The process may declare other start event types, but those are ignored on this invocation path.

question: A call activity uses the FEEL expression shown as its `processId`. When is the expression evaluated, and what must it return?

options.text: Only if `bindingType` is `latest`; it must evaluate to a boolean.
On completing the call activity; it must evaluate to a process instance key.
On activating the call activity; it must evaluate to a `string`.
At deployment time; the result can be of any FEEL type.

c.text: The `processId` expression is evaluated on activating the call activity, and the result must be a `string` so it can be resolved to a deployed process definition.

d.text: FEEL expressions on a call activity are evaluated at runtime when the activity is activated, not at deployment. The result type is fixed: it must be a string.

b.text: The `processId` resolution must happen up-front so the child process can be instantiated. It is not evaluated when the call activity completes.

a.text: Expression evaluation for `processId` is independent of `bindingType`, and the required type is a string, not a boolean.

explanation: A call activity's `processId` is usually a static value but may also be a FEEL expression. The expression is evaluated when the call activity is activated, and it must yield a string that identifies the BPMN process to instantiate.

question: What is the default behaviour of `propagateAllChildVariables` on a call activity, and what is its effect on completion?

options.text: `false` — variables remain in the child and the parent must fetch them via a job worker.
`true` — every variable of the created child process instance is propagated back to the call activity scope when the child completes.
`false` — only variables matching the call activity's output mappings are propagated; you must opt in to propagate everything.
`true` — only the child's local variables are propagated; variables in higher scopes of the child are excluded.

b.text: The default for `propagateAllChildVariables` is `true`: all variables of the created process instance are propagated to the call activity when it completes.

c.text: Defining output mappings is the way to opt out of full propagation, but it is not the default. By default, every child variable is propagated.

d.text: The propagation rule covers all variables of the child instance, not just its local variables. Local-only scoping is configured differently and on the parent side.

a.text: Variables are propagated automatically when the call activity completes; no separate job-worker fetch is needed by default.

explanation: By default, `propagateAllChildVariables` is `true`, so all variables of the child process instance are merged back into the call activity's scope on completion. Defining output mappings on the call activity restricts propagation to only the mapped variables.

question: A call activity is modelled with the XML shown. What is the effect of setting `propagateAllParentVariables="false"` on this call activity?

options.text: All parent variables, including those from higher scopes, are still copied to the child as local variables.
The child process is denied access to its own input mappings, even if they are defined.
Variables produced by the child process are not propagated back to the parent on completion.
Variables from higher scopes of the parent are no longer copied into the child; only variables created by the call activity's input mappings reach the child.

d.text: Setting `propagateAllParentVariables` to `false` stops parent-scope variables from being copied into the child. Only the variables produced by the call activity's input mappings are available in the child instance.

a.text: That describes the default (`true`). Setting the attribute to `false` is exactly what disables that behaviour.

b.text: The attribute controls parent-to-child variable copying, not whether input mappings are honoured. Input mappings still run.

c.text: Child-to-parent propagation is controlled by `propagateAllChildVariables` and output mappings, not by `propagateAllParentVariables`.

explanation: `propagateAllParentVariables` controls the parent-to-child variable flow at a call activity. With it set to `false`, variables from higher scopes are not copied into the child; the child only receives the variables explicitly produced by the call activity's input mappings.

codeBlocks.stem:
<bpmn:callActivity id="Call_Activity" name="Call Process A">
  <bpmn:extensionElements>
    <zeebe:calledElement processId="child-process-id" propagateAllParentVariables="false" />
    <zeebe:ioMapping>
      <zeebe:input source="=variableValue" target="variableName" />
    </zeebe:ioMapping>
  </bpmn:extensionElements>
</bpmn:callActivity>

question: Service task `A` has a compensation boundary event associated with a compensation handler `Undo A`. After `A` completes, the process reaches a compensation intermediate throw event. What happens next?

options.text: Service task `A` is re-executed from scratch by the engine.
An incident is raised because compensation only applies to terminated activities.
`Undo A` is invoked; once it completes, the throw event completes and the process takes its outgoing sequence flow.
The process instance terminates immediately.

c.text: The compensation throw event invokes the compensation handler attached via the compensation boundary event. The throw event stays active until `Undo A` completes, then takes its outgoing sequence flow.

a.text: Compensation does not re-execute the original activity; it runs the dedicated compensation handler that is in charge of reverting the activity's effects.

b.text: Compensation is invoked for completed activities, not for terminated ones. Reaching the throw event after `A` completes is exactly the trigger condition.

d.text: A compensation throw event does not end the process; once the invoked handlers finish, the throw event completes and the flow continues.

explanation: When the process reaches a compensation intermediate throw event, the compensation handler `Undo A` associated with the boundary event on completed `A` is invoked. The throw event remains active until the handler completes, then the outgoing sequence flow is taken.

question: A process instance enters a compensation intermediate throw event in its scope. Which statement about how compensation is triggered is NOT true?

options.text: Without targeting a specific activity, the handlers are invoked all at once and in no specific order.
Compensation handlers of currently active or terminated activities are also invoked alongside the completed ones.
The compensation throw event remains active until every invoked compensation handler has completed.
The compensation handlers of all completed activities in the same scope are invoked.

d.text: True. Triggering compensation in a scope invokes the handlers of every completed activity within that scope.

c.text: True. The throw event stays active until all invoked compensation handlers have finished, then it completes.

a.text: True. Unless compensation is targeted at a specific activity, the handlers are invoked all at once without any specific order.

b.text: False. Compensation handlers of active or terminated activities are not invoked. Only the handlers of completed activities run.

explanation: Compensation in a scope only fires handlers for activities that completed successfully. Active or terminated activities are skipped. Handlers are invoked all at once without a specific order, and the throw event stays active until they all complete.

question: A compensation intermediate throw event sits inside an embedded subprocess that contains completed activities with compensation handlers. The parent scope also has completed activities with compensation handlers. Which handlers are invoked when the throw event fires?

options.text: Handlers in both the subprocess and the parent scope are invoked, because compensation propagates up the scope hierarchy.
An incident is raised because a compensation throw event cannot be placed inside an embedded subprocess.
Only the handlers in the parent scope; the subprocess handlers were already executed when the subprocess completed.
Only the compensation handlers within the embedded subprocess; handlers in the parent scope are not invoked.

d.text: When the compensation throw event is inside an embedded subprocess, the process instance invokes only the compensation handlers within that subprocess and does not invoke any handler outside the subprocess.

a.text: Compensation does not propagate up to the parent scope from a throw event inside an embedded subprocess; its reach is limited to that subprocess.

c.text: Compensation handlers are not auto-executed when the subprocess completes. They only run when a compensation event triggers them.

b.text: Compensation throw events are valid inside embedded subprocesses; no incident is raised for this modelling pattern.

explanation: If a compensation throw event lives inside an embedded subprocess, the process invokes only the compensation handlers within that subprocess. Handlers outside the subprocess — including those in the parent scope — are not invoked.

question: How many times is the compensation handler of a multi-instance activity invoked when a compensation throw event in its scope fires?

options.text: Never — multi-instance activities cannot be compensated.
Once per item in the input collection, including any instances that failed.
Once in total, regardless of how many items were in the input collection.
Once per completed instance of the multi-instance activity.

c.text: The compensation handler of a multi-instance activity is invoked only once, rather than for each item in the input collection. That single handler is responsible for reverting all instances.

d.text: To compensate each instance separately, the compensation handler itself must be marked as multi-instance. By default, the handler runs only once.

b.text: The handler is not invoked per item by default, and compensation is only triggered once all instances of the multi-instance activity have completed.

a.text: Multi-instance activities can be compensated; the handler is just invoked once unless it is itself multi-instance.

explanation: By default, a multi-instance activity's compensation handler runs once to revert all instances. The handler is only invoked when every instance has completed. Per-instance compensation requires marking the handler itself as multi-instance.

question: A compensation activity inside a loop has completed three times during a process instance. When a compensation throw event in its scope fires, how many times is the associated compensation handler invoked?

options.text: Once, regardless of how many times the activity completed.
Once per active token in the scope when the throw event fires.
Zero, because compensation only runs for terminated activities.
Three times — once for each completion of the compensation activity.

d.text: If an activity has been completed more than once, its compensation handler is invoked the same number of times — three completions, three handler invocations.

a.text: The handler is invoked once per completion of the activity, not just once overall. Three completions yield three invocations.

c.text: Compensation invokes handlers for completed activities, not for terminated ones. Three successful completions are exactly the trigger.

b.text: Invocation count is driven by how many times the activity completed, not by the number of active tokens at the throw event.

explanation: A compensation handler reverts the effects of its associated compensation activity. When the throw event fires, the engine invokes the handler once for each successful completion of the activity — three completions in this case mean three handler invocations.

question: An interrupting boundary event is attached to an embedded subprocess. While two parallel branches inside the subprocess are still active, the boundary event is triggered. What happens?

options.text: The entire subprocess, including every active element in both branches, is terminated and the flow continues from the boundary event.
The boundary event waits until both branches finish before completing the subprocess.
A new instance of the subprocess is created with the active elements rolled back to their initial state.
Only the branch with the token nearest the boundary event is terminated; the other branch continues running.

a.text: When an interrupting boundary event on an embedded subprocess is triggered, the entire subprocess — including all active elements inside it — is terminated and the outgoing flow of the boundary event is taken.

d.text: Interrupting boundary events do not terminate only a subset of branches. The entire enclosing subprocess and everything currently active inside it is terminated.

b.text: An interrupting boundary event does not wait for inner branches to finish; it terminates the subprocess immediately.

c.text: There is no automatic re-instantiation or rollback to an initial state; the subprocess simply terminates and the outgoing flow of the boundary event is taken.

explanation: Embedded subprocesses are commonly paired with boundary events. When an interrupting boundary event on the subprocess is triggered, the entire subprocess — every active element inside, in any branch — is terminated, and execution continues along the boundary event's outgoing flow.

question: A modeler adds a collapsed subprocess to a diagram and notices the drill-down link on the activity. Where does this drill-down link take them?

options.text: Into another process definition referenced via `calledElement`.
Into the embedded subprocess defined inside the same diagram.
Into the Optimize process view scoped to that subprocess.
Into a separate `.bpmn` file generated for the collapsed subprocess.

b.text: Per the docs, the drill-down link only opens the embedded subprocess within the same diagram; it cannot target or reuse a different process.

a.text: Targeting another process definition requires a call activity, not a collapsed subprocess link; the docs explicitly recommend call activities for reuse.

d.text: A collapsed subprocess is inline in the same diagram; the drill-down does not create or open a separate file.

c.text: Drill-down is a Modeler display feature. Optimize only partially supports collapsed subprocesses and cannot drill into them.

explanation: Collapsed subprocesses are a display-only feature. Modeler's drill-down link expands the embedded subprocess within the same diagram and cannot point to another process definition. For reusable processes, the docs recommend call activities.

question: An error end event has its `errorCode` configured as the FEEL expression `=failureReason`, and an error event subprocess in the parent scope omits the error reference entirely. When the end event is reached with `failureReason = "PAYMENT_DECLINED"`, what happens?

options.text: The error is unhandled because the catch event has no `errorCode` defined.
The expression is rejected at deployment because throw events only accept static `errorCode` values.
The catch-all event subprocess catches the error regardless of the evaluated code.
An incident is raised because catch events cannot use expressions for `errorCode`.

c.text: Throw events may define `errorCode` as a FEEL expression, and an error catch event that omits the error reference catches all thrown errors.

d.text: The static-value restriction applies to the catch event's `errorCode`, not to the throw event; the catch event here has no `errorCode` at all because it is catch-all.

a.text: An error catch event that omits the error reference catches all thrown errors per the docs, so the error is handled.

b.text: Throw events explicitly accept expressions for `errorCode`; only catch events are restricted to static values.

explanation: On an error throw event the `errorCode` may be an expression evaluated when the event is reached. On a catch event the `errorCode` must be static, but the catch event may omit the error reference entirely, in which case it catches all thrown errors.

question: Which statement about catching a BPMN error in Camunda 8.8 is NOT true?

options.text: Error boundary events and error event subprocesses may be configured as non-interrupting so the original task keeps running while the error path executes.
If a process is started via a call activity, an error not caught in the called process can be caught in the calling parent process instance.
An error can be caught by an error boundary event or an error event subprocess.
When an `errorCode`-specific catch event and an error catch-all event exist in the same scope, the matching specific catch event is prioritized.

c.text: True. The docs state errors are caught by an error boundary event or an error event subprocess.

d.text: True. When both a specific `errorCode` catch event and a catch-all coexist in the same scope, the matching specific catch event is prioritized.

a.text: False. The docs state error boundary events and error event subprocesses MUST be interrupting; non-interrupting is not supported.

b.text: True. If the process was created via a call activity, the error can also be caught in the calling parent process instance.

explanation: Unlike escalation catch events, error boundary events and error event subprocesses are always interrupting in BPMN. The instance does not continue along the regular path; it follows the path leaving the catching error event.

question: A scoring service is temporarily unavailable and the team must decide how to handle this in BPMN. Which approach best aligns with Camunda 8.8's recommended distinction between business reactions and technical reactions?

options.text: Add an error catch path that continues with a default rating when a business reaction is needed; leave transient failures to retries or incidents hidden in the visual.
Throw a BPMN escalation event whenever the service is unreachable so business users can intervene manually.
Model retries as an explicit loop around the service task so the BPMN visibly tries several times before failing.
Always classify the outage as a technical error and let the process instance fail, since business processes should not react to technical problems.

c.text: The docs explicitly warn that modeling retries inline bloats the visual model and confuses business personnel; retries should stay hidden.

a.text: The docs recommend deciding by reaction: if you want a business reaction (e.g., proceed with a default rating), model it; transient technical failures should be handled with retries or incidents.

d.text: The docs explicitly state that even a technical problem can qualify for a business reaction and give the default-rating example, so this is wrong.

b.text: Escalation is a different BPMN construct; the guidance is about choosing between a modeled business reaction and hidden technical retries, not switching to escalation.

explanation: Camunda recommends classifying error handling by the reaction, not the source. Business reactions (continue with a default outcome, alternate path) belong in the model. Technical reactions (transient outage retries) should stay hidden behind retries and incidents to avoid bloating the visual model.

question: A subprocess reaches an escalation throw event that is caught by an escalation boundary event in the parent scope. By default, what happens to the outgoing sequence flows of the throw event and the catch event?

options.text: Both outgoing sequence flows are taken because escalations are non-critical.
Only the throw event's outgoing flow is taken; the catch event acts as a passive observer.
Neither flow is taken; the subprocess is interrupted and the parent restarts from its start event.
Only the catch event's outgoing flow is taken; the throw event's flow is discarded.

a.text: The docs state that because escalation events are non-critical, the outgoing sequence flows of both the Throw and the Catch event are taken.

d.text: Discarding the throw event's flow would match interrupting error semantics, not the default non-critical escalation behaviour.

b.text: The catch event is an active participant: its outgoing flow is also taken, in addition to the throw event's flow.

c.text: Escalations do not interrupt by default; execution continues from the throw event and the parent scope continues from the catch event.

explanation: Escalation events communicate to a higher flow scope but are non-critical. Execution continues at the throw event's outgoing flow and the higher scope continues from the catch event's outgoing flow — both flows are taken.

question: Which statement about defining the `escalationCode` of an escalation event in Camunda 8.8 is correct?

options.text: An escalation catch event must always reference an `escalation` element; omitting the reference is rejected at deployment.
Both throw and catch events accept FEEL expressions for the `escalationCode`.
On an escalation throw event the `escalationCode` may be a FEEL expression, but on a catch event it must be a static value.
Both throw and catch events require the `escalationCode` to be a static value at deployment time.

c.text: Per the docs, throw events may use an expression or a static value for `escalationCode`; catch events' `escalationCode` must be a static value.

d.text: Throw events explicitly accept expressions for `escalationCode`; only catch events are restricted to static values.

b.text: Catch events must use static values for `escalationCode`; only throw events accept expressions.

a.text: An escalation catch event may omit the escalation reference. When it does, it catches all thrown escalations.

explanation: The throw side evaluates `escalationCode` when the event is reached, so an expression is allowed. The catch side needs a stable identifier at deployment time, so the code must be static — or the reference can be omitted to catch all escalations.

question: A subprocess throws an escalation with `escalationCode="OUT_OF_STOCK"` but no catch event in any parent scope references that code, and there is no catch-all event either. What happens?

options.text: The escalation is silently ignored; no incident is raised and the process continues normally.
An incident is created at the throw event, identical to the behaviour for an unmatched error code.
The escalation propagates indefinitely until a catch event referencing `OUT_OF_STOCK` is deployed in any process definition.
The process instance is terminated because every escalation must be caught somewhere.

a.text: Per the docs, if no escalation catch event matches the `escalationCode`, the escalation is not caught, no incident is raised, and the process continues without escalating.

b.text: This is the key contrast with error events: unmatched escalations do NOT raise an incident, unlike unmatched errors.

d.text: Escalations are non-critical; the instance continues from the throw event's outgoing flow rather than terminating.

c.text: Escalations are scoped to the throwing instance's parent flow scope hierarchy, not broadcast across deployments.

explanation: Escalation events are non-critical. An unmatched escalation is dropped silently — no incident is raised and the process continues along the throw event's outgoing flow. This is a deliberate contrast with error events, where an unmatched error does raise an incident.

question: Compare the documented XML representations of an escalation boundary catch event and an escalation boundary catch-all event. Which attribute is present on the regular catch event but intentionally omitted on the catch-all variant?

options.text: The `cancelActivity` attribute on `bpmn:boundaryEvent`.
The `escalationRef` attribute on `bpmn:escalationEventDefinition`.
The `attachedToRef` attribute on `bpmn:boundaryEvent`.
The `id` attribute on `bpmn:escalationEventDefinition`.

b.text: The regular escalation boundary catch event carries `escalationRef="Escalation_2alpsjo"` on `bpmn:escalationEventDefinition`; the catch-all variant omits that attribute so it matches all thrown escalations.

c.text: `attachedToRef="Activity_1q7i1lv"` appears on both XML examples; it identifies the activity the boundary event is attached to, not catch-all behaviour.

a.text: `cancelActivity="false"` appears on both XML examples; it controls interruption behaviour, not whether the event is catch-all.

d.text: Both XML examples include an `id` on `bpmn:escalationEventDefinition`; the `id` is an XML identifier and is unrelated to catch-all behaviour.

explanation: In the documented XML, the only difference between the regular escalation boundary catch event and the catch-all variant is the presence or absence of `escalationRef` on `bpmn:escalationEventDefinition`. Omitting `escalationRef` makes the boundary event catch all thrown escalations.

codeBlocks.stem:
<!-- Regular escalation boundary catch event -->
<bpmn:boundaryEvent id="Event_1wpcmdz" cancelActivity="false"
    attachedToRef="Activity_1q7i1lv">
  <bpmn:escalationEventDefinition id="EscalationEventDefinition_1fpge5i"
      escalationRef="Escalation_2alpsjo" />
</bpmn:boundaryEvent>

<!-- Escalation boundary catch-all event -->
<bpmn:boundaryEvent id="Event_1wpcmdz" cancelActivity="false"
    attachedToRef="Activity_1q7i1lv">
  <bpmn:escalationEventDefinition id="EscalationEventDefinition_1fpge5i" />
</bpmn:boundaryEvent>

question: An event-based gateway has outgoing sequence flows to a `messageEventDefinition` intermediate catch event and a `timerEventDefinition` intermediate catch event with `PT1H`. Based on the documented XML representation, which additional event would also be a valid target after the gateway?

options.text: A user task placed directly after the gateway with no intermediate event in between.
An intermediate catch event with a `signalEventDefinition`.
An intermediate catch event with an `errorEventDefinition`.
An intermediate throw event with a `messageEventDefinition`.

b.text: The documented XML representation shows message, timer, and signal intermediate catch events as valid event-based gateway targets.

c.text: Errors are not caught by intermediate catch events on an event-based gateway; error handling uses boundary events or event subprocesses.

d.text: An event-based gateway routes to catch events waiting for an event, not to throw events that emit one.

a.text: An event-based gateway is followed by intermediate catch events that race against each other, not by a plain user task.

explanation: Event-based gateways are followed by intermediate catch events that race for the next incoming event. The docs' XML example shows message, timer, and signal intermediate catch event definitions as valid targets.

question: Which set of start event types is permitted for a BPMN event subprocess in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: None — event subprocesses use a boundary event as their entry point instead of a start event.
Timer, Message, Error, Signal, and Escalation.
Timer and Message only; all other start event types are restricted to top-level processes.
Any start event type, including a plain none start event.

b.text: The docs explicitly list these five start event types — Timer, Message, Error, Signal, and Escalation — as the allowed triggers for an event subprocess.

c.text: Error, Signal, and Escalation start events are also explicitly listed as allowed triggers for event subprocesses.

a.text: An event subprocess sits inside the scope and has its own start event; it is not entered via a boundary event.

d.text: A none start event is not in the allowed list; the start event must be one of the five trigger types named in the docs.

explanation: An event subprocess must have exactly one start event, and that start event must be a Timer, Message, Error, Signal, or Escalation type. The start event can be interrupting (solid border) or non-interrupting (dashed border).

question: A timer-triggered event subprocess inside an embedded subprocess writes to `orderStatus`, a variable declared in the embedded subprocess scope. A teammate asks whether a boundary timer event attached to the same subprocess could equally modify `orderStatus`. What's the correct distinction?

options.text: A boundary event can modify the variable; the event subprocess cannot, because event subprocesses operate in an isolated child scope.
Neither can modify `orderStatus`; only the original embedded subprocess's tasks may write scope-local variables.
Both can modify `orderStatus` because they share identical scope semantics.
Only the event subprocess can directly read and modify `orderStatus`, because it is inside the scope; a boundary event is outside the scope.

d.text: Per the docs, an event subprocess is inside the scope and can access and modify all local variables; this is not possible with a boundary event, which is outside the scope.

c.text: The docs explicitly contrast the two: boundary events are outside the scope and cannot access its local variables the way event subprocesses can.

b.text: Activities and event subprocesses inside the containing scope can read and modify its local variables; the docs say so directly for event subprocesses.

a.text: The relationship is inverted. The event subprocess is inside the scope; the boundary event is outside and cannot reach scope-local variables.

explanation: Because an event subprocess sits inside its containing scope, it can read and modify all of that scope's local variables. A boundary event lives outside the activity it is attached to, so it cannot reach those local variables.

question: A diverging exclusive gateway has three outgoing sequence flows in BPMN XML order: Flow A with `=amount > 100`, Flow B with `=amount > 50`, and Flow C marked as the default flow. With `amount = 250`, which outgoing flow is taken?

options.text: Flow B.
Flow A.
Both Flow A and Flow B.
Flow C, because two conditions match and the gateway falls back to the default.

b.text: The docs state the gateway evaluates conditions in the order they appear in the BPMN XML and selects the first flow whose condition is fulfilled. Flow A comes first and matches.

a.text: Flow B's condition is also true, but evaluation stops at the first match. Flow A is evaluated first.

c.text: An exclusive gateway always takes exactly one outgoing flow; it never forks like an inclusive or parallel gateway.

d.text: The default flow is only taken when no other condition is fulfilled; that is not the case here because Flow A's condition is true.

explanation: An exclusive gateway evaluates outgoing conditions in BPMN XML order and selects the first matching flow. The default flow is the fallback when no condition is fulfilled. With overlapping conditions, ordering decides the outcome.

question: A diverging inclusive gateway has three outgoing flows with conditions `=list contains(courses, "pasta")` (Pasta), `=list contains(courses, "steak")` (Steak), and `=list contains(courses, "salad")` (Salad). With `courses = ["pasta", "salad"]`, which flows are taken?

options.text: Only Pasta, since inclusive gateways still select a single flow per token.
All three flows, because the default flow is also taken.
Pasta and Salad.
None — the gateway raises an incident when more than one condition is fulfilled.

c.text: The docs state the inclusive gateway takes all outgoing sequence flows whose conditions are fulfilled. Both `pasta` and `salad` are in the list, so both flows are taken.

a.text: Selecting one flow per token is exclusive-gateway behaviour. The inclusive gateway is designed to take multiple paths simultaneously.

b.text: The default flow is only taken when NO condition is fulfilled. Here two conditions are true, so the default is not used.

d.text: Multiple matching conditions are the normal case for an inclusive gateway; no incident is created.

explanation: An inclusive gateway evaluates all outgoing conditions and takes every flow whose condition is fulfilled. The default flow is the fallback only when none match. With `["pasta", "salad"]`, the Pasta and Salad flows are both taken.

question: In the documented BPMN XML representation of an inclusive gateway, how is the default outgoing sequence flow indicated?

options.text: The gateway element has a `default` attribute referencing the sequence flow id, and that flow has no `conditionExpression`.
The default flow has a `conditionExpression` with the value `=true`.
The first outgoing sequence flow listed in the XML is always treated as the default.
The default flow uses an `isDefault="true"` attribute on the `bpmn:sequenceFlow` element.

a.text: The documented XML shows `<bpmn:inclusiveGateway ... default="Flow_05d0jjq">` and the referenced Salad flow has no `conditionExpression` element.

b.text: The default flow should not have a condition; the docs note its behaviour depends on the other conditions, so adding `=true` would defeat the purpose.

d.text: BPMN marks the default via a `default` attribute on the gateway, not via an attribute on the sequence flow.

c.text: XML order matters for evaluation in some constructs, but the default flow is identified by the gateway's `default` attribute, not by position.
