explanation: BPMN identifies the default outgoing flow of an inclusive gateway by setting the gateway's `default` attribute to the sequence flow id. That referenced flow must not carry a `conditionExpression`, because its behaviour depends on whether none of the other conditions match.

question: A modeler draws a throwing link event named `Retry` at the root process level and a catching link event with the same name inside an embedded subprocess, intending to jump from the root into the subprocess. What is the result?

options.text: The link does not connect, because link events can only link sections of a process within the same scope.
The link works only if both events also share a Camunda-specific `linkScope` attribute.
The link works because matching link names connect any throw to any catch globally across scopes.
The catching link inside the subprocess is automatically promoted to the root scope so the link can connect.

a.text: Per the docs, link events can only be used to link sections of a process within the same scope, so a root-level throw cannot pair with a catch inside a subprocess.

c.text: Link names match only within the same scope; they are not a global pairing mechanism across scopes.

b.text: There is no such attribute; the same-scope restriction is not overridable.

d.text: BPMN does not promote events across scopes. The same-scope limitation applies to both link events and sequence flows.

explanation: Link events are scope-local shortcuts equivalent to a pair of intermediate none events joined by a sequence flow. They can only pair within the same scope — either both at root level or both inside the same subprocess — and cannot cross subprocess boundaries.

question: A process instance reaches a manual task `Confirm physical delivery` that has no `zeebe:taskDefinition` and no assignee. How does the Camunda 8.8 engine handle this activity at runtime?

options.text: The engine creates a job of type `io.camunda:manual` for a job worker to complete.
The engine treats it as a pass-through and continues immediately to the next element.
The engine creates a user task in Tasklist that a human must claim and complete.
The engine raises an incident because the manual task lacks an assignee.

b.text: Within the engine and BPMN model, a manual task is handled as a pass-through activity that automatically continues the process at the moment the instance arrives. No job, user task, or external action is required.

a.text: Manual tasks do not create jobs. That is the behaviour of service tasks and other job-worker activities; manual tasks model work done entirely outside the engine.

c.text: Manual tasks differ from user tasks: user tasks define an actionable task assisted by the engine and Tasklist, while manual tasks have no UI or workflow assistance and simply pass through.

d.text: An assignee is not required for a manual task. The activity is informational only and does not block on any external completion.

explanation: A manual task documents human work performed outside the process engine, such as reviewing a document or handling a physical item. The BPMN engine treats it as a pass-through activity, so the process instance continues immediately upon arrival. This contrasts with user tasks, which are actionable tasks managed by the engine and surfaced in Tasklist.

question: Which set of activity markers is currently supported in Camunda 8.8 BPMN modeling?

options.text: Multi-instance, loop, and compensation.
Multi-instance, compensation, and ad-hoc.
Multi-instance, transactional, and timer.
Parallel, sequential, and event-based.

b.text: The Camunda 8.8 modeling docs list multi-instance, compensation, and ad-hoc as the currently supported markers.

a.text: A standard BPMN loop marker is not listed as a currently supported Camunda 8.8 marker; multi-instance is the supported iteration construct.

d.text: Parallel and sequential describe the orientation of a multi-instance marker rather than separate marker types, and event-based is a gateway, not an activity marker.

c.text: Transactional subprocesses and timer markers are not in the list of supported activity markers for Camunda 8.8.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 markers page enumerates three currently supported markers: multi-instance, compensation, and ad-hoc. The loop marker from older BPMN tooling and gateway-related symbols are not part of this list.

question: How does the recipient model of a BPMN message event differ from that of a BPMN signal event in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A message is delivered to a single recipient (1:1); a signal is delivered to multiple recipients (1:N).
Both messages and signals are 1:1 deliveries, but signals additionally require a `correlationKey`.
A message is broadcast to all subscribers (1:N); a signal is targeted to one specific instance (1:1).
Both messages and signals are 1:N broadcasts; the only difference is the visual notation.

a.text: Message events form a single sender to single recipient relationship (1:1); the message cannot have more than one recipient. Signal events form a single sender to several-recipient relationship (1:N).

c.text: Reversed. Messages are not broadcast; signals are. A signal triggers every process instance with a token waiting at a corresponding catch event, even across different processes.

d.text: The difference is semantic, not only visual. Messages target one recipient; signals reach every matching subscriber.

b.text: Correlation keys belong to the message world. Signals are broadcast by name and reach all matching subscribers, so the cardinality is genuinely different.

explanation: Message events are used when a process instance must wait for a message from a specific sender — a 1:1 relationship. Signal events are broadcasts; a single thrown signal triggers every catching subscriber across processes, giving a 1:N relationship.

question: A process has a message start event for `Order placed`. An instance was started by a message with `correlationKey = "order-42"` and is still active. A new `Order placed` message arrives with `correlationKey = "order-42"` and a TTL greater than zero. What happens?

options.text: A new process instance is created immediately because message start events ignore correlation keys.
The active instance is terminated and replaced by a new instance for the same `correlationKey`.
The message is discarded and an incident is raised for duplicate correlation.
The message is buffered; no new instance is created while the original instance is active.

d.text: When an instance triggered by a message with this `correlationKey` is already active, a new message with the same key is not correlated and no new instance is created. Because the TTL is greater than zero, the message is buffered.

a.text: Message start events do honour the `correlationKey` on incoming messages. They only skip the correlation check when the published message has an empty `correlationKey`.

b.text: The active instance is not terminated. The engine waits for it to complete; only then can a buffered message with the same `correlationKey` create a new instance.

c.text: Duplicate correlation is a normal, handled situation. No incident is raised; the message is simply buffered until the active instance ends or the TTL expires.

explanation: Message start events use the `correlationKey` to deduplicate creation: if an instance triggered by a message with the same key is still active, a new matching message is not correlated. When TTL is greater than zero, the message is buffered and may correlate later, once the active instance completes or is terminated, to start a new instance of the latest process version.

question: A process instance enters an intermediate message throw event configured with job type `kafka-publish`. What does the Camunda 8.8 engine do at this point?

options.text: It broadcasts the message to all subscribers like a signal throw event.
It internally correlates the message to any waiting message catch event with the same name.
It creates a job of type `kafka-publish` and waits for a job worker to complete it before continuing.
It serializes the message and publishes it to a Kafka topic directly without involving a job worker.

c.text: Intermediate message throw events behave like service tasks or send tasks: the engine creates a job with the configured job type and waits for a job worker to process and complete it.

b.text: Message throw events are not processed by Zeebe to correlate a message to a message catch event. They only create jobs; correlation is the job worker's responsibility, e.g. by publishing to Kafka.

d.text: Zeebe itself does not publish to external systems. A job worker must request the job, perform the publish, and report job completion back to the engine.

a.text: Broadcasting to multiple subscribers is the semantics of signal throw events. Message throw events delegate publishing to a job worker via the configured job type.

explanation: Intermediate message throw events and message end events behave like service tasks and send tasks: the engine creates a job of the configured job type and waits for the assigned job worker to complete it. Zeebe does not itself correlate or publish the message; the worker performs the external action.

question: Besides the message name, what must a BPMN message reference when it is used by an intermediate catch event or boundary event in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A `timeToLive` value expressed as an ISO 8601 duration.
A `producerId` matching the publishing application's identifier.
A static `messageId` that is unique within the process definition.
A `correlationKey` expression that evaluates to a `string` or a `number`.

d.text: A message must define its name and the `correlationKey` expression, evaluated on activating the message event. The expression must result in either a `string` or a `number`.

c.text: There is no `messageId` field on a message definition; the name is what is matched on publish, not an additional unique id.

a.text: `timeToLive` is supplied by the publisher of the message, not declared on the message definition in the BPMN model.

b.text: A `producerId` is not part of the BPMN message model. Messages are correlated by name and `correlationKey` value, not by producer.

explanation: A Camunda 8.8 BPMN message requires a name and, for non-start usages, a `correlationKey` expression. The expression is evaluated when the message event is activated and must produce a string or number that the publisher's `correlationKey` value will be matched against. The `correlationKey` may be omitted only when the message is referenced solely by message start events.

question: Looking at the BPMN XML for Camunda 8.8 message events, which statement is NOT true?

options.text: A message start event references its `<bpmn:message>` via `messageRef` on a `<bpmn:messageEventDefinition>` inside the `<bpmn:startEvent>`.
An intermediate message catch event declares a `<zeebe:subscription correlationKey="..."/>` inside the `<bpmn:message>`'s `<bpmn:extensionElements>`.
A boundary message event uses `attachedToRef` on `<bpmn:boundaryEvent>` to point at the activity it is attached to.
A message start event must declare its own `<zeebe:subscription correlationKey="..."/>` extension on the message definition.

a.text: True. The XML example shows `<bpmn:startEvent>` containing `<bpmn:messageEventDefinition messageRef="Message_0z0aft4"/>` that points at a `<bpmn:message>` element.

b.text: True. The intermediate catch example places `<zeebe:subscription correlationKey="= orderId"/>` inside `<bpmn:extensionElements>` on the `<bpmn:message>` element.

c.text: True. The boundary event example uses `attachedToRef="collect-money"` on `<bpmn:boundaryEvent>` to bind the boundary event to its host activity.

d.text: False. A `correlationKey` is not specified for a message start event in the BPMN model; the example shows a bare `<bpmn:message name="order-placed"/>` without `<zeebe:subscription>`.

explanation: Message catch and boundary events declare a `<zeebe:subscription correlationKey="..."/>` inside their message's extension elements, but message start events do not — the publisher supplies the `correlationKey` when sending the message, and the engine tags the created instance with that value.

codeBlocks.stem:
<bpmn:message id="Message_0z0aft4" name="order-placed" />

<bpmn:startEvent id="order-placed" name="Order placed">
  <bpmn:messageEventDefinition messageRef="Message_0z0aft4" />
</bpmn:startEvent>

question: Which BPMN element does the Camunda 8.8 documentation explicitly call out as NOT supporting the multi-instance marker?

options.text: Intermediate throw events with a `JobWorker` implementation.
Call activities.
Embedded subprocesses.
Service tasks.

a.text: The docs note explicitly states that events with a `JobWorker` implementation, such as intermediate throw events, do not support the multi-instance marker.

d.text: Service tasks are listed as a supported activity for the multi-instance marker.

c.text: Embedded subprocesses are listed as a supported activity for the multi-instance marker.

b.text: Call activities are listed as a supported activity for the multi-instance marker.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 supports the multi-instance marker on supported activities such as service tasks, receive tasks, embedded subprocesses, and call activities. The documentation calls out one exception: events implemented as `JobWorker` jobs, such as intermediate throw events, do not support this marker.

question: How does a sequential multi-instance activity differ from a parallel multi-instance activity in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Sequential uses a single Zeebe stream processor while parallel spawns one OS thread per instance.
Sequential creates all instances up front but runs them in `inputCollection` order; parallel creates all instances up front and runs them in random order.
Sequential creates one instance at a time and starts the next only after the previous completes; parallel creates all instances at activation and executes them concurrently and independently.
Sequential is the default; the parallel variant requires `isParallel="true"` to be set on the loop characteristics.

c.text: In a sequential multi-instance activity, instances are executed one at a time and a new instance for the next `inputCollection` element is created only when the current one completes. In a parallel multi-instance activity, all instances are created when the multi-instance body is activated and execute concurrently and independently.

b.text: Parallel multi-instance does not create all instances up front and then run them in random order; it creates all instances on activation and runs them concurrently. Sequential does not create them all up front at all.

a.text: Concurrency in Zeebe is not about OS threads. Records of a process instance are written to the same partition; the distinction is logical, not threading-related.

d.text: Parallel is the default. Sequential is selected by setting `isSequential="true"` on the multi-instance loop characteristics, not the other way around.

explanation: A sequential multi-instance executes instances one after another, creating each only when the previous finishes. A parallel multi-instance — the default — instantiates all instances when the body activates and runs them concurrently and independently. The BPMN notation reflects this: three horizontal lines for sequential, three vertical lines for parallel.

question: A parallel multi-instance service task is configured with `inputCollection = items` and `inputElement = item`. When the multi-instance body activates, the variable `items` evaluates to `[]`. What happens?

options.text: An incident is raised because `inputCollection` must contain at least one element.
A single instance is created with `item = null` so that the activity is still executed once.
The multi-instance body is completed immediately and no instances are created; the activity behaves as if it were skipped.
The engine waits at the multi-instance body until `items` becomes non-empty before creating any instances.

c.text: If the `inputCollection` value is empty, the multi-instance body is completed immediately and no instances are created; the docs describe this as the activity behaving like it is skipped.

d.text: The `inputCollection` expression is evaluated when the multi-instance body is activated. The engine does not wait for the variable to change later.

a.text: An empty `inputCollection` is a normal, supported case. It does not raise an incident; it simply skips the activity.

b.text: No fallback instance with `item = null` is created. With zero elements, zero instances are created and the body completes.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 evaluates the multi-instance `inputCollection` expression once, when the multi-instance body is activated. If the result is an empty array, the body completes immediately with no inner instances created — effectively skipping the activity — and `outputCollection`, if defined, is propagated as an empty array.

question: A parallel multi-instance activity defines `outputCollection = results` and `outputElement = = result`. Which statement about how its output is collected is NOT true?

options.text: When the multi-instance body completes, `results` is propagated from the body's local scope into its parent scope.
If an instance completes without ever setting a `result` variable, `null` is inserted at that index of `results`.
Entries are appended to `results` in the order in which the parallel instances actually complete, so the result order depends on worker timing.
The order of entries in `results` matches the order of the corresponding entries in `inputCollection`.

d.text: True. Each `outputElement` result is inserted into `outputCollection` at the same index as the corresponding `inputElement` in `inputCollection`, so the order is determined and matches — even for parallel multi-instance.

b.text: True. If the `outputElement` variable is not updated by an instance, `null` is inserted instead of a value at that index.

a.text: True. `outputCollection` is created as a local variable of the multi-instance body and is propagated to the parent scope when the body completes.

c.text: False. Order does not depend on completion timing. Even for parallel multi-instance, entries are placed at the index of the matching `inputElement`, so the resulting order mirrors `inputCollection`.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 stores each instance's `outputElement` result at the same index in `outputCollection` as that instance's `inputElement` in `inputCollection`. This holds for parallel as well as sequential multi-instance, so the output order is deterministic and mirrors the input, regardless of which worker finishes first. Missing values become `null`.

question: A parallel multi-instance call activity has `outputElement = output`. Each child instance produces a result in a variable named `x`. Without any further configuration, the merged child variables cause every completing child to overwrite the same `output` in the parent, corrupting `outputCollection`. Which output mapping on the call activity fixes this race condition?

options.text: source `= output`, target `results`
source `= x`, target `output`
source `= output`, target `x`
No mapping is needed; Camunda 8.8 isolates child variables automatically.

b.text: Mapping source `= x` to target `output` restricts which child variables are merged: only the child's `x` is exposed, and it lands in a local `output` variable per instance. The multi-instance body then picks each local `output` up via `outputElement`, eliminating the shared write.

c.text: Source and target are reversed. This would try to derive `x` from the parent's `output`, which is exactly the variable being corrupted.

a.text: Renaming `output` to `results` does not stop the race: every child still merges its full variable set into the same parent scope; only the variable name has changed.

d.text: By default, variables produced inside a child instance are propagated up via variable merging, which is precisely what creates the race. Output mappings are how you scope the merge.

explanation: On a parallel multi-instance call activity, each completed child merges its variables into the parent's process instance. Without an output mapping, every child overwrites the same parent-scope variable that `outputElement` reads, corrupting `outputCollection`. An output mapping `source: =x` and `target: output` restricts the merge to a single value and stores it under `output` as a local variable per instance, which the multi-instance body then collects in order.

question: A parallel multi-instance activity has 10 instances and the completion condition `= numberOfCompletedInstances / numberOfInstances >= 0.5`. The 5th instance finishes successfully. What happens to the remaining 5 active instances?

options.text: They continue running, and the body waits for all 10 instances to complete before propagating `outputCollection`.
They are terminated and the multi-instance body completes.
The completion condition is ignored because `numberOfCompletedInstances` is not exposed as a process variable.
They keep running but their results are dropped from `outputCollection` because the condition was already met.

b.text: The `completionCondition` is evaluated each time an instance completes. When it evaluates to `true`, any instances that are still active are terminated and the multi-instance body completes.

a.text: A `completionCondition` exists precisely to short-circuit the wait-for-all behaviour. Once it evaluates to `true`, the body completes without waiting for the remaining instances.

d.text: The remaining instances are terminated, not allowed to keep running with discarded output. They simply stop.

c.text: `numberOfCompletedInstances` and the other BPMN multi-instance properties are available specifically for use in the `completionCondition` expression and take precedence over process variables with the same name.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 re-evaluates the `completionCondition` whenever an instance of the multi-instance body completes. When the boolean expression becomes `true`, every still-active instance is terminated and the body itself completes. The properties `numberOfInstances`, `numberOfActiveInstances`, `numberOfCompletedInstances`, and `numberOfTerminatedInstances` are exposed for use in this expression and shadow process variables of the same name.

question: In the BPMN XML of a Camunda 8.8 multi-instance service task, which attribute distinguishes a sequential multi-instance from a parallel one?

options.text: `executionMode="sequential"` on `<bpmn:serviceTask>`.
`order="serial"` on `<zeebe:loopCharacteristics>`.
`isParallel="false"` on `<zeebe:loopCharacteristics>`.
`isSequential="true"` on `<bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics>`.

d.text: The XML example for a sequential multi-instance service task uses `<bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics isSequential="true">`. Parallel is the default and omits this attribute.

c.text: There is no `isParallel` attribute on `<zeebe:loopCharacteristics>`. That element holds `inputCollection`, `inputElement`, `outputCollection`, and `outputElement` only.

a.text: No `executionMode` attribute is defined on `<bpmn:serviceTask>` for this purpose. The sequential/parallel distinction lives on the loop characteristics element.

b.text: No `order` attribute exists on `<zeebe:loopCharacteristics>`. The standard BPMN flag for sequential execution is `isSequential` on `<bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics>`.

explanation: A multi-instance activity is parallel by default. To declare it sequential, the BPMN XML sets `isSequential="true"` on `<bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics>`. The Camunda-specific `<zeebe:loopCharacteristics>` extension carries `inputCollection`, `inputElement`, `outputCollection`, and `outputElement`, not the sequential/parallel flag.

codeBlocks.stem:
<bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics isSequential="true">
  <bpmn:extensionElements>
    <zeebe:loopCharacteristics
        inputCollection="= items" inputElement="item"
        outputCollection="results" outputElement="= result" />
  </bpmn:extensionElements>
</bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics>

question: A process has a parallel (AND) gateway splitting into three outgoing sequence flows, joined later by a second parallel gateway. Which statement about this construct in Camunda 8.8 is NOT true?

options.text: The join gateway waits until each of its incoming sequence flows has been taken before continuing.
The split gateway evaluates each outgoing flow's condition expression and takes only those whose condition is `true`.
The concurrent paths run as concurrent tokens on the same Zeebe partition, not as parallel OS threads.
Entering the split gateway causes all three outgoing sequence flows to be taken concurrently.

d.text: True. When a parallel gateway with multiple outgoing sequence flows is entered, all flows are taken and the resulting paths execute concurrently and independently.

a.text: True. A parallel join waits at the gateway until each incoming sequence flow has been taken before the instance continues.

c.text: True. The docs note that outgoing paths of a parallel gateway are concurrent rather than parallel in the sense of OS threads; all records of a process instance are written to the same partition.

b.text: False. Parallel gateways do not evaluate conditions on their outgoing flows. Conditional routing is the job of exclusive or inclusive gateways; a parallel gateway always activates all outgoing flows.

explanation: A parallel (AND) gateway unconditionally splits the flow into all of its outgoing sequence flows and joins by waiting until each incoming flow has produced a token. It never inspects condition expressions on its outgoing flows — that is the role of exclusive and inclusive gateways. The concurrency is logical: records run on a single Zeebe partition, not on separate OS threads.

question: A receive task references message `Money collected` with `correlationKey` set to `= orderId`. When is the expression evaluated, and what type must the result be?

options.text: Evaluated when the receive task is activated; the result must be a `string` or `number`.
Evaluated when the message is published; the result must be a `UUID`.
Evaluated when the receive task is activated; the result must be a `string` only — numeric correlation keys are not supported.
Evaluated at deployment of the process; the result must be a `string`.

a.text: The docs state the `correlationKey` expression is evaluated on activating the receive task and must result either in a `string` or `number`.

d.text: The expression is not evaluated at deployment; it is evaluated on activation so it can read the current process instance variables.

c.text: Both `string` and `number` are valid result types for a `correlationKey`; numeric keys are explicitly supported.

b.text: The publisher does not evaluate the expression; the receive task evaluates it from process variables when it activates.

explanation: Per the receive task message correlation docs, the `correlationKey` expression is evaluated when the receive task activates and must produce a `string` or `number`. That value is then matched against the published message's correlation key to deliver the message to the waiting instance.

question: A script task defined with `zeebe:script` evaluates a FEEL expression that fails (for example, division by zero). What does the runtime do?

options.text: The job is retried by the Zeebe broker using the default count of three retries.
The process instance terminates via an error end event.
An incident is raised at the script task; when the incident is resolved, the FEEL expression is evaluated again.
The script task is skipped and the token moves along the outgoing sequence flow.

b.text: FEEL evaluation failure does not end the process; it raises an incident at the script task so the cause can be investigated and evaluation resumed.

c.text: The docs say if the FEEL expression evaluation is unsuccessful, an incident is raised at the script task, and when the incident is resolved the script task is evaluated again.

a.text: The FEEL script task implementation does not use jobs or worker retries; the integrated FEEL Scala engine evaluates the expression directly and raises an incident on failure.

d.text: The script task is not skipped; the engine raises an incident and blocks further progress until the incident is resolved.

explanation: The FEEL-implementation script task is evaluated by Zeebe's integrated FEEL Scala engine. On evaluation failure an incident is raised at the script task; resolving the incident triggers re-evaluation. Worker retries apply only to the alternative job worker implementation, not to the inline FEEL form.

question: Which combination of attributes on the `zeebe:script` extension element defines an inline FEEL script task and stores its result as a process variable?

options.text: `feel` for the expression and `output` for the variable name.
`expression` for the FEEL expression and `resultVariable` for the variable name.
`engine` set to `feel` and `body` for the FEEL expression.
`type` for the FEEL expression and `headers` for the variable name.

b.text: The docs say to put the FEEL expression in the `expression` attribute and the result variable name in the `resultVariable` attribute of `zeebe:script`.

d.text: `type` and `headers` belong to the job worker implementation via `zeebe:taskDefinition` and `zeebe:taskHeaders`, not to an inline FEEL script.

c.text: `zeebe:script` has no `engine` or `body` attributes; the inline FEEL form uses `expression` and `resultVariable`.

a.text: Those attribute names do not exist on `zeebe:script`; the correct attributes are `expression` and `resultVariable`.

explanation: An inline FEEL script task uses the `zeebe:script` extension element with two attributes: `expression` holds the FEEL expression and `resultVariable` names the process variable that stores its result. By default that variable is merged into the process instance, which can be customised via an output mapping.

question: How does a script task using the job worker implementation differ from one using the inline FEEL expression?

options.text: Both variants require a registered job worker; the only difference is the task marker shown in the modeler.
The job worker variant is evaluated synchronously inside the broker, while the FEEL variant offloads execution to an external job worker.
The job worker variant creates a job that Zeebe does not process itself — an external worker must complete it — while the FEEL variant is evaluated by Zeebe's integrated FEEL engine.
The job worker variant uses `zeebe:script` with `expression`, while the FEEL variant uses `zeebe:taskDefinition` with `type`.

c.text: The docs state job-worker script tasks behave like service tasks: they create a job that Zeebe does not process, and a job worker must request and complete it.

b.text: The mapping is reversed: the FEEL variant is evaluated by Zeebe; the job worker variant requires an external worker.

a.text: The FEEL variant does not require a job worker — Zeebe's integrated FEEL Scala engine evaluates the expression in-process.

d.text: The attributes are swapped: the inline FEEL variant uses `zeebe:script`/`expression`; the job worker variant uses `zeebe:taskDefinition`/`type`.

explanation: With the job worker implementation, a script task behaves exactly like a service task: Zeebe creates a job that an external worker subscribes to and completes. With the inline FEEL implementation, Zeebe's integrated FEEL Scala engine evaluates the `expression` itself and no worker is required.

question: Two script task XML snippets are deployed. Which one is evaluated by Zeebe's integrated FEEL engine without any external job worker?

options.text: The snippet that uses `zeebe:script` with `expression` and `resultVariable`.
Either; Zeebe evaluates all script tasks internally regardless of the extension elements used.
The snippet that uses `zeebe:taskDefinition type="script"` with task headers `language` and `script`.
Neither; both forms require an external worker to complete the job.

a.text: The inline FEEL form uses `zeebe:script` with the `expression` attribute and is evaluated by Zeebe's integrated FEEL Scala engine; no external worker is needed.

c.text: The `zeebe:taskDefinition` with type `script` plus headers is the job worker form. Zeebe does not process these jobs; an external worker (such as the community Zeebe Script Worker) must complete them.

b.text: Zeebe only evaluates the inline FEEL form internally; the job worker form explicitly requires an external worker.

d.text: The inline FEEL form does not require a worker — only the job worker form does.

explanation: Two script task forms exist. `zeebe:script` with `expression`/`resultVariable` is evaluated by Zeebe's integrated FEEL Scala engine in-broker. `zeebe:taskDefinition type="script"` plus headers is the job worker form and behaves like a service task — Zeebe creates a job and an external worker must complete it.

perOption.a:
<bpmn:scriptTask id="calculate-sum" name="Calculate sum">
  <bpmn:extensionElements>
    <zeebe:script expression="=a + b" resultVariable="sum" />
  </bpmn:extensionElements>
</bpmn:scriptTask>
perOption.c:
<bpmn:scriptTask id="calculate-sum" name="Calculate sum">
  <bpmn:extensionElements>
    <zeebe:taskDefinition type="script" />
    <zeebe:taskHeaders>
      <zeebe:header key="language" value="javascript" />
      <zeebe:header key="script" value="a + b" />
    </zeebe:taskHeaders>
  </bpmn:extensionElements>
</bpmn:scriptTask>

question: A process instance reaches a send task with `taskDefinition` type `kafka`, but no job worker is currently subscribed to that type. What does Zeebe do?

options.text: Zeebe creates a job and waits; the send task stays activated until a worker subscribes and completes the job.
An incident is raised immediately because there is no worker registered for `kafka`.
The send task is skipped and the token continues along the outgoing sequence flow.
Zeebe publishes the message itself using a built-in Kafka producer, since send tasks are handled by the broker.

a.text: Send tasks behave exactly like service tasks: Zeebe creates a job and waits for completion. Jobs for send tasks are not processed by Zeebe itself, so the job simply waits until a worker subscribes.

d.text: Zeebe has no built-in Kafka producer; the docs say jobs for send tasks are not processed by Zeebe itself and a job worker must be provided.

b.text: The absence of a subscribed worker is not an incident; the job is simply unactivated and waits to be picked up.

c.text: Send tasks do not skip; the process instance pauses at the task until the job is completed by a worker.

explanation: A send task is a job-based activity like a service task — Zeebe creates a job and pauses the token until the job is completed. Because Zeebe does not process send task jobs itself, the job remains available for activation until a registered job worker requests and completes it.

question: You are modeling a send task that publishes to a Kafka topic `payment`. Which BPMN elements do you set so the job worker knows the job type and the destination topic?

options.text: A `zeebe:signalEventDefinition` with `name` = `payment`; the worker subscribes to that signal name.
A `bpmn:messageEventDefinition` referencing a message named `payment` with `correlationKey` set to the topic name.
A `zeebe:taskDefinition` with `type` (for example `kafka`) and a `zeebe:taskHeader` such as `kafka-topic` = `payment`.
Only a `zeebe:taskDefinition` with `type`; the worker discovers the topic from process variables automatically.

c.text: A send task must define a `taskDefinition` job type the same way a service task does, and static parameters such as the Kafka topic name are passed via task headers.

b.text: `messageEventDefinition` is for message events, not send tasks; send tasks are job-based and use `taskDefinition` plus task headers.

a.text: Signal events are unrelated to send tasks. Send tasks are job-based and configured with `taskDefinition` plus task headers.

d.text: Zeebe does not auto-resolve static parameters from variables; the topic name belongs in a task header, with variable mappings used only when the value must be dynamic.

explanation: A send task is defined the same way as a service task. Set a `taskDefinition` with a `type` for the worker to subscribe to (for example `kafka`) and use task headers to pass static configuration such as the destination topic. Variable mappings can additionally transform variables passed to the worker.

question: Which BPMN XML snippet correctly models a send task that publishes via a `kafka` job worker to the topic `payment`?

options.text: A `<bpmn:receiveTask>` with `<zeebe:taskDefinition type="kafka" />` and a header `kafka-topic` = `payment`.
A `<bpmn:serviceTask>` with `<zeebe:script expression="= publish(payment)" />`.
A `<bpmn:sendTask>` with `<zeebe:taskDefinition type="kafka" />` and a header `kafka-topic` = `payment`.
A `<bpmn:sendTask>` with only `<bpmn:messageEventDefinition messageRef="payment" />`.

c.text: The documented send task XML uses `bpmn:sendTask` with a `zeebe:taskDefinition` for the job type and `zeebe:taskHeaders` for static parameters like the Kafka topic name.

a.text: A `receiveTask` waits for an incoming message rather than publishing one; it is not used to send to Kafka.

b.text: `serviceTask` plus `zeebe:script` is a script task expression form; send tasks are modelled with `bpmn:sendTask`.

d.text: Send tasks are job-based and require a `taskDefinition` with a job type; `messageEventDefinition` is not used on a send task in Camunda 8.

explanation: A send task in Camunda 8 uses `<bpmn:sendTask>` with a `<zeebe:taskDefinition type="..." />` for the worker job type and `<zeebe:taskHeaders>` to pass static configuration such as `kafka-topic`. The matching job worker (for example, a Kafka publisher) completes the job to advance the token.

perOption.c:
<bpmn:sendTask id="publish-message" name="Publish message">
  <bpmn:extensionElements>
    <zeebe:taskDefinition type="kafka" />
    <zeebe:taskHeaders>
      <zeebe:header key="kafka-topic" value="payment" />
    </zeebe:taskHeaders>
  </bpmn:extensionElements>
</bpmn:sendTask>

question: A service task has `taskDefinition` `type` set to the FEEL expression `= "order-" + priorityGroup` with no `retries` attribute. When is `type` evaluated, and what retry count applies if the worker signals failure?

options.text: `type` is evaluated at deployment of the process; retries default to 1.
`type` must be a static string — a FEEL expression in `type` is rejected at deployment.
`type` is evaluated when the service task activates; retries default to 3.
`type` is evaluated the first time a worker subscribes; retries default to 0 (no retry).

c.text: The docs say expressions on a service task are evaluated on activating the task, and the default value of `retries` is three.

a.text: `type` is not evaluated at deployment; it is evaluated on activation so it can use the current process instance variables. The default for `retries` is three, not one.

d.text: Worker subscription does not drive evaluation; activation does. The default `retries` is three, not zero.

b.text: `type` can be a static value or a FEEL expression prefixed by `=` that evaluates to a string, so it is not rejected.

explanation: On a service task, `type` is required and may be a static value or a FEEL expression prefixed by `=` that evaluates to a string. Expressions are evaluated when the service task activates. `retries` is optional and defaults to three when not specified.

question: How do signal events differ from message events in their sender-to-recipient relationship?

options.text: Signals are 1:1 and require a correlation key; messages are 1:N broadcasts.
Both are 1:N broadcasts; the only difference is that signals match by name while messages match by correlation key.
Both are 1:1; signals are scoped to the throwing process definition while messages may cross definitions.
Signals are a 1:N broadcast — all matching catch events fire — while messages are 1:1 and target a single recipient via a correlation key.

d.text: The docs describe signals as a single-sender-to-several-recipient relationship and contrast this with messages, which are a single-sender-to-single-recipient relationship.

b.text: Messages are 1:1, not 1:N; the docs explicitly note that a message cannot have more than one recipient.

a.text: The roles are reversed: signals broadcast to many recipients, and messages are 1:1.

c.text: Signals are not scoped to the throwing process definition; broadcasting triggers all matching subscriptions, even across different processes.

explanation: Broadcasting a signal triggers every signal catch event whose name matches, even across different process definitions, so signals form a 1:N collaboration. Messages are used when a single process instance needs to wait for a message from a single sender, so they are 1:1 and correlate to one instance.

question: A process with BPMN process ID `order` and a signal start event for `order-cancel` is at version 3. Version 4 of `order` (also with a signal start for `order-cancel`) is then deployed. Which signal subscription is active?

options.text: Neither; signal start events register subscriptions when an instance reaches them at runtime, not at deployment.
Only the subscription for version 4; deploying version 4 deleted the version 3 subscription and opened a new one.
Only the version 3 subscription; new versions register their signal subscription only after the previous version is deleted.
Both version 3 and version 4 subscriptions; broadcasts can start instances of any deployed version simultaneously.

b.text: Signal subscriptions only exist for the latest version of a process definition; deploying a new version of the same BPMN process ID deletes the old subscription and opens one for the new version.

d.text: Only the latest version of a given BPMN process ID holds the subscription; older versions do not retain their signal start subscriptions.

c.text: The order is reversed: the new version replaces the subscription on deployment; deletion of the previous version is not required.

a.text: Signal start subscriptions are established at deployment, not when a process instance reaches the event.

explanation: Signal subscriptions for signal start events are tied to the latest deployed version of a process definition (per BPMN process ID). Deploying a new version deletes the prior subscription and opens a new one for the latest version, so broadcasting only starts instances of the newest deployed version.

question: A signal start event has its signal `name` defined as the FEEL expression `= "order " + awaitingAction`. When is this expression evaluated, and what type must the result be?

options.text: When the process is deployed; the result must be a `string`.
When the signal event is activated; the result must be a `string`.
When a matching signal is broadcast; the result must be a `string` or `number`.
When the process is deployed; the result must be a `string` or `number`.

a.text: If the expression belongs to a signal start event of the process, the docs say it is evaluated on deploying the process. The evaluation must result in a `string`.

b.text: Evaluation on activation applies to non-start signal events (intermediate, end, boundary), not to signal start events.

c.text: Signal names are evaluated at deployment for start events, not at broadcast, and the result type is `string`, not `string` or `number`.

d.text: The result must be a `string`; `string` or `number` is the rule for the message `correlationKey`, not for signal names.

explanation: For signal start events, the signal name expression is resolved when the process is deployed so the broker can register the subscription. The expression must evaluate to a `string`. For other signal events (intermediate, end, boundary), the expression is evaluated when the event is activated.

question: Which BPMN XML element defines the signal that a `signalEventDefinition` references via its `signalRef`?

options.text: `<bpmn:signal id="..." name="..." />` declared at the process or definitions level.
No separate element is needed; the signal name is set directly on `signalEventDefinition`.
`<zeebe:signal name="..." />` declared inside `<bpmn:extensionElements>`.
`<bpmn:message id="..." name="..." />` — signals reuse the message element.

a.text: The XML examples in the docs declare a `<bpmn:signal id="signalId" name="..." />` element that the event's `signalEventDefinition` references via `signalRef`.

c.text: There is no `zeebe:signal` extension element; the signal is declared with the standard BPMN `<bpmn:signal>` element.

d.text: Signals are declared with `<bpmn:signal>`, not `<bpmn:message>`; messages are a separate concept with their own element and correlation key.

b.text: `signalEventDefinition` carries `signalRef`, but the signal itself (with its `name`) must be declared in a separate `<bpmn:signal>` element.

explanation: Signal events reference a separate `<bpmn:signal>` element that defines the signal's `id` and `name`. Each signal event (start, intermediate, boundary, end) uses a `signalEventDefinition` with a `signalRef` attribute pointing to that `<bpmn:signal>`.

question: A process token reaches a service task `Charge customer` whose job worker is registered. What does Zeebe do?

options.text: The token stops at the task; Zeebe creates a job and notifies the worker. When the worker signals completion, the token continues along the outgoing sequence flow.
The token waits until the engine polls the worker on a fixed schedule, regardless of when the worker becomes ready.
The token continues immediately; the worker runs asynchronously and the outgoing flow does not wait for it.
Zeebe executes the task logic in-process; the worker is only invoked when the task fails.

a.text: The docs say when a token reaches a task, the token stops, Zeebe creates a job and notifies a registered worker, and when the handler signals completion the token continues on the outgoing sequence flow.

c.text: The token does not continue immediately; it pauses at the task until the job is completed.

d.text: Zeebe does not execute task logic itself for tasks like service tasks; an external worker handles the job.

b.text: Workers request (activate) jobs on demand rather than being polled by the engine on a fixed schedule.

explanation: Tasks are the atomic units of work in BPMN. When a token reaches a task, Zeebe creates a job, the token waits, and the registered worker requests and processes the job. When the worker reports completion, the token proceeds along the outgoing sequence flow.

question: A multi-instance embedded subprocess runs with three parallel iterations and contains a terminate end event. One iteration reaches the terminate end event. What happens?

options.text: All three iterations and the parent process instance are terminated immediately.
The terminate end event is invalid inside a multi-instance subprocess and raises a deployment error.
All three iterations are terminated; the subprocess then completes and the process instance takes the subprocess's outgoing sequence flow.
Only the current iteration is terminated; the other iterations continue, and the subprocess completes when all iterations finish.

d.text: If the subprocess is multi-instance, the docs say the terminate end event only terminates the element instances of the current iteration; other iterations are unaffected.

c.text: Other multi-instance iterations are not terminated; only the current iteration is ended.

a.text: Terminate is scoped to its containing subprocess and does not terminate elements outside that scope or other iterations.

b.text: Terminate end events are valid inside multi-instance subprocesses; their effect is just scoped to the current iteration.

explanation: Inside an embedded subprocess, a terminate end event ends all element instances of that subprocess and the subprocess then completes normally, taking its outgoing flow. When the containing subprocess is multi-instance, the terminate end event only ends the current iteration's element instances; other iterations continue independently.

question: A non-interrupting boundary timer attached to a user task is configured with a time duration of `PT1H`. After the timer triggers once, how does it continue to behave for the same activity instance?

options.text: It triggers only that single time; a time duration on a non-interrupting boundary event does not repeat.
It throws a deployment-time validation error because non-interrupting boundary events must use a time cycle.
It re-schedules itself every hour until the user task completes, because non-interrupting timers always repeat.
It cancels the user task on the second hour because the duration is interpreted as a deadline.

c.text: Repetition only happens for non-interrupting boundary events defined with a time cycle whose repetitions are greater than zero, not for a time duration.

a.text: When a non-interrupting boundary event is defined with a time duration, the docs state explicitly that it triggers only a single time once the date is reached.

d.text: Non-interrupting means the activity is not terminated when the timer fires, so the user task continues regardless of how many times the timer fires.

b.text: Non-interrupting boundary timers may use a time duration, a time cycle, or a time date; only the interrupting variant is restricted to duration or date.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 BPMN timer-events docs state that a non-interrupting timer boundary event defined with a time duration triggers only a single time once the date is reached. Repetition requires a time cycle definition with repetitions greater than zero.

question: Under high system load, when can a Zeebe timer fire relative to its configured due date?

options.text: It may fire later than the due date, but it will never fire earlier than the due date.
It may fire either earlier or later than the due date depending on scheduler pressure.
It is silently dropped if it cannot be delivered within the configured time.
It always fires at exactly the configured time because the broker reserves a dedicated thread per timer.

b.text: Zeebe is asynchronous, so late triggering is possible, but the docs explicitly forbid firing earlier than the due date.

a.text: The docs note that timers may trigger later than their due date under load, but will never trigger earlier than the due date.

d.text: Zeebe does not guarantee exact timing; the docs state explicitly that there is no guarantee a timer triggers exactly at the configured time.

c.text: Timers are not dropped on overload; they are simply triggered later, never earlier.

explanation: Because Zeebe is an asynchronous system, the BPMN timer-events docs state there is no guarantee timers fire exactly at the configured time. Under load they may fire later, but never earlier than the due date.

question: What does the time cycle `R/P1D` configure when used on a Zeebe timer?

options.text: Once per day, for a single occurrence, then the timer subscription is removed.
Every day for a default of three repetitions, because `R` without a number falls back to the default retries value.
It is rejected at deployment because the `R` token requires an explicit repetition count.
Every day, infinitely, until the subscription is cancelled.

a.text: The cycle would only fire once if it were written as `R1/P1D`. Without a repetition count the cycle repeats.

d.text: The docs list `R/P1D` as `Every day, infinitely`. When the repetitions are not defined, the timer repeats infinitely until it is cancelled.

b.text: There is no default-retries fallback for cycles. An omitted repetition count means infinite repetitions, not three.

c.text: ISO 8601 repeating intervals allow omitting the repetition count, and the docs explicitly use `R/P1D` as a valid example.

explanation: Per the BPMN timer-events docs, an ISO 8601 cycle that omits the repetition count repeats infinitely until the subscription is cancelled. `R/P1D` therefore fires every day, indefinitely.

question: What does the XML below model for the activity `process-order`?

options.text: A timer start event that launches `process-order` three times, one hour apart.
A non-interrupting boundary timer that fires up to three times, once per hour, while `process-order` is active.
An intermediate catch event that pauses the process for three hours before continuing.
An interrupting boundary timer that cancels `process-order` after one hour.

d.text: An interrupting boundary timer would carry `cancelActivity="true"` (the default), but here `cancelActivity` is explicitly `false`, so the activity is not terminated when the timer fires.

b.text: `bpmn:boundaryEvent` with `cancelActivity="false"` attached to `process-order`, plus a `timeCycle` of `R3/PT1H`, is the docs' XML example of a non-interrupting boundary timer that fires up to three times, every hour, while the activity is active.

c.text: The element is `bpmn:boundaryEvent`, not an `intermediateCatchEvent`, and boundary timers do not pause the flow they are attached to.

a.text: Timer start events use `bpmn:startEvent`, not `bpmn:boundaryEvent` attached to an activity via `attachedToRef`.

explanation: The docs' XML example shows a `bpmn:boundaryEvent` with `cancelActivity="false"` and a `timeCycle` of `R3/PT1H` attached to `process-order`. That makes it a non-interrupting boundary timer that fires up to three times, every hour, while the activity is active.

question: After adding a new task in Web Modeler without changing its element type, a process instance reaches that task. How does the Zeebe engine handle it at runtime?

options.text: It opens a Tasklist entry assigned to the process initiator and waits for completion.
It treats the task as a pass-through and continues immediately, because Web Modeler defaults new tasks to undefined tasks.
It raises an incident at deployment time because untyped tasks are not deployable.
It creates a job of type `default` and waits for a worker to complete it.

d.text: Undefined tasks are not service tasks. The engine does not create a job for them; only typed tasks like service or business rule tasks dispatch jobs.

a.text: A Tasklist entry requires a user task with `zeebe:userTask`, not an unchanged default task element.

b.text: The docs state that Web Modeler and Desktop Modeler default to undefined tasks, and for the engine an undefined task is a pass-through that automatically continues the process at the moment the process instance arrives.

c.text: Undefined tasks deploy successfully; they simply have no execution semantics and are passed through.

explanation: Web Modeler and Desktop Modeler default new tasks to undefined tasks. For the engine, an undefined task is a pass-through activity that automatically continues the process as soon as the instance arrives, so no job, form, or worker is involved.

question: A user task has a `zeebe:AssignmentDefinition` whose attributes are populated by FEEL expressions. Which statement about how those values are evaluated is NOT true?

options.text: The assignment expressions are evaluated on activating the user task.
The `candidateUsers` expression must evaluate to a single `string` containing one username.
The `assignee` expression must evaluate to a `string`.
The `candidateGroups` expression must evaluate to a `list of strings`.

c.text: True. The docs state the `assignee` expression must result in a `string`.

b.text: False. The docs require the `candidateUsers` expression to evaluate to a `list of strings`, not a single string. A single-username string would not match the documented type.

d.text: True. The docs require `candidateGroups` to evaluate to a `list of strings`.

a.text: True. The docs state that assignment expressions are evaluated on activating the user task.

explanation: The user-tasks docs specify the FEEL evaluation types for assignment: `assignee` resolves to a `string`, while `candidateUsers` and `candidateGroups` both resolve to a `list of strings`. The expressions are evaluated on activation of the user task.

question: A user task's `zeebe:taskSchedule` uses the FEEL expression `= now() + duration("PT15S")` for the `dueDate` attribute. When is this expression evaluated, and what must it return?

options.text: On deployment of the process, and it must return a `number` of milliseconds since the epoch.
On activation of the user task, and it must return a `string` conforming to an ISO 8601 combined date and time representation.
On every task list refresh, and it must return a FEEL `date` value with no time component.
Once when the process is parsed, and it must return a Java `Date` instance.

a.text: Schedule expressions are not deployment-time, and they return an ISO 8601 date-time string, not a millisecond number.

b.text: The docs state that `dueDate` and `followUpDate` expressions are evaluated on activating the user task and must result in a `string` conforming to an ISO 8601 combined date and time representation.

c.text: Tasklist does not re-evaluate the expression on every refresh; it is resolved once on activation, and the result is an ISO 8601 string.

d.text: FEEL expressions do not produce Java `Date` instances; the documented result type is an ISO 8601 string.

explanation: The scheduling section states the `dueDate` and `followUpDate` expressions are evaluated on activating the user task and must result in a `string` conforming to an ISO 8601 combined date and time representation, so `= now() + duration("PT15S")` is resolved once when the task activates.

question: A user task is modelled without a `zeebe:priorityDefinition`. What priority does the task have at runtime, and how is priority ordered?

options.text: Priority `100`, with `100` meaning highest importance and `0` meaning lowest.
Priority `0`, with `0` meaning highest importance and `100` meaning lowest.
Priority `50`, with higher integer values indicating higher importance.
No priority is assigned, and the task is treated as `null` priority by Tasklist.

b.text: The docs state the default is `50`, and a higher priority value indicates higher importance, not the inverse.

c.text: Per the docs, when no value is provided the default priority is `50`, and a higher priority value indicates higher importance.

a.text: `100` is the upper bound of the allowed range, not the default value applied when none is specified.

d.text: There is no `null` priority. If no value is provided, the default value of `50` is used.

explanation: The `zeebe:priorityDefinition` docs state that priority must be an integer between `0` and `100`, that the default is `50` when omitted, and that higher values indicate higher importance.

question: A Camunda user task's `zeebe:formDefinition` sets `formId` to `configure-control-process` but does not set `bindingType`. The same form has been deployed multiple times with different revisions. Which version of the form does Tasklist render when the task activates?

options.text: The version that was deployed together with the currently running process, because `deployment` is the default `bindingType`.
The earliest deployed version, because Tasklist locks the form on first deployment.
An incident is raised because `bindingType` is a required attribute on `zeebe:formDefinition`.
The latest deployed version at the moment the user task is activated, because `bindingType` defaults to `latest` when not specified.

a.text: `deployment` is a valid value but it must be set explicitly; it is not the default for `bindingType`.

d.text: The docs state that if the `bindingType` attribute is not specified, `latest` is used as the default, so Tasklist resolves the form to its latest deployed version at activation time.

b.text: Tasklist does not lock a form to the earliest deployment; the default behaviour is to use the latest deployed version.

c.text: `bindingType` is optional. Omitting it is supported and falls back to the `latest` default.

explanation: For Camunda Forms linked via `formId`, the `bindingType` attribute selects which version is used. If it is not specified, the docs say `latest` is used as the default, so the version resolved at task activation is the latest deployed version of that form.

question: Which statement about a `zeebe:taskListener` element on a user task is NOT true?

options.text: The `type` attribute is optional, because Zeebe routes listener jobs to any subscribed worker on the broker.
The `eventType` attribute is required and accepts values `creating`, `assigning`, `updating`, `completing`, and `canceling`.
If `retries` is omitted, the listener job defaults to 3 retries.
The `type` attribute is required and may be a static string or a FEEL expression prefixed with `=`.

b.text: True. The docs list `creating`, `assigning`, `updating`, `completing`, and `canceling` as the possible `eventType` values, and mark the attribute as required.

d.text: True. The docs mark `type` as required and explicitly allow a static value or a FEEL expression prefixed by `=` that evaluates to a FEEL string.

c.text: True. The docs state that the `retries` attribute is optional and defaults to 3 when omitted.

a.text: False. The docs mark `type` as required because it is the reference used to specify which job workers request the respective task listener job. Without it, no worker can be subscribed.

explanation: Per the user-tasks docs, both `eventType` and `type` are required on a `zeebe:taskListener`. `eventType` selects the lifecycle hook (`creating`, `assigning`, `updating`, `completing`, `canceling`) and `type` identifies the listener job for workers to subscribe to. Only `retries` is optional, defaulting to 3.

question: A user task is modelled without the `zeebe:userTask` extension element, so it uses the job worker implementation. Which job type must the worker subscribe to in order to process these tasks?

options.text: `io.camunda.tasklist:claim`, because Tasklist creates and dispatches the job for the worker.
Whatever type is configured on the user task's `zeebe:taskDefinition`, just like a service task.
`io.camunda.zeebe:userTask`, the job type Zeebe creates for job worker-based user tasks.
`camunda:user-task`, the legacy Camunda 7 job type retained for backward compatibility.

c.text: The docs state that when a user task uses the job worker implementation, a job worker processes jobs of the type `io.camunda.zeebe:userTask`.

a.text: Tasklist does not create the job. For job worker-based user tasks, the engine creates the `io.camunda.zeebe:userTask` job directly.

d.text: The Camunda 7 job type is not used in Camunda 8. The documented type is `io.camunda.zeebe:userTask`.

b.text: Job worker-based user tasks use the fixed `io.camunda.zeebe:userTask` type, not a custom type from `zeebe:taskDefinition`. That is service-task behaviour.

explanation: Removing the `zeebe:userTask` extension makes the user task use the job worker implementation. Zeebe then creates a job and a job worker processes jobs of type `io.camunda.zeebe:userTask`, while task-specific attributes such as assignments and schedule are passed through as task headers.

question: A Camunda user task (with `zeebe:userTask`) must reference an external system using a custom identifier instead of a deployed Camunda Form. Which attribute of `zeebe:formDefinition` carries that identifier?

options.text: `formId`, the same attribute used for Camunda Forms; only the value differs.
`formKey`, because Camunda user tasks always use `formKey` for non-Camunda forms.
`bindingType`, with the value `external` to switch the reference mode.
`externalReference`, the custom form reference attribute on `zeebe:formDefinition`.

a.text: `formId` references a deployed Camunda Form by ID. It is not the attribute documented for arbitrary external identifiers.

d.text: The docs show that for a Camunda user task using a custom form reference, the identifier is set on the `externalReference` attribute of `zeebe:formDefinition`.

b.text: `formKey` is the attribute used for custom form references only when the user task uses the job worker implementation, not for Camunda user tasks.

c.text: `bindingType` selects which deployed version of a Camunda Form is used. It does not provide an external identifier and has no `external` value.

explanation: The user-tasks reference shows that a Camunda user task with a custom form reference uses the `externalReference` attribute on `zeebe:formDefinition`. The `formKey` attribute is reserved for the job worker implementation, where custom form references are set there instead.

question: A user task `Review document` has a non-interrupting boundary timer `R3/PT1H` whose outgoing flow leads to a send task `Send reminder`. The user completes the review after 2.5 hours. How many reminders are sent, and what happens to the user task?

options.text: 3 reminders; the task is cancelled by the third timer.
1 reminder; subsequent timer events are suppressed.
0 reminders; the task is cancelled by the first timer.
2 reminders; the task remains active and completes normally.

c.text: A non-interrupting boundary timer does not cancel the attached activity, so the user task keeps running and the user can still complete it.

d.text: Non-interrupting boundary timers spawn parallel flows without affecting the task. `R3/PT1H` fires at 1h, 2h, and 3h, but by 2.5h only the 1h and 2h timers have fired, so 2 reminders are sent before the user completes.

a.text: The task is not cancelled by a non-interrupting timer, and only two timers have fired by 2.5 hours; the third would have fired at the 3h mark.

b.text: ISO 8601 cycle expressions like `R3/PT1H` fire repeatedly, not just once; the engine does not suppress subsequent events.

explanation: Non-interrupting boundary timers spawn a parallel token down their outgoing path each time they fire and leave the attached activity running. With cycle `R3/PT1H` the timer can fire up to three times, but at 2.5 hours only two firings have occurred (at 1h and 2h), so two reminders are sent and the user task still completes normally.

question: An instance of Process A reaches a signal throw event for signal `Cancelled`. An instance of Process B, a completely different process definition, has a signal intermediate catch event waiting for `Cancelled`. What happens?

options.text: An incident is raised because signals require a correlation key.
Nothing — signals are scoped to the throwing process definition.
Process B only receives the signal if it is a child of Process A via a call activity.
The signal is delivered to the Process B instance.

d.text: Signals are global broadcasts. Any subscriber waiting on a matching signal name receives the signal, regardless of which process definition or instance threw it.

b.text: Signals are not scoped to the throwing process definition; that is the behaviour of local subprocess scopes, not of signals.

c.text: Signal subscriptions are not limited to a process hierarchy; any subscriber on the same cluster receives the signal.

a.text: Correlation keys are a messages concept. Signals are broadcast by name and do not use correlation keys.

explanation: Signals are global broadcasts. The thrower and the subscribers do not need to share a process definition, instance hierarchy, or correlation key — only a matching signal name.

question: Which statement about a terminate end event is NOT true?

options.text: When placed at the top level of the process, it ends the entire process instance.
When placed inside an embedded subprocess, it only ends that subprocess; the parent process continues.
It automatically triggers compensation handlers for completed activities in its scope before ending.
It destroys all tokens within its containing scope.

d.text: True. A terminate end event ends every active execution inside its containing scope at once.

b.text: True. The terminate scope is the activity that contains it, so an embedded subprocess only ends itself; the parent flow continues.

a.text: True. At the top level, the containing scope is the whole process instance, so the instance ends.

c.text: False. Terminate ends its scope abruptly and does not trigger compensation. If compensation is needed, a separate compensation throw event must be placed in the flow.

explanation: A terminate end event ends every active execution inside its containing scope at once. It does not invoke compensation handlers; compensation must be modelled explicitly with a compensation throw event.
